3d-understanding.alt: A flat 2D image of a house on the left, linked by a dotted line to a camera, which looks out into a 3D scene on the right where the same house is reconstructed as a solid object inside a view frustum; the two panels are labeled image and scene.
3d-understanding.caption: 3D understanding is the recovery of a scene's spatial structure, its depth, shape, and layout, from flat images that have already collapsed it. The model has to invert the projection the camera performed, inferring the solid world that lies behind the pixels.

activation-function.alt: Schematic of one artificial neuron: inputs x1 through xn each scaled by a weight feed a summation node (the transfer function) that produces a net input, which then passes through an activation function box labeled phi to give the output, with a threshold input.
activation-function.caption: The activation function is the nonlinearity applied after a neuron sums its weighted inputs. Here the weighted sum (the transfer function) produces a net input, and the activation function phi maps it to the neuron's output. Without this nonlinear step a stack of layers would collapse into a single linear map, so the activation is what gives a network its expressive power.

activation-patching.alt: A schematic of the activation patching setup on the Indirect Object Identification task. A green Clean Run box reads the prompt where John gave a drink and predicts Mary; a red Corrupted Run box reads the prompt where Mary gave a drink and predicts John. A blue arrow between them is labeled Replace activation at layer or head, and a metric box reads Logit Difference equals logit of Mary minus logit of John.
activation-patching.caption: Activation patching localizes a behavior by copying one internal activation from a clean run into a corrupted run and measuring how the output moves. The two prompts differ in a single fact, so when transplanting one component's activation restores the clean prediction, that component is shown to be causally responsible. The logit difference quantifies the effect; the test is interventional, not merely correlational.

activation-steering.alt: A schematic of the Activation Addition method. Two contrasting prompts (for example I love talking about weddings and I hate talking about weddings) are each embedded and run through the model; the recorded activations are differenced to form a steering vector. That steering vector is then injected during the forward pass on a user prompt to produce a steered output. Stage labels along the bottom read find contrasting prompts, embed, forward pass, record activations, diff activations, and prompt and inject.
activation-steering.caption: Activation steering edits behavior at inference time by adding a direction vector into the hidden activations rather than changing any weights. The steering vector is built by differencing the activations of a contrasting pair of prompts, then added into the residual stream during a new forward pass, shifting the output toward or away from the target concept. The edit is runtime only and needs no retraining.

active-learning.alt: A model drawn as a small neural network surveys a scattered cloud of unlabeled points and singles one out, circled in amber; a person points at that point and a labeled box returns to the cloud.
active-learning.caption: Active learning lets the model choose which examples a human should label next, spending a limited labeling budget on the points it finds most informative rather than on a random sample. The loop, query the most uncertain case, get its label, retrain, is what makes a little supervision go a long way.

actor-critic.alt: A block diagram of the actor-critic loop. A state s_t feeds into three boxes: Actor, Critic, and Environment. The Actor outputs an action A_t, which is passed to the Critic and the Environment; the Critic outputs an action-value estimate written q-hat_w(S_t, A_t).
actor-critic.caption: Actor-critic splits the learner into two parts that share the same state. The actor is the policy: it chooses the action. The critic is a learned value estimator: it scores how good that action was, here as the action-value q-hat_w(S_t, A_t). The critic's estimate replaces the noisy episode return in the actor's update, which sharply lowers the variance of policy-gradient learning.
actor-critic.credit: Hugging Face Deep RL Course (Thomas Simonini and contributors)

adagrad.alt: Contour plot of an ill-conditioned objective with the AdaGrad optimizer path descending and turning to reach the minimum at the origin.
adagrad.caption: AdaGrad gives each parameter its own learning rate by dividing the step by the square root of the sum of that parameter's past squared gradients, so directions with large gradients are damped and rarely updated ones keep large steps. This per-parameter scaling lets it move efficiently across directions of very different steepness.
adagrad.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

adam.alt: A descending, oscillating loss curve labeled momentum sits beside a balance scale weighing a pan marked gradient against a pan marked learning rate; the figure is titled Adam.
adam.caption: A conceptual illustration of Adam, which combines momentum, a running average of past gradients that smooths the descent, with a per-parameter step size scaled to each weight's recent gradient magnitude. Balancing those two running estimates is what makes it robust enough to be the default optimizer for most deep networks.

advantage-function.alt: A curve rises above a flat horizontal baseline; the baseline is labeled state value, the curve's peak is labeled action value, and a vertical amber arrow spans the gap between them, marked Advantage.
advantage-function.caption: The advantage function measures how much better a specific action is than the state's average outcome: the action value minus the baseline state value. Subtracting that baseline strips out how good the situation already was and leaves only the credit due to the choice, which sharply lowers the variance of a policy gradient.

adversarial-ai.alt: A person aims an arrow labeled attack at a chip with a distressed face; a raised open hand labeled defense shields it; off to the side a cat carries a checkmark, standing for the correct, unperturbed prediction.
adversarial-ai.caption: Adversarial AI studies inputs crafted to make a model fail, perturbations too small for a person to notice that flip its answer, together with the defenses raised against them. It tends to be a standing arms race, where each new defense invites a new attack shaped to slip past it.

agent-design.alt: An agent robot surrounded by the facets it is designed around: perceive, plan, act, memory, the tools it holds, and a bounding check; titled Agent Design.
agent-design.caption: Agent design is the engineering discipline of deciding how an agent perceives, plans, acts, and remembers: which tools it holds, how it decomposes a goal, where its state lives, and what checks bound it. Good agent design is mostly about constraining a capable model into something reliable, not making it more capable.

agent-evaluation.alt: The SWE-bench evaluation pipeline. A real GitHub issue and the corresponding codebase are given to a language model, which generates a pull request that edits several source files. A hidden unit-test suite is then run: tests that failed before the change (Pre PR) must pass after it (Post PR) for the task to count as resolved.
agent-evaluation.caption: Agent evaluation judges whether an agent reached the goal, not just whether one answer looks right. SWE-bench illustrates the cleanest signal: give the agent a real issue and codebase, let it produce a patch, then run a hidden test suite that decides pass or fail by execution. An outcome verified against tests is evidence, where a graded transcript is opinion.

agent-memory.alt: Diagram of a language agent's memory architecture. At the top sit long-term stores: procedural memory (the LLM and agent code), semantic memory, and episodic memory, each connected by retrieval and learning arrows to a central working memory where reasoning happens. A decision procedure issues actions to, and receives observations from, an external environment of dialogue, physical, and digital interfaces.
agent-memory.caption: Agent memory compensates for a stateless model by storing history outside the context window and feeding back only what is relevant. This architecture splits it into a working (short-term) memory holding the current task and several long-term stores, episodic for past experience, semantic for knowledge, and procedural for reusable skills, that the agent writes to and retrieves from across steps.

agent-optimization.alt: A robot feeds a stacked list of levers, prompts, tools, memory, and control, into an upward-trending chart marked with a checkmark, set beside the targets reliability, cost, and speed.
agent-optimization.caption: Agent optimization is the work that begins once an agent functions at all: tuning its prompts, tools, memory, and control flow against measured outcomes to make it more reliable, cheaper, and faster. It is the move from a demo that works to a system that works in production, and it is impossible without good evaluation.

agent-orchestration.alt: Six multi-agent application patterns built with AutoGen, including math problem solving with student, assistant, and expert agents; retrieval-augmented chat; multi-agent coding; conversational chess; and a dynamic group chat in which a manager agent broadcasts to and coordinates a set of worker agents.
agent-orchestration.caption: Agent orchestration is the control layer that decides which agent or tool runs when and how results flow between steps. These patterns make the coordination concrete: the dynamic group chat puts a manager agent in charge of routing work to specialists, an orchestrator-worker shape central to coordinating a multi-agent system, distinct from the reasoning inside any single agent.

agent-teams.alt: A planner agent at the top branches downward to two agents, a coder marked with a code symbol and a reviewer marked with a checkmark, labeled Planner, Coder, and Reviewer.
agent-teams.caption: Agent teams split a task across specialized roles, a planner, a coder, a reviewer, the way a human team divides labor, so that specialization and cross-checking improve on what any single agent does alone. It is the practical, role-based face of multi-agent systems.

agentic-ai.alt: A prompt note feeds a robot agent that climbs a short flight of steps toward a flag at the top, a dotted path tracing its route; titled Agentic AI.
agentic-ai.caption: Agentic AI names the shift from models that answer a single prompt to systems that pursue a goal over many steps, choosing actions and reacting to results without a human directing each move. It is as much a market category as a precise technique, which is both its usefulness and its vagueness.

agentic-harness.alt: A scaffold frame, its struts labeled conventions, tools, and memory and hung with verification checkmarks, surrounds a central web of connected nodes; titled Agentic Harness.
agentic-harness.caption: An agentic harness is the scaffolding arranged around a model so it can work reliably without a human at every step: the conventions it auto-loads, the tools it can call, a persistent memory, and the verification gates that bound it. The harness is not the model; it is everything that turns a capable model into a dependable worker.

agentic-rag.alt: Comparison of conventional retrieval-augmented generation with Self-RAG. The left side always retrieves a fixed set of documents and generates an answer in one pass. The right side adaptively decides whether to retrieve, retrieves on demand, evaluates each passage's relevance, and critiques whether its own output is supported, using special reflection tokens across several steps.
agentic-rag.caption: Agentic RAG puts the model in control of retrieval rather than running a fixed retrieve-then-generate pipeline. As Self-RAG shows on the right, the agent decides whether retrieval is needed, issues queries on demand, judges the passages, and critiques its own draft, iterating until satisfied, which is what lets it handle multi-hop and open-ended questions a single shot cannot.

agentic-workflow.alt: A gear labeled task flows into a language-model figure that drives a stack of layered steps downward through successive arrows; titled Agentic Workflow.
agentic-workflow.caption: An agentic workflow is a task structured so one or more language models drive it through multiple steps of reasoning, tool use, and self-correction, sitting between a single fixed prompt and a fully autonomous agent. It is the controlled middle where most working agent applications live, and its guiding principle is to use the least agency that works.

ai-adoption.alt: An S-curve of adoption rises over time as a few early users give way to mass uptake and then saturation; labeled users and time; titled AI Adoption.
ai-adoption.caption: AI adoption tends to follow the S-curve of any new technology: a slow start among early users, then a steep climb as it crosses into the mainstream, then a plateau as the market saturates. Where on that curve a given application sits explains much of the gap between breathless demos and the slower reality of changed behavior.

ai-agency-strategy.alt: An AI head beside a dollar coin sends work, automations, and agents out of an agency building toward a client silhouette; titled AI Agency Strategy.
ai-agency-strategy.caption: AI agency strategy treats building with AI as an ordinary services business: package the model's leverage as delivered work, automations, and agents a client will pay for. The scarce input is not the model, which everyone can rent, but the judgment about which problems are worth solving.

ai-agent.alt: Block diagram of an agent interacting with its environment: percepts enter the AGENT box through Sensors, feed a What is the world like now block, then an Action to be done block driven by condition-action rules, and leave through Actuators as actions back into the ENVIRONMENT.
ai-agent.caption: An agent is a model placed in a loop with its environment: it takes in percepts through sensors, decides what to do, and emits actions through actuators, which change the environment and produce the next percepts. This perceive, decide, act cycle, repeated until the goal is met, is what separates an agent from a model that answers in a single pass.

ai-alignment.alt: A human specifies intent to a central AI box, which branches to two bullseye targets labeled outer alignment and inner alignment.
ai-alignment.caption: AI alignment is the problem of making a system's goals match human intent, and it splits in two: outer alignment, whether the objective we specify captures what we actually want, and inner alignment, whether the goal the model internalizes during training matches the one we specified. A miss at either level is a distinct way to hit the wrong target.

ai-assisted-coding.alt: A developer works at a laptop while code-filled chat bubbles pass back and forth with an AI model; titled AI-Assisted Coding.
ai-assisted-coding.caption: AI-assisted coding is the everyday practice of building software with a language model in the loop, from inline autocomplete through chat-based help to an agent that edits and runs the code. It shifts the programmer's job from typing every line toward specifying intent and reviewing what the model produces.

ai-driven-development.alt: A human pauses in thought while an AI brain, through an amber arrow, generates a window of code; labeled human and development.
ai-driven-development.caption: AI-driven development moves AI from assistant to primary engine of creation: the human sets direction and reviews while agents do the bulk of the writing. It is the more autonomous end of the spectrum that runs from autocomplete to fully delegated work, and it stands in deliberate contrast to a human merely assisted by a tool.

ai-ethics.alt: A balance scale weighs an AI head against a shield bearing a checkmark, with scales of justice, a padlock, and a heart arrayed above; titled AI Ethics.
ai-ethics.caption: AI ethics weighs a system's growing capability against the human values it can harm or serve: fairness, privacy, accountability, and welfare. The hard part is that these goods trade off against one another and against raw performance, so ethics is less a checklist than a standing negotiation.

ai-operating-systems.alt: A user addresses a central AI operating system that coordinates tools, sub-agents, and data through branching connections; titled AI Operating System.
ai-operating-systems.caption: An AI operating system reframes the model as the coordinating layer of a computer: the thing that receives a user's intent and marshals tools, sub-agents, memory, and data to carry it out. The bet is that natural language becomes the primary interface and the model becomes the kernel that schedules everything beneath it.

ai-regulation.alt: An AI chip stands beside the scales of justice and a judge's gavel; titled Regulation.
ai-regulation.caption: AI regulation is the body of law and policy that governs how AI systems may be built and deployed, from transparency and safety requirements to liability and outright bans on some uses. Its central difficulty is pacing: rules written for today's systems must bind a technology that changes faster than legislation can.

ai-research-automation.alt: An AI figure with a robotic arm operates a laptop and an experiment card bearing a flowchart, beside a rising results curve.
ai-research-automation.caption: AI research automation turns the model loose on the research loop itself: proposing hypotheses, designing and running experiments, reading the results, and iterating. It is a striking prospect because the same capability could compound, an AI that improves AI, which is why it sits close to the most consequential questions in the field.

ai-risk.alt: A robot marked AI risk sits beside a seesaw weighing the labels misuse, bias, and fraud against a label reading catastrophic.
ai-risk.caption: AI risk spans a wide range, from concrete present harms like misuse, bias, and fraud to speculative catastrophic failures of highly capable future systems. Much of the disagreement in the field is really a disagreement about where on that range to spend attention now.

ai-safety.alt: An AI figure stands by axes whose capability curve rises over time toward a warning triangle, with icons for verification, oversight, and institutions beneath; labeled safety.
ai-safety.caption: AI safety is the effort to keep systems reliable and under control as their capability climbs, through verification, oversight, red-teaming, and the institutions that hold them to account. The animating worry is the gap between how fast capability rises and how fast our ability to assure it does.

ai-winter.alt: A hype-over-time curve spikes, crashes, and freezes an AI block in ice before a thaw and a renewed rise; labeled hype and time; titled AI Winter.
ai-winter.caption: An AI winter is a period when inflated expectations collapse into disappointment, funding and interest dry up, and the field goes dormant, as happened in the 1970s and again in the late 1980s. The pattern is a caution worth keeping in view during any boom: the gap between a demo and a dependable product is where winters are made.

alignment-tax.alt: A seesaw pivots on a fulcrum labeled capability; a plain round weight on one side lifts as a heavy block labeled alignment tax presses the other side down.
alignment-tax.caption: The alignment tax is the cost in performance, money, or effort paid to make a model safer or more aligned than the cheapest version that would score well. When the tax is high there is commercial pressure to skip it, which is why so much alignment research aims to drive the tax toward zero.

approximate-nearest-neighbor.alt: A green query point at the center surrounded by red triangles and blue squares. A solid inner circle encloses the three points closest to the query, and a dashed outer circle encloses a larger set of nearby points.
approximate-nearest-neighbor.caption: Nearest neighbor search asks which stored points lie closest to a query (green). The inner circle captures its few nearest neighbors and the outer circle a wider set. Approximate methods find these neighbors without comparing against every point, trading a small, tunable chance of missing a true neighbor for an enormous gain in speed, which is what makes vector search practical at scale.

artificial-general-intelligence.alt: A general mind handles many different domains in turn, rather than one narrow task; labeled domain; titled Artificial General Intelligence.
artificial-general-intelligence.caption: Artificial general intelligence names a system with the broad, flexible competence of a human mind: able to learn and reason across essentially any domain rather than excelling at one narrow task. It is the field's most loaded term, a moving target with no agreed definition, and the threshold around which much of the hope and fear about AI is organized.

artificial-intelligence.alt: Three nested circles: a large outer circle labeled Artificial Intelligence, a smaller circle inside it labeled Machine Learning, and a smallest circle inside that labeled Deep Learning, each with a short definition.
artificial-intelligence.caption: Artificial intelligence is the broad outer container: any machine that mimics intelligent behavior. Machine learning is the subset that learns from data instead of hand-written rules, and deep learning is the narrower subset of machine learning that uses many-layered neural networks. The nesting shows that these are not rivals but successive answers to the same question.

async-agents.alt: A person sends a chat to an agent robot, which works on a task folder marked with a clock and later reports back along a dashed return arrow; titled Async Agents.
async-agents.caption: Async agents run in the background on long tasks and report back when done, rather than holding a live, turn-by-turn conversation. The shift from a chat you wait on to a job you check on later is what lets agents take on work that takes minutes or hours.

attention-mechanism.alt: Diagram of attention pooling. A single query and a column of keys feed an attention scoring function (labeled a) that produces one score per key. The scores pass through a softmax to become attention weights, each weight is multiplied by its corresponding value, and the products are summed into a single output vector.
attention-mechanism.caption: Attention compares a query against every key with a scoring function, passes the scores through a softmax to get weights that sum to one, then returns the weighted sum of the values. The output is therefore a content-dependent blend of information drawn from across the whole input, which is what lets a model focus on the most relevant context regardless of distance.
attention-mechanism.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

attention-schema-theory.alt: Two-panel line drawing. In panel A a person looks at an apple and a thought bubble shows only the apple; in panel B the thought bubble instead contains the label S plus A plus V, with an arrow from Me to the apple, representing an internal model that binds self, attention, and stimulus.
attention-schema-theory.caption: Attention schema theory says awareness is the brain's internal model of its own attention. In panel A, attending to the apple yields accurate processing of the stimulus but gives the brain no basis to report that it is aware. In panel B the brain additionally models the self, the stimulus, and the attention linking them, and that schematic self-model is what it reports as subjective awareness.
attention-schema-theory.credit: Michael S. A. Graziano and Taylor W. Webb

attention-sink.alt: Tokens flow down a row of arrows into a basin and out a drain, where a pool of amber attention collects; titled Attention Sink.
attention-sink.caption: An attention sink is a position, often the very first token, that soaks up a large share of a transformer's attention weight regardless of content, giving the softmax somewhere harmless to dump probability it cannot place elsewhere. Models lean on it so heavily that discarding those tokens during long-context inference can wreck generation.

attribution-graph.alt: A simplified attribution graph. On the left, two token-embedding nodes (The, cat) connect by weighted edges to two interpretable feature nodes (subject detection, animal category), which connect to a third feature node (verb agreement), which connects on the right to an output logit node (sat) and a reconstruction-error node. Edge labels give the linear effect of each connection. A note reads that real attribution graphs have hundreds to thousands of nodes.
attribution-graph.caption: An attribution graph traces how interpretable features causally influence one another to produce a model's output on a single input. Nodes are features active on the prompt and edges are the linear effects between them, so reading the graph from input embeddings to the output logit reveals the chain of intermediate steps the model ran. It is a circuit caught firing on one concrete example.

autoencoder.alt: Schematic of an autoencoder showing an encoder compressing input to a latent code and a decoder reconstructing the output
autoencoder.caption: An autoencoder compresses an input to a small latent code and reconstructs it; the bottleneck forces the network to keep only the most informative structure.

automatic-differentiation.alt: A computation graph for a function of two inputs built bottom to top through operator nodes (sin, times, plus); a large arrow down the left marks the backward propagation of derivative values, with adjoint quantities flowing from the output (seeded at one) back to each input.
automatic-differentiation.caption: Automatic differentiation records a program as a graph of elementary operations and applies the chain rule across it to get exact derivatives. In reverse mode, shown here, a forward pass computes the values and a single backward pass propagates derivative contributions from the output (seeded at one) back to every input; applied to a neural network this reverse pass is exactly backpropagation.

automatic-speech-recognition.alt: Diagram of the Whisper speech model. On the left, examples of multitask training data: English transcription, any-to-English speech translation, non-English transcription, and no-speech audio. In the center, audio becomes a log-mel spectrogram that passes through transformer encoder blocks, and a transformer decoder with cross attention emits tokens. At the bottom, the multitask training format threads special tokens for language identification, transcription versus translation, and timestamps through one sequence.
automatic-speech-recognition.caption: Modern automatic speech recognition is a sequence-to-sequence transformer: the input waveform becomes a log-mel spectrogram that the encoder reads, and the decoder generates the transcript token by token. Whisper folds transcription, translation, language identification, and timestamps into one model by prefixing the target sequence with special task tokens.
automatic-speech-recognition.credit: Radford, Alec and Kim, Jong Wook and Xu, Tao and Brockman, Greg and McLeavey, Christine and Sutskever, Ilya (OpenAI)

automation.alt: A hand drops a task card into a vertical pipeline of steps driven by a gear marked AI.
automation.caption: Automation hands a recurring, multi-step task to a system that carries it out without a person driving each step, increasingly by wrapping a language model in tools, triggers, and checks. The hard part is rarely competence on any single step; it is reliability across the whole chain when no one is watching.

autonomous-agent.alt: Five levels of agent autonomy shown as columns, from L1 Operator through L2 Collaborator, L3 Consultant, and L4 Approver to L5 Observer. A horizontal axis beneath them runs from high User Involvement on the left to high Agent Autonomy on the right, with the human's role shrinking as the agent's independence grows.
autonomous-agent.caption: Autonomy is a spectrum, not a switch. At low levels the human drives nearly every action and at high levels the agent operates independently while a person only observes. An autonomous agent sits at the right of this scale: given a high-level goal, it chooses its own steps and tools over a long horizon, which is more flexible but harder to keep reliable and safe.
autonomous-agent.credit: K. J. Kevin Feng, David W. McDonald, Amy X. Zhang

autoregressive-model.alt: A grid of image pixels labeled in raster order from x_1 at the top left to x_n squared at the bottom right. The pixels above and to the left of the current pixel are shaded blue (already generated), the current pixel x_i is red, and the pixels after it are white (not yet generated).
autoregressive-model.caption: An autoregressive model generates one element at a time in a fixed order, here pixels in raster scan. The current pixel x_i (red) is predicted from all the pixels generated before it (blue), and the joint probability of the whole image is the product of these per-element conditionals.

autoresearch.alt: An AI figure reads a book beneath a magnifier holding a question mark, with a search arrow and a test bar chart alongside; titled Autoresearch.
autoresearch.caption: Autoresearch uses an agent to carry an open-ended research task end to end: formulating questions, gathering and reading sources, running searches or experiments, and synthesizing findings with little step-by-step direction. It is a demanding test of agency, because research has no fixed procedure and no obvious stopping point.

backpropagation.alt: Computational graph of a small one-hidden-layer network: the input x is multiplied by weights to give z, an activation gives h, a second weight multiply gives the output o, which with the labels yields the loss; weight-decay terms feed a regularization term s, and the loss plus s give the objective J.
backpropagation.caption: Backpropagation treats the network as a computational graph of operations and intermediate values, like this forward graph from input x through the hidden activation h to the loss and objective J. It then applies the chain rule in reverse along these same edges, computing the gradient of the loss with respect to every weight in a single backward pass that costs about as much as the forward one.
backpropagation.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

backpropagation-through-time.alt: A recurrent unit is unrolled across timesteps t, t+1, t+2, and the gradient is propagated back along the unrolled chain; labeled time and gradient.
backpropagation-through-time.caption: Backpropagation through time trains a recurrent network by unrolling it across the timesteps of a sequence and backpropagating the error along that whole chain, as if it were one very deep feedforward network. The length of that chain is exactly what makes recurrent training prone to vanishing and exploding gradients over long sequences.

bag-of-words.alt: On the left, a Renaissance portrait of a woman. On the right, the same portrait cut into small fragments (an eye, the mouth, the necklace, a patch of hair) scattered loosely inside an open paper bag.
bag-of-words.caption: The bag-of-words model represents a text by which pieces it contains while discarding their order, like cutting a portrait into fragments and dropping them into a bag. The collection of parts is kept but the arrangement is lost, so 'the dog bit the man' and 'the man bit the dog' look identical. For many tasks the words present matter more than their order, which is why this crude representation works.

basis.alt: A 2D grid with two unit arrows from the origin: e1 pointing one unit along the horizontal axis and e2 pointing one unit along the vertical axis.
basis.caption: A basis is a minimal set of independent directions from which every vector in the space is built uniquely by scaling and adding. The standard basis e1 and e2 spans the plane, so any vector is just so much e1 plus so much e2, and those amounts are exactly its coordinates.

batch-normalization.alt: A layer's activations are rescaled to zero mean and unit variance, then given a learned scale and shift, gamma x plus beta; titled Batch Norm.
batch-normalization.caption: Batch normalization rescales a layer's activations across the current batch to a stable mean and variance, then applies a learned scale and shift so the network can undo the normalization where it needs to. It dramatically sped up and stabilized deep network training, though its dependence on batch statistics is why transformers reached for layer normalization instead.

batch-size.alt: Plot of training loss versus wall-clock time on a log scale, comparing full-batch gradient descent, single-example stochastic gradient descent, and mini-batches of size 100 and 10.
batch-size.caption: Batch size is how many examples are used per update, and it trades gradient quality against speed. Full-batch descent takes accurate but slow steps and single-example updates are noisy; moderate mini-batches reach a low loss fastest in wall-clock time by using hardware efficiently while keeping the gradient stable.
batch-size.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

bayes-theorem.alt: Two probability tree diagrams of the same two events: the top tree branches on A first then B, the bottom tree branches on B first then A, with Bayes' theorem written between them.
bayes-theorem.caption: Bayes' theorem inverts the direction of conditioning. The same joint probabilities can be factored two ways, conditioning on A first or on B first; Bayes' rule is the bridge that converts P(B given A) into P(A given B).

bayesian-brain.alt: Teaching figure on Bayesian inference. The top left shows inference as reallocation of probability over a solution space and Bayes rule. The lower left overlays a likelihood, a prior, and the resulting posterior as curves for an uninformative uniform prior and an informative Gaussian prior, and walks through a neuroscience example comparing firing rates with broad versus narrow priors.
bayesian-brain.caption: The Bayesian brain treats perception as inference: it combines a prior, what it expected, with a likelihood, how well each cause explains the current input, to form a posterior belief. The figure shows the posterior as a precision-weighted compromise between prior and evidence; a narrower (more certain) distribution pulls the posterior more strongly toward itself.

bayesian-epistemology.alt: Four stacked panels, one each for the parameters alpha, beta0, beta1, and sigma, every panel holding many overlaid probability density curves colored from yellow through green to dark purple. The yellow curves are low and broad, the purple curves are tall and narrow, and across each panel the curves tighten and pile up around a vertical black line that marks the true parameter value.
bayesian-epistemology.caption: Bayesian epistemology treats belief as a probability distribution that is revised by evidence. Here a model is updated in successive rounds, and each round's posterior becomes the next round's prior. The earliest distributions (yellow) are wide, expressing high uncertainty; as more data arrives the distributions (greening, then purple) grow narrow and concentrate on the true value (black line). This sharpening of a degree of belief by repeated conditionalization on new data is the core of the Bayesian account of rational learning.

bayesian-inference.alt: Three panels labeled Prior, Likelihood, and Posterior: a broad prior over a parameter, a likelihood from the data, and a narrower posterior concentrated where the two agree.
bayesian-inference.caption: Bayesian inference combines a prior distribution of belief with the likelihood of the observed data to produce a posterior distribution. The posterior concentrates where prior and data agree, carrying uncertainty forward rather than returning a single point estimate.

bayesian-optimization.alt: Five stacked rows, each one iteration of Bayesian optimization. The left column plots the true objective as a red dashed curve with the Gaussian process mean as a green dashed curve inside a shaded uncertainty band and red dots for the points evaluated so far. The right column plots the acquisition function in blue with the next point to sample marked at its peak. Across the rows the band narrows as more points are added.
bayesian-optimization.caption: Bayesian optimization in five iterations. The left column fits a Gaussian process surrogate (green mean, shaded uncertainty band) to the points evaluated so far, with the band wide where the space is unexplored. The right column turns that belief into an acquisition function whose peak picks the single most informative next point, balancing sampling where the value looks good against sampling where uncertainty is high. As points accumulate the band tightens around the true objective.

beam-search.alt: A branching diagram across three time steps with beam width two. From the start, candidates A through E are expanded but only A and C are kept (highlighted); each is expanded again, keeping AB and CE; then again, reaching ABD and CED.
beam-search.caption: Beam search keeps the few best partial sequences at every step rather than just one. With a beam width of two, all next tokens are expanded but only the two highest-scoring sequences survive each step (A and C, then AB and CE, then ABD and CED). Carrying several hypotheses forward lets a locally weaker token prove it leads somewhere better, which greedy decoding (beam width one) cannot do.
beam-search.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

behavior-cloning.alt: An expert at a laptop emits an action; a head labeled policy learns to copy it, with a dashed arrow back to the expert and a warning triangle marking the failure mode; titled Behavior Cloning.
behavior-cloning.caption: Behavior cloning learns a policy by plain supervised imitation, mapping observed states to the expert's actions. Its weakness is compounding error: one small mistake moves the agent into states the expert never visited, where the training offers no guidance and errors snowball.

bellman-equation.alt: A flow chart of a sequential decision process. States x_t, x_t+1, x_t+2 are linked by transition boxes T, with payoffs F at each step depending on the state and an action a. The full value function V(x_t) is written as a discounted sum of payoffs, and a red arrow marks the recursive Bellman equation V(x_t) equals the max over the action of F(x_t, a_t) plus beta times V(T(x_t, a_t)).
bellman-equation.caption: The Bellman equation rewrites the value of a state as a recursion. Instead of summing the entire infinite future, the value of the current state equals the immediate payoff plus the discounted value of the state the chosen action leads to (the red equation). This one self-consistent step, repeated, is the backbone of value iteration, Q-learning, and nearly every reinforcement learning algorithm.

benchmark.alt: Several model curves climb across a chart toward a horizontal target line marked by a benchmark signpost; the rising lines are labeled model.
benchmark.caption: A benchmark is a fixed task and dataset that turns a vague question, which model is better, into a single comparable score. Its strength, a common yardstick, is also its weakness: once everyone optimizes for it the number saturates and stops distinguishing the very systems it was built to rank.

benchmark-contamination.alt: A benchmark question-and-answer sheet leaks an amber stream into a model tank that is wired to a dataset.
benchmark-contamination.caption: Benchmark contamination is when test questions leak into a model's training data, so a high score reflects memorization rather than ability. It is insidious because it is usually invisible: with web-scale training sets, no one can easily prove a given benchmark was or was not seen.

benign-overfitting.alt: A flexible model curve passes exactly through noisy data points yet stays smooth enough to generalize; labeled noisy and fitted model; titled Benign Overfitting.
benign-overfitting.caption: Benign overfitting is the surprising modern finding that a vastly overparameterized model can fit its training data exactly, noise and all, and still generalize well, contradicting the textbook warning that interpolating noise must hurt. Understanding why is a central project of deep learning theory, tied to double descent and the implicit bias of how these models are trained.

bert.alt: Three side-by-side model diagrams labeled ELMo, GPT, and BERT. Each takes tokens at the bottom, produces per-token representations, and feeds a task layer that outputs labels. ELMo uses a bidirectional architecture but a task-specific head; GPT uses a unidirectional architecture with a head that needs only minimal changes; BERT combines a bidirectional architecture with the minimal-change head, and is reached by pretraining followed by fine-tuning.
bert.caption: BERT's defining choice is to combine deeply bidirectional encoding (each token attends to both left and right context, as in ELMo) with GPT's task-agnostic design, so one pretrained model is fine-tuned for many tasks with only minimal architectural changes.
bert.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

bertscore.alt: Two boxes, reference and candidate, feed into a grid of embedding dots inside a panel with one dot highlighted; labeled BERTScore and Embeddings.
bertscore.caption: BERTScore grades generated text by comparing the contextual embeddings of its tokens against a reference, rather than counting exact word overlap. Because it matches meaning in embedding space, it credits a sound paraphrase that a surface metric like BLEU would penalize.

best-of-n-sampling.alt: Three side-by-side panels comparing PRM search methods. The left panel, labeled Best-of-N, shows several independent answer chains generated in parallel from one question; each full solution is scored by a verifier and the highest-scoring chain is selected. The center panel shows beam search keeping the top candidates at each step, and the right panel shows lookahead search.
best-of-n-sampling.caption: Best-of-N sampling generates N complete answers independently from the same prompt, scores each with a verifier, and returns the one the verifier ranks highest (the left panel). It spends parallel inference compute to lift quality on tasks where checking an answer is easier than producing one, and contrasts with step-level search methods like beam search and lookahead search.
best-of-n-sampling.credit: Charlie Snell, Jaehoon Lee, Kelvin Xu, et al.

bi-encoder.alt: A symmetric two-tower diagram. Sentence A and Sentence B each pass up through their own BERT block, then a pooling step, producing vectors u and v. The two vectors feed a single cosine-sim(u, v) box at the top, whose output ranges from minus 1 to 1.
bi-encoder.caption: A bi-encoder runs each text down its own tower, encoding the query and the document independently into vectors u and v, then scores relevance by a cheap similarity such as cosine. Because a document's vector never depends on the query, every document can be embedded once and indexed ahead of time, so search becomes a fast nearest-neighbor lookup. That independence is what lets a bi-encoder scale to a large corpus.

bias-variance-tradeoff.alt: Plot of error against model complexity showing bias squared falling as complexity rises, variance rising, and the total error forming a U-shaped curve with a marked optimum at the bottom.
bias-variance-tradeoff.caption: As model complexity grows, bias falls but variance rises, and their sum, the total error, traces a U. The lowest point marks the optimal complexity: simpler models underfit on the left with high bias, more complex models overfit on the right with high variance, and no single model drives both errors to zero at once.

bleu-score.alt: A candidate sentence and a reference are compared box by box, their overlap feeding a precision gauge; labeled candidate, reference, and precision; titled BLEU Score.
bleu-score.caption: BLEU scores a machine translation by how much of its wording overlaps a human reference, counting matching word sequences with a precision orientation and a penalty for being too short. It was the metric that let machine translation be tuned automatically, and its weakness is the flip side of its strength: it rewards matching the reference's words, not conveying the same meaning.

bm25.alt: A magnifier reading a query points to a stack of documents with one passage highlighted; labeled weight plus length and titled BM25.
bm25.caption: BM25 is the workhorse keyword ranking function: it scores a document for a query by how often the query's terms appear, dampened so repeated words give diminishing returns, and adjusted for length so long documents do not win by sheer size. Decades on, it remains a stubbornly strong baseline that dense retrievers struggle to beat outright.

byte-pair-encoding.alt: Rows of single-character tiles are progressively merged into larger subword tiles through an arrow, with one newly merged tile highlighted; titled Byte-Pair Encoding.
byte-pair-encoding.caption: Byte-pair encoding builds a subword vocabulary by starting from individual characters and repeatedly merging the most frequent adjacent pair into a single new token. The result lands between characters and whole words: common words become one token while rare ones split into pieces, so the model can spell out anything it has never seen.

calibration.alt: A reliability diagram plotting mean predicted probability on the x-axis against the observed fraction of positives on the y-axis. A dotted diagonal marks perfect calibration, and four classifiers are shown: logistic regression hugs the diagonal, uncalibrated naive Bayes bows away from it, and isotonic and sigmoid recalibration pull naive Bayes back toward the line. A histogram below shows how predictions are distributed across probability bins.
calibration.caption: A model is calibrated when its stated confidence matches its observed accuracy, so among cases it calls 80 percent likely, about 80 percent are positive. The reliability diagram plots predicted probability against the actual fraction of positives; a perfectly calibrated model lies on the dotted diagonal, and a curve that bows above or below it reveals over or under confidence that recalibration can correct.

catastrophic-forgetting.alt: Three line plots tracking a model's score on many held-out benchmarks across successive stages of continual instruction tuning, labeled from Initial through later tuning stages. Almost every line falls from its initial value as more tuning stages are applied.
catastrophic-forgetting.caption: Catastrophic forgetting is visible here as a steady decline: as a model is fine-tuned on a sequence of new tasks (left to right), its measured ability on previously learned benchmarks drops, because the shared weights that stored the old skills are overwritten to fit the new data. Nothing in the loss on current data protects the earlier knowledge, so the degradation can be broad and severe.
catastrophic-forgetting.credit: Yun Luo, Zhen Yang, Fandong Meng, Yafu Li, Jie Zhou, Yue Zhang

category-theory.alt: On the left, a vertical arrow f from object X to object Y. On the right, a square whose four corners are F(X), G(X), F(Y), G(Y); the vertical arrows are F(f) and G(f), and the horizontal arrows are the components eta-X and eta-Y.
category-theory.caption: The three tiers of category theory in one picture. A morphism f between objects X and Y (left) is sent by two functors F and G to the vertical maps F(f) and G(f), and a natural transformation supplies the horizontal components eta-X and eta-Y. The square commuting (both paths from F(X) to G(Y) agree) is exactly what makes the transformation natural: it relates the two constructions uniformly across every object.

causal-attention.alt: Block diagram of scaled dot-product attention flowing upward: queries and keys enter a matrix multiply, then a scale step, then a mask step, then softmax to produce attention scores, which a final matrix multiply combines with the values to give the context vector.
causal-attention.caption: Causal attention is scaled dot-product attention with the mask step shown here doing the work. After the query-key scores are scaled, the mask sets every score that points to a future token to negative infinity, so the following softmax gives those positions zero weight. Each position can then attend only to itself and earlier tokens, which is what lets a model be trained on next-token prediction over a whole sequence at once.

causal-inference.alt: Two causal diagrams. Top: Exposure causes Mediator causes Outcome. Bottom: a Confounder causes both Exposure and Outcome, while a dashed arrow marks that Exposure does not cause Outcome.
causal-inference.caption: Why correlation is not causation, in one picture. A mediator (top) sits on the causal path from exposure to outcome. A confounder (bottom) is a common cause of both, manufacturing a correlation between exposure and outcome even though neither causes the other. Telling these structures apart is exactly the job standard machine learning, built to find correlations, cannot do on its own.

cell-assembly.alt: Within a network of neurons, a co-active subgroup is circled as a single functional unit; titled Cell Assembly.
cell-assembly.caption: A cell assembly is Hebb's idea that a memory or concept is held not by one neuron but by a group wired to fire together, so activating part of the group revives the whole. It is an early and influential answer to how distributed brains represent things, and a clear ancestor of distributed representations in neural networks.

central-limit-theorem.alt: Panels showing the distribution of the sum of n dice for n from 1 to 5: a flat bar chart for one die that grows more bell-shaped as more dice are added, with a final panel overlaying the curves against a normal.
central-limit-theorem.caption: The central limit theorem says sums of many independent variables tend toward a Gaussian. A single die is flat and uniform, but the distribution of the sum of several dice grows into the familiar bell curve as their number increases.

chain-of-thought.alt: Two side-by-side prompt examples. Under Standard Prompting the model is given a worked example then answers a new question directly and gets it wrong (red). Under Chain-of-Thought Prompting the example includes step-by-step reasoning (highlighted), and the model writes out its own reasoning before giving the correct answer (green).
chain-of-thought.caption: Chain-of-thought prompting has the model write out intermediate reasoning steps (highlighted) before its final answer, rather than jumping straight to it. On multi-step problems this raises accuracy: the direct answer on the left is wrong, while the worked reasoning on the right reaches the right one, because each generated step is extra computation the later steps can build on.
chain-of-thought.credit: Jason Wei, Xuezhi Wang, Dale Schuurmans, Maarten Bosma, Brian Ichter, Fei Xia, Ed Chi, Quoc Le, Denny Zhou

chain-rule.alt: A computation graph for a function of two inputs, with square input nodes feeding circular operator nodes (sin, times, plus) up to the output; each edge is annotated with a derivative formula that multiplies and sums contributions along the paths.
chain-rule.caption: The chain rule differentiates a composition by multiplying the local rates of change along the chain, summing over every path from an input to the output. Here the derivative of each node is built from the derivatives of the nodes feeding it (for example the sin node contributes cos(w1) times the incoming rate), which is exactly how a network's gradients are assembled.

chatbot-arena.alt: A screenshot of the Chatbot Arena interface. The same prompt, a request to draft a professional email, is sent to two anonymous models labeled Model A and Model B, whose responses appear side by side. Below the responses are four voting buttons: A is better, B is better, Tie, and Both are bad. Posted rules explain that model identities are hidden and that votes where names are revealed do not count.
chatbot-arena.caption: Chatbot Arena evaluates models by live human preference rather than a fixed answer key. A user sends one prompt, sees two anonymous models answer side by side, and votes for the better response; the hidden identities keep the vote about quality, not brand, and the aggregated head-to-head votes feed an Elo-style leaderboard.
chatbot-arena.credit: Lianmin Zheng, Wei-Lin Chiang, Ying Sheng, Siyuan Zhuang, Zhanghao Wu, Yonghao Zhuang, Zi Lin, Zhuohan Li, Dacheng Li, Eric P. Xing, Hao Zhang, Joseph E. Gonzalez, Ion Stoica

china-brain.alt: The outline of a human brain drawn entirely out of many small identical figures of people, each standing for one person in the population.
china-brain.caption: The China Brain asks us to imagine every person in a huge population acting as a single neuron, passing signals by radio so the whole nation reproduces the functional organization of a brain. If functionalism is true the network should have the brain's mental states, yet it seems incredible that a billion people exchanging messages add up to one conscious subject, which is the absent-qualia objection the image makes vivid.

chinese-room.alt: Inside a sealed room, a person receives Chinese symbols, looks up responses in a rulebook, and passes Chinese symbols back out without understanding the language; labeled input and output; titled Chinese Room.
chinese-room.caption: Searle's Chinese Room imagines a person who knows no Chinese following a rulebook to manipulate Chinese symbols well enough to answer questions, producing fluent replies with zero understanding. The argument is that running a program, manipulating symbols by their shape, can never by itself amount to understanding their meaning, a direct challenge to the idea that a language model comprehends what it says.

chunking.alt: A long document is split into several smaller text cards, one of which maps to an embedding dot; titled Chunking.
chunking.caption: Chunking splits a long document into passages small enough to embed and retrieve precisely, the unavoidable first step in any retrieval system. The chunk size is a real tradeoff: too large and a passage dilutes the relevant sentence, too small and it loses the context that made the sentence meaningful.

circuit.alt: Three feature detectors on the left, labeled Windows, Car Body, and Wheels, each shown as an optimized image next to a grid of its spatial weights colored red for positive (excitation) and blue for negative (inhibition); arrows feed them into a car detector on the right, captioned as assembled from these earlier units.
circuit.caption: A circuit is a set of named feature detectors wired together by weights to compute a higher-level feature. Here window, car-body, and wheel detectors combine into a car detector: the weight maps show that windows excite the car detector at the top and inhibit it at the bottom, while wheels do the reverse, so the spatial arrangement of parts, not just their presence, is what the circuit checks.
circuit.credit: Olah, Cammarata, Schubert, Goh, Petrov, Carter

classification.alt: A grid comparing the decision boundaries of many classifiers across three two-dimensional datasets. Each cell shades the plane into red and blue regions for the two classes, with the training and test points overlaid, and the boundaries differ in shape from one classifier to the next.
classification.caption: Classification assigns each input to one of a fixed set of categories by carving the feature space into regions, one per class. Running several classifiers on the same data shows that each draws the boundary differently: some are straight, others curved or patchy. The shaded regions are the learned decision rule, and a new point is labeled by the region it lands in.

classifier-free-guidance.alt: From a field of noise, an unconditional path and a conditional path both run toward a generated image of a horse, meeting at a marked point; labeled unconditional and condition.
classifier-free-guidance.caption: Classifier-free guidance steers a diffusion model toward a prompt by running the network twice, once with the condition and once without, then extrapolating away from the unconditional prediction. Turning the guidance strength up sharpens how faithfully the image follows the prompt, at some cost to diversity.

claude-code.alt: A coding agent works at a terminal with direct access to a project's files and a laptop; titled Claude Code.
claude-code.caption: Claude Code is Anthropic's command-line coding agent, which runs in a terminal with direct access to a developer's files, shell, and tools, so it can read a codebase, make multi-file changes, run tests, and iterate, rather than only suggesting snippets in a chat window. It is a reference example of an agentic coding harness.

clip.alt: CLIP diagram in three parts: (1) contrastive pre-training where a text encoder and an image encoder produce embeddings whose pairwise dot products form a similarity matrix, with the matching image-text pairs on the diagonal pulled together; (2) building a zero-shot classifier by encoding label texts like a photo of a dog; (3) zero-shot prediction matching an image embedding to the closest label embedding.
clip.caption: CLIP aligns images and text in one shared embedding space by contrastive learning over many image-caption pairs. During training the model pulls each true image-text pair together and pushes mismatched pairs apart (the matrix diagonal), which lets it classify a new image with no labeled examples by comparing it to encoded category names.

clustering.alt: A grid of scatter plots: rows are different shaped datasets (two concentric circles, two interleaving moons, blobs, anisotropic and varied-variance blobs, and uniform noise) and columns are different clustering algorithms, each coloring the points by the cluster it assigned.
clustering.caption: Clustering groups points by similarity with no labels, but algorithms disagree about what a cluster is. Across these dataset shapes, center-based methods like k-means split the concentric circles and moons wrongly, while density-based methods recover the curved shapes. There is no single correct clustering, because the chosen notion of similarity quietly decides the answer.

code-generation.alt: A prompt bubble and a language model produce a complete function, def add returning x plus y, from a partial stub; titled Code Generation.
code-generation.caption: Code generation uses a language model to write or complete source code from a description, surrounding context, or a partial snippet, reframing programming as predicting the next token of a program. That framing is powerful for boilerplate and idiom but indifferent, on its own, to whether the result compiles or is correct.

code-interpreter.alt: An AI model writes code into an editor with a Run button, whose execution produces an Output panel of charts and a checkmark; titled Code Interpreter.
code-interpreter.caption: A code interpreter lets a model write code, run it in a sandbox, and read back the result, turning a probabilistic text generator into something that can compute exact answers and check its own work by execution. The single tool it exposes, run this code, is itself general-purpose, which is what makes it so powerful.

cognitive-architecture.alt: A figure on scaffolding lowers a knowledge banner into a head whose interior holds the standing components memory, reasoning, and action.
cognitive-architecture.caption: A cognitive architecture is the fixed structural blueprint of a mind or agent: the standing components of memory, perception, reasoning, and action, into which specific knowledge is then poured. It is the claim that intelligence needs an organized scaffold around the learning, not just one big undifferentiated model.

collective-communication.alt: Four panels showing ring synchronization across four GPUs arranged in a ring. In each successive panel a GPU sends a segment of its gradient vector to its left neighbor, which adds it in, so the accumulated segments march around the ring until every device holds the fully summed result.
collective-communication.caption: Collective operations synchronize tensors across a whole group of devices at once. This is the ring algorithm for an all-reduce: each GPU is arranged in a ring and repeatedly passes one slice of its gradient to a neighbor, which accumulates it, until every device ends up with the complete sum. Casting the reduction as a ring keeps each link busy and makes the cost almost independent of the number of GPUs.
collective-communication.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

computational-theory-of-mind.alt: A diagram of a Turing machine: a tape divided into cells holding the symbols a, b, a and blanks, a read/write head pointing at one cell, and a Finite Control box below listing states q0, q1, q2, and h with an arrow indicating a transition.
computational-theory-of-mind.caption: The computational theory of mind holds that thinking just is computation of this kind. A Turing machine has a tape of symbols (the representations), a read/write head, and a finite control that rewrites symbols by formal rules (the program). The claim is that mental states are representations and thought is the rule-governed manipulation of them, so a mind could in principle run on any hardware that carries out the right computation.

compute-efficiency.alt: A chip labeled compute drives a steeply rising curve toward a brain labeled capability, beneath a wrench labeled efficiency.
compute-efficiency.caption: Compute efficiency is how much capability a system wrings from each unit of computation, in training or at inference. It matters because raw compute is the field's largest and least elastic cost, so an algorithmic gain that does more with the same hardware can outrun simply buying more of it.

computer-use.alt: Interaction loop between an agent and a computer. At the top, an Environment of Web, Android, and PC platforms holds a state. Observations (image, textual, bi-modal, indirect) flow down the left to an Agent, which holds a policy and memory; actions (mouse, direct input, tailored mechanisms, code) flow back up the right to change the environment, forming a closed perceive-and-act cycle.
computer-use.caption: Computer use lets an agent operate software the way a person does. The agent receives an observation of the screen (often a screenshot), decides what to do, and emits low-level actions such as mouse moves, keystrokes, or code; the environment changes and produces the next observation. This perceive-act loop generalizes to almost any application without a per-app API.

concentration-inequality.alt: A plot of the probability that a sum of n random variables deviates from its mean by at least x times the square root of n. Two decreasing curves are shown: a red Hoeffding bound that drops off very fast, and a blue Chebyshev bound that drops off more slowly, with the red curve below the blue one for larger deviations.
concentration-inequality.caption: A concentration inequality bounds the chance that an average strays far from its mean. Plotted against the size of the deviation, the Hoeffding bound (red) decays exponentially and is far tighter than the older Chebyshev bound (blue), which decays only polynomially. This exponential control of large deviations is the engine beneath every generalization guarantee in learning theory.

conditional-probability.alt: Venn diagram of the numbers 1 to 10 split into a prime set and an even set, with only the number 2 in the overlap of both.
conditional-probability.caption: Conditioning restricts attention to a smaller world and renormalizes. Among 1 to 10 the even numbers are 2, 4, 6, 8, 10; given that a draw is even, the chance it is also prime is just one in five, since 2 is the only even prime.

conditional-random-field.alt: A trellis grid with five columns for time steps t=1 to t=5 and five rows for possible states i=1 to i=5, every cell a circle. Most circles are gray, and one circle per column is ringed and joined by a bold line into a single path that zigzags across the columns.
conditional-random-field.caption: Sequence labeling can be drawn as a trellis: at each position the token could take any of several labels (the rows), and the bold path is the one assignment chosen for the whole sequence. A conditional random field scores entire label paths rather than each position alone, using the transitions between neighboring labels, and selects the single best consistent path through the lattice.

conformal-prediction.alt: A model emits not a single answer but a prediction set, drawn as a box holding a question mark; titled Conformal Prediction.
conformal-prediction.caption: Conformal prediction converts any model's raw output into a set of candidate answers guaranteed, on average, to contain the true one at a chosen rate, say ninety percent. The guarantee is distribution-free and needs no retraining; the price is that the harder the input, the larger and less useful the set it must return to keep its promise.

confusion-matrix.alt: Four copies of a two-by-two confusion matrix with predicted class on the columns and real class on the rows, each highlighting a different metric computed from the true positive, true negative, false positive, and false negative cells: accuracy, precision, recall, and specificity.
confusion-matrix.caption: A confusion matrix tabulates predictions against truth, splitting them into true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives. Almost every classification metric is a ratio of these four cells: accuracy uses all four, precision divides true positives by all predicted positives, and recall divides them by all actual positives. The breakdown reveals which kind of error a model makes, not just how often it is wrong.

conjugate-gradient.alt: Contour plot of an elongated quadratic bowl with two paths from a start point x0 to the minimum x: a red steepest-descent path that zig-zags down the valley and a green conjugate-gradient path that reaches the minimum in two straight steps.
conjugate-gradient.caption: On a stretched quadratic bowl, steepest descent (red) steps perpendicular to the contours and slowly zig-zags down the valley, undoing part of its own progress each step. Conjugate gradient (green) instead picks directions that do not interfere with each other, so for a quadratic in two variables it reaches the exact minimum in just two steps.

connectionism.alt: A row of five units, some filled dark and some empty, each labeled with a feature (singular, masculine, first, second, third person), so that a single concept is encoded as the pattern across all five units.
connectionism.caption: Connectionism stores knowledge in the pattern of activity across many simple units rather than in any one place. A concept is a distributed code: here it is the particular combination of which feature units are on (dark) and which are off, so meaning lives in the whole pattern, not a single dedicated unit.

consistency-model.alt: A noise field maps through a neural network directly to a clean output in one step, short-cutting the slow dashed diffusion trajectory arcing overhead; titled Consistency Model.
consistency-model.caption: A consistency model is trained so that any point along a diffusion trajectory maps directly to the same clean result, collapsing the dozens of denoising steps a diffusion model needs into one or a few. It trades a little image quality for an enormous speedup, which is what makes near-real-time generation possible.

constitutional-ai.alt: A two-phase flow. In the supervised phase, a helpful model generates responses to red-teaming prompts, then critiques and revises each response against a principle, and the revised responses fine-tune a supervised model. In the reinforcement phase, the model compares pairs of its own responses to produce AI preference labels, which train a preference model used to optimize the final model.
constitutional-ai.caption: Constitutional AI aligns a model using AI feedback guided by a written set of principles. In the supervised phase the model critiques and revises its own answers against a principle, and the revisions become fine-tuning data. In the reinforcement phase the model ranks pairs of its own responses by how well they satisfy the constitution, and those AI-generated preferences train a preference model, shifting most of the labeling from humans to the model.

context-management.alt: Retrieved passages pass through a funnel that admits only some into a context-window box, with one item routed in by an amber arrow, beside a model.
context-management.caption: Context management decides what occupies a model's limited context window at each step, and what to summarize, retrieve, or drop, so it has what it needs without drowning in what it does not. It becomes the central engineering problem once an agent runs long enough to generate more history than it can hold.

context-window.alt: A sequence of token tiles streams through a highlighted context-window frame, above a curve labeled quadratic attention.
context-window.caption: The context window is the span of tokens a model can attend to at once, the working memory that bounds how much it can consider in a single pass. The window is costly to grow because attention scales with the square of its length, which is why so much engineering goes into using it well rather than just enlarging it.

continual-learning.alt: Earlier tasks feed a central network that then takes on a new task drawn in amber; labeled task one and task two.
continual-learning.caption: Continual learning asks a model to keep absorbing new tasks over time without erasing what it already knows. The central obstacle is catastrophic forgetting: the same weight updates that master the new task quietly overwrite the old, so the hard part is learning and remembering at once.

continuous-batching.alt: Three rows of colored blocks, one row per serving system (vLLM, Orca, and DeepSpeed), where each block is one forward pass and an upward arrow marks a pass that generated one or more tokens. The rows show how each system composes prompt and generation tokens into successive batches at every step rather than running fixed batches to completion.
continuous-batching.caption: Continuous batching schedules work at the granularity of a single decoding step, so the running batch is recomposed at every forward pass instead of waiting for a whole batch to finish. As sequences complete they are evicted and newly arrived requests are admitted, keeping the GPU saturated and sharply raising throughput under workloads where requests have very different lengths.

contrastive-decoding.alt: An expert language model and a smaller amateur model each give next-token probabilities for a prompt about where Barack Obama was born. The contrastive score is the expert log-probability minus the amateur log-probability: 1961 scores highest at 4.13, Hawaii 2.34, Washington minus 0.73. Sample continuations show greedy repeating Hawaii, nucleus drifting to Washington, and contrastive decoding correctly continuing with 1961.
contrastive-decoding.caption: Contrastive decoding scores each token by the gap between a strong expert model and a weak amateur model's log-probabilities, preferring what the expert uniquely favors. Generic or repetitive tokens that even the amateur likes (Hawaii) are cancelled out, so the method avoids the greedy loop and the nucleus drift and continues with the distinctive correct token (1961).

contrastive-learning.alt: A two-dimensional embedding space with an anchor point a1. A green arrow points from a1 to a single matching point b1 (a positive), and several red arrows point from a1 to other points b2 through b5 (negatives) scattered farther away.
contrastive-learning.caption: Contrastive learning shapes an embedding space by comparison. For each anchor it pulls the matching example (the positive b1, green) closer and pushes the non-matching ones (the negatives b2 to b5, red) farther away. Trained with many in-batch negatives like this, the model learns the single rule that distance should mean dissimilarity, which is what makes the resulting embeddings effective for dense retrieval and semantic search.
contrastive-learning.credit: Nils Reimers and the sentence-transformers contributors

control-problem.alt: A person strains to rein in a large boxed AI optimizer with a tether labeled control as an arrow labeled misalignment pulls away.
control-problem.caption: The control problem is the challenge of keeping a highly capable, goal-directed system reliably under human direction, including the freedom to correct or shut it down. The worry is that a sufficiently powerful optimizer may, as a side effect of pursuing almost any goal, resist the very interventions meant to rein it in.

controlnet.alt: Two columns. On the left, a Stable Diffusion U-Net with all of its encoder, middle, and decoder blocks marked locked. On the right, a trainable copy of the encoder and middle blocks receives the conditioning input and connects back into the locked model through zero convolution layers, with prompt and time also fed in.
controlnet.caption: ControlNet adds spatial control to a frozen diffusion model. The pretrained Stable Diffusion weights stay locked, while a trainable copy of its encoder takes the control map (an edge, pose, or depth image) and feeds it back through zero convolution layers, so the output follows that structure while the prompt still sets the content.
controlnet.credit: Lvmin Zhang, Anyi Rao, Maneesh Agrawala (Adding Conditional Control to Text-to-Image Diffusion Models)

convex-optimization.alt: Three filled shapes, each with two marked interior points joined by a straight segment. On the left a pac-man shape (a disk with a wedge cut out) is nonconvex: the segment between its two points passes through the missing wedge, outside the shape. The middle pentagon and the right rounded blob are convex: each connecting segment lies entirely inside the shape.
convex-optimization.caption: A set is convex when the straight segment between any two of its points stays inside the set; the left pac-man shape fails this because a chord exits through its notch, while the pentagon and the blob satisfy it. Convex optimization minimizes a convex function over exactly such a convex feasible region, the property that makes every local minimum a global one.
convex-optimization.credit: Aston Zhang, Zachary C. Lipton, Mu Li, Alexander J. Smola (Dive into Deep Learning)

convexity.alt: Three side-by-side plots, each a function curve with a dashed straight segment joining two points on it. Left, an upward parabola with the segment lying above the curve. Center, a wavy cosine where the segment dips below the curve between the dips. Right, an exponential with the segment lying above the curve.
convexity.caption: A function is convex when the straight segment joining any two points on its graph stays on or above the graph. The parabola (left) and the exponential (right) pass this test, their dashed chords sitting above the curve, while the cosine (center) fails it where its chord cuts below the curve, so it is nonconvex.
convexity.credit: Aston Zhang, Zachary C. Lipton, Mu Li, and Alexander J. Smola

convolutional-neural-network.alt: Data flow through the LeNet convolutional network: a 28 by 28 input image feeds a convolution to a 6-channel 28 by 28 feature map, a pooling step to a 6-channel 14 by 14 map, a second convolution to a 16-channel 10 by 10 map, a second pooling to a 16-channel 5 by 5 map, and then three fully connected (dense) layers ending in 10 outputs.
convolutional-neural-network.caption: A convolutional network interleaves convolution layers, which slide shared filters over the grid to produce feature maps, with pooling layers that downsample. Early layers detect simple local patterns and deeper layers compose them; the stack ends in fully connected layers that map the learned features to class scores. This is LeNet, the architecture that established the pattern.
convolutional-neural-network.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

coordinate-descent.alt: Contour plot of the quadratic function f(x,y) equals 5x squared minus 6xy plus 5y squared, with a red path that moves to the minimum in alternating axis-aligned segments: first a vertical move, then a horizontal move, then vertical again, each step shrinking toward the center.
coordinate-descent.caption: Coordinate descent minimizes along one axis at a time. Each red segment holds every variable fixed but one and finds the best value along that single direction, so the path moves in alternating vertical and horizontal steps. This turns a hard multidimensional problem into a sequence of easy one-dimensional minimizations that spiral in toward the minimum.

coreference-resolution.alt: In the text Maria found a book, She read it, an arrow links the pronoun she back to its antecedent Maria; labeled pronoun and antecedent.
coreference-resolution.caption: Coreference resolution is the task of figuring out which words refer to the same entity, linking a pronoun like she or it back to the noun it stands for. It is essential for any real understanding of a passage and surprisingly hard, because the right antecedent often depends on world knowledge rather than grammar alone.

corrigibility.alt: A human reaches in to adjust an AI figure that accepts the correction, a dashed arrow returning from the AI to the human; labeled human and AI.
corrigibility.caption: Corrigibility is the property of an AI that accepts correction: it lets humans change its goals, fix its behavior, or switch it off without resisting or manipulating to prevent it. It is subtle to engineer because a system rewarded for reaching a goal has an implicit incentive to avoid being stopped before it does.

counterfactual-learning.alt: A decision splits into the action taken, with its observed outcome, and the road not taken, whose counterfactual outcome stays unknown; labeled taken, not taken, and counterfactual.
counterfactual-learning.caption: Counterfactual learning tries to estimate what would have happened under actions that were never taken, the road not traveled, from data that only records the outcome of the choice that was actually made. It is the core difficulty in learning from logged decisions, recommendations, ad placements, treatments, where you never observe the outcome of the option you skipped.

covariance.alt: Grid of scatter plots: a top row showing correlation coefficients from plus one through zero to minus one, and a bottom row of strongly patterned shapes that all have a correlation of zero.
