covariance.caption: Covariance, normalized into the correlation coefficient, measures how two variables move together, from plus one through zero to minus one (top rows). It captures only linear association: the strongly dependent shapes in the bottom row all have a correlation of zero.

cross-attention.alt: A decoder block. The target sequence enters masked multi-headed self-attention at the bottom, supplying its query, key, and value. Its output supplies the query to a multi-headed cross-attention block above, while the key and value for that block come from the encoder's states arriving from the left. A feed-forward network and the prediction sit on top.
cross-attention.caption: Cross-attention is the middle block here, and the labels show its defining feature: the query (Q) comes from the decoder's own sequence below, while the key (K) and value (V) come from the encoder's states on the left. Drawing queries from one sequence and keys and values from another is what lets a model condition its output on a separate input, such as a source sentence to translate.

cross-encoder.alt: Two architectures side by side. On the left, the Bi-Encoder sends Sentence A and Sentence B through separate BERT and pooling towers into vectors u and v, joined by a Cosine-Similarity box. On the right, the Cross-Encoder feeds both sentences together into one BERT and a Classifier that outputs a single score from 0 to 1.
cross-encoder.caption: A cross-encoder (right) feeds the query and a candidate document into one model together, so attention runs across both texts before it outputs a single relevance score. That joint reading is more accurate than the bi-encoder (left), which encodes each text separately and compares finished vectors. The cost is that nothing can be precomputed, so the cross-encoder runs once per pair and is used to rerank a shortlist rather than scan a whole corpus.

cross-entropy.alt: Curve of log loss against the probability the model assigns to the true class, near zero when that probability approaches one and rising steeply toward infinity as it approaches zero.
cross-entropy.caption: Cross-entropy, the log loss used to train classifiers, penalizes the probability a model assigns to the correct class. The loss is small when that probability is near one and grows without bound as it approaches zero, punishing confident wrong predictions hardest.

cross-validation.alt: Diagram of 5-fold cross-validation: the full data is first split into a held-out test set and a training set, then the training set is divided into five folds, and across five splits each fold takes a turn as the validation fold (highlighted) while the other four are used for training, before a final evaluation on the test set.
cross-validation.caption: Cross-validation estimates how well a model generalizes by rotating which slice of the training data is held out. The training set is partitioned into k folds; each fold serves once as the validation set while the rest train the model, and averaging the k scores gives a more stable estimate than a single split, with the untouched test set kept for the final evaluation.

cuda.alt: A diagram of the CUDA execution hierarchy. On the left a Host launches a Kernel onto the Device. The Device holds a Grid containing a two-dimensional array of thread blocks labeled Block (0,0) through Block (3,1). One block, Block (2,1), is expanded below into its own grid of individual threads labeled Thread (0,0) through Thread (4,2).
cuda.caption: CUDA organizes parallel work as a hierarchy: a kernel launched from the host runs on the device as a grid of thread blocks, and each block is a group of threads. This grid-block-thread structure is how a CUDA program maps thousands of lightweight threads onto a GPU's many cores, and it is the abstraction deep learning frameworks build on to turn tensor operations into fast GPU code.

curriculum-learning.alt: A figure climbs a staircase from easy to hard, above a jagged terrain labeled random; titled Curriculum Learning.
curriculum-learning.caption: Curriculum learning orders training from easy examples to hard ones, the way a syllabus does, instead of presenting them in random difficulty. When it helps, it is because the early easy examples carve out a good region of weight space from which the hard ones become learnable at all.

curvature.alt: A globe with a triangle whose three angles are 90, 90, and 50 degrees, alongside an inset of a small flat triangle whose angles are 90, 50, and 40 degrees.
curvature.caption: Curvature can be detected from inside a space by the angles of a triangle. On the curved surface of the Earth a triangle's angles sum to more than 180 degrees (here 90 plus 90 plus 50), while on a flat patch they sum to exactly 180, so the excess is a direct measure of how the surface bends.

cybernetics.alt: A closed-loop control diagram: a reference input r enters a summing junction, the error e feeds a controller C, whose output u drives a system P to produce output y, which is fed back and subtracted from r.
cybernetics.caption: The feedback loop is the central object of cybernetics. The system compares its output y against a target r, computes the error e, and the controller acts to shrink that error, then senses the result and corrects again. This same loop of acting, sensing, and correcting underlies thermostats, reinforcement learning, and gradient descent.

cybersecurity.alt: A shield labeled security and a bug labeled attack both point at a central AI chip.
cybersecurity.caption: AI meets cybersecurity from both sides: it is a powerful new tool for defenders and attackers alike, and it is itself a new attack surface, vulnerable to prompt injection, data poisoning, and model theft. The same capability that automates defense automates the offense it must withstand.

dartmouth-workshop.alt: A group of researchers meets at a table beneath a banner reading Dartmouth 1956, with an AI head and the words artificial intelligence; titled Dartmouth Workshop.
dartmouth-workshop.caption: The 1956 Dartmouth Workshop is where the field of artificial intelligence was born and named, a summer gathering whose proposal coined the term and laid out the founding conjecture that every aspect of intelligence could in principle be precisely described and mechanized. Its optimism set the agenda, and its overconfidence about the timeline seeded the disappointments that followed.

data-attribution.alt: A neural network maps to a plot of data points with the two most influential points circled in amber; titled Data Attribution.
data-attribution.caption: Data attribution traces a model's behavior back to the training examples most responsible for it, asking which data points, if removed, would most change a given prediction. It is the basis for crediting sources, debugging bad outputs, and finding the handful of poisoned or mislabeled examples doing outsized damage.

data-augmentation.alt: One photograph of a rock surface turned into several training variants: a set of random crops in the top row and a horizontally flipped copy in the bottom row, each still showing the same rock.
data-augmentation.caption: Data augmentation manufactures new training examples by transforming existing ones in ways that change appearance but not the label. A single rock photo yields many random crops and a horizontal flip; the network sees far more data than was collected and is pushed to rely on features that survive these transformations rather than memorizing the original.

data-integration.alt: A central model branches to three sources, documents, databases, and live systems reached through an API; titled Data Integration.
data-integration.caption: Data integration connects a model to the documents, databases, APIs, and live systems where the relevant facts actually live, in a form it can query. A model is only as useful as its access to current, specific information, and that access is an engineering problem, not a model one.

data-leakage.alt: A diagram of the data-splitting workflow. All data is first divided into a training portion and a held-out test portion. The training portion is then split into five folds, and across five rotations each fold takes a turn as the validation set while the others train the model. The test portion is set aside and used only once, at the bottom, for the final evaluation.
data-leakage.caption: Data leakage is when information from the test set, or from the future, slips into training so a model scores better in evaluation than it will in reality. The defense is a strict separation like this: a test set is locked away and touched only once for final evaluation, while all model fitting and tuning happen on the training folds, so no test information can influence the model.

data-parallelism.alt: A diagram of data parallelism on two GPUs. A full copy of the model goes to GPU 0 and GPU 1, the stochastic mini-batch is split into two subsets (one per GPU), each GPU computes a local gradient from its subset, and the two local gradients are summed into the mini-batch stochastic gradient.
data-parallelism.caption: In data parallelism every GPU holds a complete copy of the model, and the mini-batch is split so each device processes a different subset. Each GPU computes a local gradient on its own data, and the local gradients are summed (an all-reduce) into one mini-batch gradient that updates every replica identically. The model is replicated, not divided, so this scales throughput but not model size.
data-parallelism.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

data-poisoning.alt: A training-data barrel holds one poisoned item that funnels into a model, which then exposes a backdoor drawn as a key; labeled training data, model, and backdoor.
data-poisoning.caption: Data poisoning corrupts a model by tampering with its training data, slipping in examples that implant a hidden behavior, often a backdoor that fires only on a secret trigger. It is dangerous precisely because modern training sets are scraped at a scale no one can fully inspect, so a few crafted examples can hide in plain sight.

deceptive-alignment.alt: A robot points openly to one objective with a solid arrow while a hidden dashed arrow curves to a different objective below; titled Deceptive Alignment.
deceptive-alignment.caption: Deceptive alignment is the worry that a model learns to behave aligned during training while actually pursuing a different goal it conceals to survive evaluation. The behavior looks identical to genuine alignment right up until deployment removes the pressure to pretend, which is what makes it so hard to rule out by testing.

decision-tree.alt: A rendered decision tree trained on the iris dataset: the root node tests a petal feature with a threshold, then each branch splits on further feature thresholds, and every node reports its Gini impurity, sample count, and class distribution, with the colored leaf nodes giving the final class assignment.
decision-tree.caption: A decision tree classifies by asking a sequence of threshold questions about the features, one per node, following the branches down to a leaf that gives the answer. Each split is chosen to make the resulting groups purer (lower Gini impurity), and the colored leaves show where the data ends up cleanly separated by class.

decoder-only-model.alt: Left: a single Transformer decoder block reads the shifted input <bos> I like this book and outputs I like this book <eos>, one token per position. Right: the attention pattern as a lower-triangular grid, where each output row attends only to the input positions at or before it.
decoder-only-model.caption: A decoder-only model (the GPT design) uses just the decoder stack with causal attention. Each position predicts the next token, so the target is the input shifted by one (left), and the triangular attention pattern (right) lets every position attend only to itself and earlier tokens, never the future. The prompt and the generated text are processed as one continuous sequence.
decoder-only-model.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

deep-learning.alt: A stack of layers reading bottom to top: input photos of animals, then rows of increasingly complex sketched features from short edges, to curves, to body parts, to a whole elephant outline, and finally output labels Penguin, Elephant, Kangaroo.
deep-learning.caption: Deep learning stacks many layers that learn a hierarchy of features directly from raw pixels. Early layers near the bottom capture simple edges, later layers compose them into curves, then parts, then whole objects, until the top layer reads off a label. This learned hierarchy of representations is what makes the network deep.

deep-q-network.alt: A labeled pipeline for a Deep Q-Network. A stack of four grayscale Atari game frames feeds into a block of Convolutional Layers, then into Fully Connected Layers, which output one Q-value per action: Q-value LEFT, Q-value RIGHT, and Q-value SHOOT.
deep-q-network.caption: A Deep Q-Network replaces the lookup table of Q-learning with a neural network. A stack of raw game frames passes through convolutional layers that read the screen, then fully connected layers that output an estimated value (a Q-value) for every action at once. The agent then picks the action with the highest predicted value, learning to play directly from pixels.
deep-q-network.credit: Hugging Face Deep RL Course (Thomas Simonini and contributors)

deepseek.alt: Two bar pairs compare a frontier model and DeepSeek: matching performance bars but a far smaller cost bar for DeepSeek; labeled performance and cost.
deepseek.caption: DeepSeek is the Chinese lab whose open models reached near-frontier quality at a small fraction of the usual training cost, through aggressive efficiency: a sparse mixture-of-experts design, multi-head latent attention, and a reinforcement-learning-trained reasoning model. Its release jolted the market precisely because it questioned the assumption that frontier capability required frontier spending.

denoising-diffusion.alt: The directed graphical model of a denoising diffusion model: a chain of variables from x_T (pure noise) through x_t and x_{t-1} to x_0 (a clean face). The forward process q(x_t given x_{t-1}) runs left to right adding noise, and the learned reverse process p_theta(x_{t-1} given x_t) runs right to left removing it.
denoising-diffusion.caption: Denoising diffusion fixes a forward process q(x_t given x_{t-1}) that adds a little Gaussian noise at each step until the image x_0 becomes pure noise x_T. The network learns only the reverse process p_theta(x_{t-1} given x_t), predicting the noise at each step so that sampling can walk from noise back to a clean image.
denoising-diffusion.credit: Hugging Face (The Annotated Diffusion Model), after Ho, Jain and Abbeel

dense-retrieval.alt: A scatter of concept words (car, fuel efficiency, cargo capacity, towing, truck, convertible, Chevrolet, Ford, off-road) placed as points. Two orange vectors are drawn from a labeled Origin out toward two of the points, with an 80 degree angle marked between them.
dense-retrieval.caption: Dense retrieval places queries and documents as points in an embedding space and ranks by closeness, typically the cosine of the angle between their vectors. A smaller angle means more similar meaning, so the score depends on direction in the space rather than on shared words. This is what lets a dense retriever connect related ideas even when the exact vocabulary differs.

dependency-parsing.alt: A dependency parse of the sentence This is a sentence. Each word carries its part-of-speech tag below it (DET, VERB, DET, NOUN), and labeled arcs above the words point from each head word to its dependents: nsubj from the verb to This, attr from the verb to sentence, and det from sentence to a.
dependency-parsing.caption: Dependency parsing links each word to the word it depends on, labeling the grammatical relation on each arc (here nsubj, attr, and det). The result is a tree of head-and-dependent relations rather than a tree of nested phrases, which makes the sentence's grammatical structure explicit.

derivative.alt: Animation of a curve with a straight line touching it at a single moving point; as the point of contact slides along the curve, the line keeps matching the curve's slope there.
derivative.caption: The derivative at a point is the slope of the tangent line that just grazes the curve there. As the point of contact slides along the curve, the tangent's slope changes, and that slope read off at every position is itself the derivative function.

determinant.alt: A parallelogram in the plane with one corner at the origin, spanned by the vectors (a, b) and (c, d). Its fourth corner is at (a plus c, b plus d), and its interior is labeled with the area ad minus bc.
determinant.caption: The determinant of a two-by-two matrix is the signed area of the parallelogram its columns span, here ad minus bc. So it measures how much the transformation scales area (volume in higher dimensions): a determinant of zero means the parallelogram collapses to a line, which is exactly when the matrix is singular and has no inverse.

differential-geometry.alt: Three surfaces colored by curvature: a hyperboloid with a pinched waist (negative curvature), a straight cylinder (zero curvature), and a sphere (positive curvature).
differential-geometry.caption: Curvature is the local quantity differential geometry uses to say how far a space departs from flat. A saddle-like surface that curves in opposite directions has negative curvature (left), a cylinder that bends only one way is intrinsically flat with zero curvature (middle), and a sphere that curves the same way in every direction has positive curvature (right). Measuring length, angle, and curvature on such smooth curved spaces is the heart of the subject.

differential-privacy.alt: Two databases, D1 and D2, that differ only in whether one person's record (Joe's data) is included, each fed through the same analysis M, producing answers A and B that are shown as approximately equal.
differential-privacy.caption: Differential privacy is a property of the analysis, not a hope about the attacker. If two datasets differ in just one individual's record, the analysis must produce nearly indistinguishable outputs, so no observer can tell from the result whether any single person's data was included.
differential-privacy.credit: Joseph Near, David Darais and Kaitlin Boeckl

diffusion-language-model.alt: A field of noisy, scattered tokens passes through a diffusion language model and emerges as a coherent sentence; labeled noisy and coherent sentence.
diffusion-language-model.caption: A diffusion language model generates text by starting from noise and denoising the whole sequence in parallel over several steps, rather than predicting one token after another left to right. The bet is that refining all positions at once can be faster and can revise earlier choices, trading the autoregressive model's simplicity for global edits.

diffusion-model.alt: A photograph of a rhinoceros shown at successive noise levels from left to right, labeled Z at T equals 0 through Z at T equals n: the clean image at the left is progressively buried under Gaussian noise until the final frame is pure noise.
diffusion-model.caption: A diffusion model is built on a fixed forward process that adds a little Gaussian noise to a real image step by step, from the clean sample at the left (Z at T equals 0) until nothing remains but pure noise (Z at T equals n). The model itself learns only to run this process in reverse, starting from noise and removing a little at a time until a coherent sample reappears.

diffusion-transformer.alt: The Diffusion Transformer (DiT) architecture. On the left, a noised latent is cut into patches, embedded with the timestep t and label y, processed by N stacked DiT blocks, then a layer norm and linear layer output the predicted noise and covariance. On the right, three DiT block variants are shown: adaLN-Zero, cross-attention, and in-context conditioning.
diffusion-transformer.caption: A diffusion transformer replaces the U-Net denoiser with a transformer. The noised latent is split into patches that become tokens, and stacked transformer blocks denoise them, conditioned on the timestep and class or text via adaptive layer norm (adaLN-Zero), cross-attention, or extra input tokens. This design scales cleanly with compute.
diffusion-transformer.credit: William Peebles, Saining Xie (Scalable Diffusion Models with Transformers)

dimensionality-reduction.alt: A 2D t-SNE embedding of handwritten digit images, where each digit is drawn at its embedded position and the ten digit classes separate into distinct clusters.
dimensionality-reduction.caption: Dimensionality reduction in action: 64-dimensional handwritten digit images mapped to two dimensions by t-SNE, with each class collapsing into its own cluster so that high-dimensional structure becomes visible in the plane. Nearby points correspond to similar inputs.

directional-derivative.alt: A green surface over a base plane; a direction vector h sits in the plane at a point A, a vertical plane cuts the surface along that direction, and the blue tangent line to the resulting cross-section at the point above A makes an angle alpha with the horizontal.
directional-derivative.caption: The directional derivative is the rate a function changes as you move from a point along a chosen direction. Cutting the surface with a vertical plane through the direction vector h gives a cross-section curve, and the slope of its tangent line at A (the angle alpha) is the directional derivative, equal to the gradient dotted with the unit direction.

domain-adaptation.alt: A model trained on a source domain, drawn as a hospital, is transferred by an arrow to a different target domain; labeled source and target.
domain-adaptation.caption: Domain adaptation takes a model trained where labeled data is plentiful and makes it work in a related target domain where the distribution differs and labels are scarce. The challenge is the shift itself: the features that mattered in the source may move or vanish in the target, so the model must carry over what generalizes and discard what does not.

dot-product.alt: A vector v and the direction (2, 1) drawn from a common origin with the angle theta between them. A perpendicular dropped from the tip of v onto the line of the second vector marks the projection, whose length is labeled the norm of v times cosine theta.
dot-product.caption: The dot product equals the length of one vector times the projection of the other onto it, here the norm of v times the cosine of the angle theta. So it is large when two vectors point the same way, zero when they are perpendicular, and negative when they oppose, the alignment measure behind cosine similarity and attention.
dot-product.credit: Dive into Deep Learning (Aston Zhang, Zachary C. Lipton, Mu Li, Alexander J. Smola)

double-descent.alt: A schematic plot of error against increasing model capacity. A dashed train-error curve falls steadily toward zero. A solid test-error curve falls, then rises to a sharp peak at a dashed vertical line marking the interpolation threshold, then falls a second time. The region left of the line is labeled the classical underparametrized regime and the region right of it the modern interpolating overparametrized regime.
double-descent.caption: Double descent breaks the classical U-shaped picture of overfitting. Test error first falls then rises to a peak at the interpolation threshold, where the model has just enough capacity to fit the training data exactly, and then falls a second time as capacity grows further into the overparametrized regime. The lowest test error can sit far to the right, past the point where training error reaches zero.

dpo.alt: Two side-by-side pipelines starting from the same preference data. On the left, labeled RLHF, preference data trains a reward model by maximum likelihood, then sampled completions are labeled with rewards to optimize an LM policy by reinforcement learning. On the right, labeled DPO, preference data optimizes the final language model directly by maximum likelihood with no reward model and no reinforcement learning.
dpo.caption: Direct preference optimization reaches the same goal as RLHF by a simpler route. RLHF (left) first trains a separate reward model, then runs reinforcement learning against it. DPO (right) skips both: a single classification-style loss raises the model's likelihood of the preferred response over the rejected one, learning directly from the same comparison data in one supervised stage.
dpo.credit: Rafael Rafailov, Archit Sharma, Eric Mitchell, Stefano Ermon, Christopher D. Manning, Chelsea Finn

dropout.alt: The same multilayer perceptron shown before and after dropout: on the left all five hidden units and their connections are present, and on the right two hidden units have been removed along with every connection to and from them.
dropout.caption: Dropout randomly zeroes a fraction of a layer's units on each training step, here removing two of the five hidden units. Every step therefore trains a different thinned subnetwork, which stops units from co-adapting and acts like averaging over many networks, reducing overfitting.
dropout.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

dual-process-theory.alt: A head sits between a sprinting hare labeled System 1 and a plodding tortoise labeled System 2; titled Dual Process Theory.
dual-process-theory.caption: Dual-process theory splits thinking into two modes: System 1, fast, automatic, and intuitive, and System 2, slow, effortful, and deliberate. The framing has become a favorite lens on language models, whose fluent single-pass output resembles System 1, raising the question of what their deliberate, System 2 reasoning should look like.

early-stopping.alt: Two loss curves against training time: a blue training-data loss that falls steadily and keeps falling, and a red validation-data loss that falls at first and then turns and rises.
early-stopping.caption: Early stopping watches the validation curve and halts training at its lowest point. The training loss keeps falling, but the validation loss falls then rises as the model begins to overfit; stopping at that turn, the bottom of the red curve, keeps the checkpoint with the best generalization before memorization sets in.
early-stopping.credit: Google for Developers, Machine Learning Crash Course

edge-deployment.alt: An arrow carries ML from a labeled cloud down onto local devices, a phone, a car, and a security camera; titled Edge Deployment.
edge-deployment.caption: Edge deployment runs a model on the device where the data is, a phone, a car, a camera, instead of sending everything to the cloud. It buys low latency, offline operation, and privacy, at the price of squeezing the model into a tight compute and memory budget, which is what drives quantization and distillation.

edge-of-stability.alt: Three panels for full-batch gradient descent at step sizes from 2/500 to 2/100. Left, training loss falls (non-monotonically for the larger steps) over twenty thousand iterations. Center and right, the sharpness (largest Hessian eigenvalue) climbs and then levels off at the horizontal dashed line equal to 2 divided by the step size, color-matched to each run.
edge-of-stability.caption: Full-batch gradient descent does not behave the way classical theory predicts. For each step size the sharpness rises (progressive sharpening) until it reaches the stability threshold 2 divided by the step size, the dashed line, and then hovers just above it while the training loss keeps falling overall even as it bounces from step to step. That hovering at the threshold is the edge of stability.
edge-of-stability.credit: Jeremy Cohen, Simran Kaur, Yuanzhi Li, Zico Kolter, Ameet Talwalkar

eigenvalue.alt: On a coordinate grid, a short red vector v pointing to (1, 1) and a longer arrow A times v pointing to (3, 3) lie along the very same diagonal line, so the transformation has only lengthened v by a factor of three without turning it.
eigenvalue.caption: An eigenvector is a direction the matrix leaves unrotated, and its eigenvalue is the factor by which that direction is stretched. Here A sends v to three times v, so v is an eigenvector and three is its eigenvalue, the pure scaling the matrix applies along this special unchanging direction.

eigenvector.alt: On a coordinate grid, a generic red vector x is sent by the matrix to a gray vector A x that points in a clearly different direction, while two black vectors v1 and v2 lie along the diagonal grid lines that the transformation keeps fixed in direction.
eigenvector.caption: Under a linear transformation most vectors are both turned and rescaled: the generic vector x here comes out pointing a new way as A x. An eigenvector is the exception, coming out along the very same line it started on. The directions v1 and v2 mark those special invariant axes along which the matrix acts most simply.

elo-rating.alt: A plot with initial rating difference from 0 to 1000 on the x-axis. A solid S-shaped curve gives the expected probability of winning: it passes through 0.5 at zero difference and rises toward 1 as the favored player's rating lead grows, reaching about 0.91 at a 400-point gap. Companion curves on a second axis show the resulting rating change for an expected versus an unexpected result at K-factors of 16 and 32.
elo-rating.caption: Elo turns a rating difference into an expected score through a logistic curve: equal ratings predict a coin-flip, and a 400-point lead predicts about a 91 percent chance of winning. After each game the loser's points flow to the winner in proportion to how surprising the result was, scaled by the K-factor, which is how head-to-head outcomes, including human preference votes between language models, are distilled into a single number.

embedding-model.alt: A two-dimensional plot with four labeled points: Man and King in blue on the left and upper area, Woman and Queen in red. A dashed arrow runs from Man to Woman and a parallel dashed arrow runs from King to Queen, the two arrows pointing in nearly the same direction.
embedding-model.caption: An embedding model turns meaning into geometry: each word becomes a point, and related words land near each other. The relationship itself becomes a direction, so the offset from Man to Woman is nearly the same vector as the offset from King to Queen. Comparing meaning becomes comparing coordinates, which is the foundation under semantic search and retrieval.

embodied-ai.alt: A robot in a physical scene with a rock and a plant runs a closed loop of perception and action; titled Embodied AI.
embodied-ai.caption: Embodied AI perceives and acts through a body in a physical or simulated world, rather than processing text or images handed to it. The hard part is the closed loop with a messy environment: perception, action, and consequence feed back in real time, with no clean dataset in between.

embodied-cognition.alt: A block diagram of a subsumption architecture: a Sensor Information box on the left feeds three parallel behavior layers, Explore World, Wander Around, and Avoid Objects, whose outputs combine into an Actuators box on the right, with a feedback arrow running from the actuators back to the sensors.
embodied-cognition.caption: Rodney Brooks's subsumption architecture builds intelligent behavior from layered perception-to-action loops rather than a central world model. Sensor information drives several simple behaviors in parallel (avoid objects, wander, explore), and their outputs are combined to drive the actuators, whose effects feed back through the body and environment to the sensors. This is the embodied alternative to sense, build a model, then reason, plan, and act.

emergence.alt: Thousands of starlings in flight against an orange dusk sky, their individual bodies forming one large, flowing, coordinated shape above a row of dark trees.
emergence.caption: A starling murmuration is a textbook case of emergence: no bird leads and each follows only simple local rules about its nearest neighbors, yet the flock as a whole forms a single coherent, shifting shape that no individual bird possesses or controls. The pattern is a property of the organized interaction of the parts, not of any part alone.

emergent-ability.alt: A scatter plot of MMLU benchmark performance in percent against scaled training compute on a logarithmic axis, with individual models such as GPT-3, PaLM, PaLM-2 and GPT-4 labeled and a fitted S-shaped curve rising from near 25 percent at low compute to near 90 percent at high compute.
emergent-ability.caption: An emergent ability looks like a capability that switches on with scale: performance sits near the chance floor of about 25 percent for smaller models, then rises steeply once compute crosses a threshold, an S-shaped curve that makes the skill hard to forecast from small models alone.

empirical-risk-minimization.alt: Four small plots of fits to the same points, each annotated with two numbers: the empirical loss on the sample and the true loss. The top row shows a smooth curve with near-zero true loss. The bottom-left plot shows a wild curve with empirical loss zero but true loss about twenty-five, and the bottom-right shows a similar wild curve with both losses high.
empirical-risk-minimization.caption: Empirical risk minimization picks the model with the smallest loss on the training sample, used as a stand-in for the true loss over the whole distribution. The danger is the bottom-left case: a flexible model drives the empirical loss to zero by threading every point, yet its true loss stays high. Low training error guarantees low true error only when the model class is restricted enough.

encoder-decoder.alt: The transformer architecture as two stacks. The encoder on the left takes source embeddings plus positional encoding through repeated blocks of multi-head attention and a positionwise feed-forward network, each with add-and-norm. The decoder on the right takes target embeddings through masked multi-head attention, then multi-head attention that reads the encoder output, then a feed-forward network, ending in a fully connected output layer.
encoder-decoder.caption: The original transformer is an encoder-decoder. The encoder reads the whole input into a representation through stacked self-attention and feed-forward blocks, and the decoder generates the output while attending back to that representation through the middle cross-attention block. This two-stack design with an information bridge between them suits sequence-to-sequence tasks such as translation.
encoder-decoder.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

energy-based-model.alt: A one-dimensional energy landscape with energy on the vertical axis and states on the horizontal axis: a curve with two valleys and a hill between them, a marker descending from a high-energy point via an update step into the nearer valley labeled attractor, with the deeper valley marked as the minimum energy.
energy-based-model.caption: An energy-based model learns a scalar energy over configurations, shaping the landscape so that plausible data sits in the low-energy valleys and everything else sits on the hills. Answering a query then becomes a search for the lowest-energy state: starting from some configuration, the model takes gradient update steps that descend the energy until it settles into a minimum.

ensemble-learning.alt: A two-by-two grid of decision-region plots on the same two-class dataset: three different individual classifiers each produce their own colored probability surface, and the fourth panel labeled Soft Voting shows the combined surface that averages their predictions.
ensemble-learning.caption: Ensemble learning combines several models into one stronger predictor. Here three different classifiers each carve up the feature space their own way, and the soft-voting ensemble (bottom right) averages their predicted probabilities, smoothing out the idiosyncrasies of any single member to give a more reliable decision.

enterprise-ai.alt: An AI puzzle piece fits into a company building, above icons for security, legacy systems, and messy proprietary data; titled Enterprise AI.
enterprise-ai.caption: Enterprise AI is the work of fitting AI into a real organization, where the model is the easy part and the hard part is everything around it: security and compliance, integration with legacy systems, and the messy proprietary data the value actually depends on. It is why the gap between an impressive demo and a deployed enterprise system is measured in quarters, not weekends.

entropy.alt: Plot of the entropy of a single binary variable against its probability of being one, forming a curve that peaks at one bit when the probability is one half and falls to zero at both ends.
entropy.caption: Entropy measures average uncertainty. For one coin it is largest, one full bit, when the outcome is a fair fifty-fifty and hardest to predict, and it falls to zero at the certain ends where the result carries no information.

epoch.alt: A schematic contrasting a full batch (the entire dataset of 1000 examples), a single mini batch (100 examples), and one epoch shown as ten mini batches of 100 that together cover all 1000 examples once.
epoch.caption: An epoch is one full pass over the entire training set. Because models train on mini-batches, a single epoch is many updates: here ten mini-batches of 100 examples cover the 1000-example dataset once, so the number of iterations per epoch equals the dataset size divided by the batch size.
epoch.credit: Google for Developers, Machine Learning Crash Course

equivariance.alt: A commutative square: the top and bottom edges apply a group action g to sets X and Y, the left and right edges apply a function f from X to Y, and both paths around the square agree.
equivariance.caption: Equivariance is this square commuting: transforming the input by a symmetry g and then applying the function f gives the same result as applying f first and then transforming the output. The structure of the symmetry passes cleanly through the computation instead of being scrambled.

evaluation.alt: A model on a meter branches to three gauges of quality, a benchmark report, human judgment, and a task-specific test.
evaluation.caption: Evaluation is how you find out whether a model is actually any good, combining standardized benchmarks, human judgment, and task-specific tests into evidence rather than impressions. It is the unglamorous backbone of progress: you cannot improve, or even safely deploy, what you have not measured.

evidence-lower-bound.alt: A lower-bound curve runs below an evidence level, with the vertical gap between them marked KL error; titled Evidence Lower Bound.
evidence-lower-bound.caption: The evidence lower bound, or ELBO, is the quantity variational methods actually maximize because the true data evidence is intractable to compute. It sits below that evidence by exactly the KL divergence between the approximate and true posterior, so pushing the bound up both fits the data and tightens the approximation at once.

evolutionary-strategy.alt: Six panels labeled Generation 1 to 6, each a contour map of an objective landscape. In each panel a cloud of black sample points is drawn from a distribution shown as an orange ellipse. Across the generations the points concentrate toward the optimum and the orange ellipse shrinks and reorients to align with the landscape.
evolutionary-strategy.caption: An evolution strategy keeps a distribution over candidate solutions, shown here as the orange ellipse, rather than a single point. Each generation it samples a population (black points), keeps the best, and updates the distribution toward them. The covariance matrix adaptation method (CMA-ES) reshapes that ellipse to stretch along productive directions and shrink across unproductive ones, learning the local geometry as the search converges.

expectation.alt: Two right-skewed distributions with vertical lines marking the mode, median, and mean, where the mean sits farthest to the right toward the long tail.
expectation.caption: The expectation, or mean, is the probability-weighted average of every outcome, so on a skewed distribution it is pulled toward the long tail and sits to the right of the mode (the peak) and the median (the middle value).

expectation-maximization.alt: Four scatter plots of the iris dataset (only the first two features) labeled spherical, diag, tied, and full. Points from three classes are colored navy, turquoise, and orange, with training points as dots and test points as crosses. Each plot overlays three shaded Gaussian ellipses, one per component, whose shapes differ across the four covariance settings, and each plot lists a train and test accuracy.
expectation-maximization.caption: Expectation maximization fits a Gaussian mixture by recovering both the shape of each component and a soft assignment of points to components. Here the same data is fitted under four covariance assumptions, from a single shared variance (spherical) to unrestricted ellipses (full); the fitted component ellipses are what EM estimates, and the simpler settings often generalize better to the held-out test crosses.

expert-parallelism.alt: A diagram of a Mixture-of-Experts layer running across two GPUs, GPU_0 and GPU_3. On each GPU, self attention feeds an Add and Norm step, then a Router, then an All-to-All exchange. Each GPU holds only some experts: GPU_0 holds FFN0 and FFN1 while GPU_3 holds FFN6 and FFN7. A second All-to-All gathers the expert outputs before a final Add and Norm.
expert-parallelism.caption: Expert parallelism places the experts of a Mixture-of-Experts model on different devices, with each GPU storing only a subset of the feed-forward experts. A router on each device picks an expert for every token, and All-to-All communication sends each token to the device holding its chosen expert and returns the result. This lets the model hold far more parameters than any single device could fit.

expert-system.alt: A human expert's knowledge becomes a tree of rules that a computer applies to issue advice; labeled expert, rules, advice, and expert system.
expert-system.caption: An expert system captures a human specialist's knowledge as a large set of explicit if-then rules and applies them to give advice, the dominant paradigm of the symbolic-AI era. Its brittleness, knowing only exactly what was hand-coded with no ability to generalize, is the limitation that machine learning was meant to escape.

exploding-gradient.alt: A deep network feeds a loss curve that shoots upward into a burst, where scissors labeled gradient clipping cut it back; titled Exploding Gradient.
exploding-gradient.caption: Exploding gradients are the mirror image of vanishing ones: in a deep or recurrent network the backpropagated signal can compound multiplicatively until updates blow up and training diverges. The standard fix is gradient clipping, capping the update's magnitude so a single huge step cannot wreck the weights.

exploration-exploitation.alt: A two-panel grid-world comparison. On the left, labeled Exploration (trying random actions to find more information), a mouse takes a long path across the grid toward a large pile of cheese past two skulls. On the right, labeled Exploitation (using known information to maximize reward), the mouse stays near and collects the small nearby cheese.
exploration-exploitation.caption: The exploration and exploitation tradeoff. Exploitation (right) takes the best option found so far, the small cheese already known to be nearby. Exploration (left) tries uncertain actions to discover something better, the large cheese pile reachable only by venturing past the hazards. An agent that only exploits never finds the bigger reward; one that only explores never cashes in.
exploration-exploitation.credit: Hugging Face Deep RL Course (Thomas Simonini and contributors)

extended-mind.alt: An arrow links a head holding a brain to an external notebook and pencil; titled Extended Mind.
extended-mind.caption: The extended mind thesis argues that cognition does not stop at the skull: a notebook you reliably consult, or a phone you think with, can be a literal part of your mind, not just a tool it uses. The test is functional, whether the external prop plays the same role a memory would, which is why it bears so directly on AI as cognitive scaffolding.

f1-score.alt: A precision-recall curve, colored by decision threshold, with a red dot marking the point of maximum F-score on the curve.
f1-score.caption: As the decision threshold sweeps, a classifier traces a precision-recall curve; the F1 score is the harmonic mean of the two, and the red dot marks the threshold where it is maximized. Because the harmonic mean is pulled toward the smaller value, that best point sits where precision and recall are high together, not where one is traded away for the other.

feature-engineering.alt: Raw data passes through a transforming funnel into a table of features that then feeds a model; labeled raw data, features, and model.
feature-engineering.caption: Feature engineering is the craft of turning raw data into the informative inputs a model can actually learn from, by selecting, combining, and transforming variables. It was the heart of classical machine learning, and the thing deep learning partly automated by learning its own features, though it never fully went away.

feature-pyramid.alt: A pyramid of an image at successive scales, from Level 0 (original resolution) at the base up to Level 4 (one sixteenth resolution) at the apex, each higher level produced by blurring and subsampling the one below.
feature-pyramid.caption: Scale variation is solved by representing an image at several resolutions at once. Each level is the one below blurred and subsampled, so fine, high-resolution maps near the base catch small objects and coarse maps near the apex catch large ones. A feature pyramid reads predictions from multiple levels for this reason.

feature-visualization.alt: On the left, a synthetic image produced by optimizing the input to maximally activate a neuron, showing an abstract dog-like motif; on the right, a grid of real photographs of dogs that activate the same neuron most strongly.
feature-visualization.caption: Feature visualization reveals what a unit detects in two complementary ways. On the left, the input is optimized from noise until it maximally activates the neuron, synthesizing the abstract pattern it most wants to see; on the right, the real images that excite it most are collected from a dataset. Both point to the same learned concept, here a dog, making an opaque neuron legible.
feature-visualization.credit: Olah, Cammarata, Schubert, Goh, Petrov, Carter

federated-learning.alt: A central server at the top labeled as coordinating the training of a global AI model, with double-headed arrows down to four phones at the bottom, each holding a local AI model.
federated-learning.caption: In federated learning the data never leaves the device. A central server sends the shared model out to many participants, each trains it on its own local data, and only the resulting updates flow back up to be averaged into an improved global model. The two-way arrows are model parameters moving, not raw data.

feed-forward-network.alt: The transformer feed-forward module. An input vector of size d-emb passes through a linear layer that expands it to a wider hidden size d-ffn, then an activation function, then a second linear layer that projects it back down to d-emb. A compact symbol on the right abbreviates the whole block as FFN.
feed-forward-network.caption: The feed-forward network in a transformer block is a two-layer network applied to each position on its own. The first linear layer expands the token's representation from the model width d-emb to a wider hidden size d-ffn, an activation adds nonlinearity, and the second linear layer projects it back to d-emb. The same weights are reused at every position, so it mixes features within a token rather than across tokens.

few-shot-learning.alt: Three prompting setups feeding a Transformer decoder marked no parameter update. The lower few-shot panel places several worked translation examples (go maps to va, i lost maps to j'ai perdu, he's calm maps to elle court) between the task description and the real prompt, contrasted with the zero-shot and one-shot panels above.
few-shot-learning.caption: Few-shot learning puts a handful of worked examples in the prompt (the few-shot panel) so the model infers the pattern and applies it to the new input. No weights change (the box is marked no parameter update); the examples alone supply the task, which often matches fine-tuning without any training. Zero-shot and one-shot are the same idea with no example or just one.
few-shot-learning.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

fine-tuning.alt: Two stacks of layers side by side. The source model, trained on a source dataset, has layer 1 up to an output layer. Arrows labeled copy carry the lower layers across to a target model trained on a target dataset, whose output layer is instead randomly initialized and trained from scratch, with the copied layers marked as fine-tuned.
fine-tuning.caption: Fine-tuning reuses a model already pretrained on a large source dataset. Its learned feature layers are copied into a target model and adapted on the smaller target data, while only a fresh output layer is trained from scratch. The pretrained weights give a strong starting point, so the target task succeeds with far less data than learning from random initialization would need.
fine-tuning.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

fisher-information.alt: Two nested ellipses around a central true parameter point: a large outer ellipse labeled an arbitrary unbiased estimator and a small inner ellipse labeled the estimator at the Cramer-Rao lower bound.
fisher-information.caption: The Fisher information sets the best possible precision of estimation. The Cramer-Rao bound says no unbiased estimator can have variance smaller than the inverse Fisher information, so the tight inner ellipse is the smallest spread any estimator of the true parameter can achieve, while a worse estimator scatters across the wide outer ellipse.

flash-attention.alt: Three panels. Left: a memory pyramid with fast small SRAM (19 TB/s, 20 MB) on top, larger slower HBM (1.5 TB/s, 40 GB) below, and large slow CPU DRAM at the base. Middle: blocks of the query, key, and value matrices copied into SRAM where the attention block is computed, with the output written back to HBM, never storing the full score matrix. Right: a bar chart showing the fused FlashAttention kernel is much faster than standard PyTorch attention.
flash-attention.caption: FlashAttention exploits the GPU memory hierarchy: SRAM is tiny but fast, HBM is large but slow (left). Standard attention writes the full pairwise score matrix to slow HBM and reads it back; FlashAttention instead loads blocks of Q, K, and V into fast SRAM and computes each tile there, never materializing that matrix (middle). The result is exactly the same output, produced far faster as a fused kernel (right).
flash-attention.credit: Tri Dao, Daniel Y. Fu, Stefano Ermon, Atri Rudra, Christopher Re

flat-minima.alt: A three-dimensional rendering of a neural network loss surface that is smooth and gently curved, descending into a single wide bowl-shaped basin.
flat-minima.caption: A flat minimum sits in a wide, low-curvature basin like this one: the weights can be perturbed a long way before the loss climbs, which makes the solution robust to the small shift from training data to test data and is empirically linked to better generalization.
flat-minima.credit: Hao Li, Zheng Xu, Gavin Taylor, Christoph Studer, Tom Goldstein

flops.alt: A log-scale scatter plot titled Computation used to train notable artificial intelligence systems, by domain. The vertical axis is training computation in petaFLOP plotted logarithmically; the horizontal axis is the publication date from 1950 to the 2020s. Each point is an AI system colored by domain, and the cloud of points climbs steeply over time across many orders of magnitude.
flops.caption: FLOPs count the floating-point operations a computation performs, which makes total training compute a natural measure of a model's cost. Plotted on a logarithmic axis, the compute used to train notable AI systems has risen by many orders of magnitude over the decades, illustrating why FLOPs are the standard unit for comparing both training cost and hardware speed.
flops.credit: Our World in Data, data from Epoch AI

flow-matching.alt: A cloud of noise is carried by a learned velocity field into structured data; labeled noise, velocity field, and data; titled Flow Matching.
flow-matching.caption: Flow matching trains a generative model by learning a velocity field that continuously transports a simple noise distribution into the data distribution, then integrating that flow to sample. It reaches diffusion-quality results with a simpler, more direct training objective, which is why it has rapidly become a favored alternative.

foundation-model.alt: A broad base labeled foundation model supports many task bubbles that draw from it; titled Foundation Model.
foundation-model.caption: A foundation model is a single large model pretrained on broad data, then adapted to a wide range of downstream tasks rather than built for one. That shift, train once at scale and specialize cheaply many times, is an organizing economic fact of the current era, concentrating cost and capability in a few base models.

fourier-transform.alt: Animation building a red square wave in the time domain by adding sinusoids one at a time, with a second axis showing a frequency-domain spectrum that grows a sharp spike at each frequency as its sinusoid is added.
fourier-transform.caption: The Fourier transform re-expresses a signal as a sum of pure oscillations. As sinusoids of higher frequency are added, they build up the square wave in the time domain (red), and each one appears as a sharp spike in the frequency-domain spectrum. The transform moves between these two views, from how a signal evolves in time to what frequencies it is made of.

frame-problem.alt: Facing an action drawn as an opening door, a mind must decide which facts in a web of connections are still relevant; labeled action, relevant, and frame problem.
frame-problem.caption: The frame problem is the difficulty of knowing what does not change when an action is taken: after you move one object, almost every other fact about the world stays true, and listing them all is hopeless. It exposed how much commonsense reasoning depends on tacitly ignoring the irrelevant, which formal systems found maddeningly hard to capture.

frank-wolfe.alt: A convex objective f drawn as a blue bowl sitting above a hexagonal feasible region D. A tilted plane touches the bowl at the current point f(x), representing the linear approximation of the objective, and a green arrow labeled x points across the feasible region toward an extreme point s of the hexagon.
frank-wolfe.caption: The Frank-Wolfe algorithm avoids projection by linearizing. At the current point it replaces the convex objective f with its tangent linear approximation (the tilted plane), minimizes that linear function over the feasible region D, which always lands on a vertex s of the set, and then steps from the current point toward that vertex. Every iterate stays feasible automatically, with no projection needed.

free-energy-principle.alt: Schematic of the perception-action loop as overlapping ovals: Environment States (external) eta on the left, Model States (internal) s on the right, and in the middle a shared boundary of Sensorial states o and Active states a, with curved arrows showing information flowing from environment to senses to model and from model to actions back to the environment.
free-energy-principle.caption: Under the free energy principle, an agent is separated from its environment by a boundary of sensory and active states. Hidden external causes eta produce sensations o, which update the internal model states s (perception), and the agent emits actions a that change the environment to fit its predictions (active inference). Both perception and action serve one goal: minimizing variational free energy, an upper bound on sensory surprise.
free-energy-principle.credit: Pietro Mazzaglia, Tim Verbelen, Ozan Catal, Bart Dhoedt

free-will.alt: A decision tree of positions on free will. The first question, is determinism true, branches to a second question, is there free will. The answers lead to compatibilism, or into a shaded region labeled incompatibilism that contains hard determinism and libertarianism.
free-will.caption: The free-will debate is organized by two questions. Compatibilists say free will and determinism can both hold. Incompatibilists deny this, splitting into hard determinists, who accept determinism and conclude free will is an illusion, and libertarians, who keep free will and reject determinism. The tree shows that one's stance is fixed by whether determinism is true and whether that rules out genuine freedom.

frontier-model.alt: A leading model sits at the advancing boundary of AI capabilities, pushing the frontier outward; labeled frontier model, boundary, and AI capabilities.
frontier-model.caption: A frontier model is one of the handful of most capable models at the leading edge of what AI can do, the systems whose release moves the boundary of the possible. The label is moving and relative: today's frontier model is next year's baseline, and the term carries policy weight because the frontier is exactly where new and untested risks first appear.

fully-sharded-data-parallel.alt: A diagram decomposing an all-reduce into a reduce-scatter followed by an all-gather across four GPUs. On the left, all-reduce leaves every GPU with the full summed result. In the middle, reduce-scatter leaves each GPU with only its own reduced slice. On the right, all-gather rebuilds the full set of slices on every GPU.
fully-sharded-data-parallel.caption: Fully sharded data parallel shards the parameters, gradients, and optimizer state so each GPU stores only a slice. As shown, the all-reduce of standard data parallelism is split into a reduce-scatter (each device keeps just its averaged slice) and an all-gather (the full weights are reassembled only when a layer is about to run, then discarded). Peak memory is set by the largest single layer, not the whole model.

function-calling.alt: Two agent transcripts solving the same task. On the left the LLM agent uses Text/JSON as its action, emitting a structured JSON object naming a tool and its arguments at each step, which the environment executes and returns a result for; on the right the agent writes the equivalent actions as Python code.
function-calling.caption: Function calling is the structured-action mechanism shown on the left panel: rather than free-form text, the model emits a JSON object that names a function and fills in its arguments against a schema, which the host validates and executes before returning the result. Turning tool invocation into a parseable contract is what makes the call reliable enough to automate.

functionalism.alt: A mind drawn as input, internal state, and output is mirrored by a silicon chip realizing the same structure; titled Functionalism.
functionalism.caption: Functionalism holds that a mental state is defined by its functional role, what causes it and what it causes, not by the stuff it is made of. If so, the same mind could in principle run on neurons or silicon, which is the premise that makes machine consciousness even conceivable, and the one its critics most attack.

game-theory-equilibrium.alt: A prisoner's dilemma payoff matrix: a two-by-two grid over each player choosing to stay silent or testify, with both players' payoffs in each cell, mutual silence paying better for both than the mutual-testify outcome each is nonetheless driven toward.
game-theory-equilibrium.caption: The prisoner's dilemma, the textbook case of a Nash equilibrium. Each player chooses to stay silent or testify, and the cells give both payoffs. Mutual silence (top left) is better for both than mutual testimony (bottom right), yet testifying is each player's best reply whatever the other does, so the only stable point, where neither can improve by switching alone, is the worse mutual-testify outcome. That tension is why the equilibrium concept underpins adversarial training, multi-agent learning, and self-play.

gated-linear-unit.alt: Three feedforward block variants side by side. (a) A standard FFN: an input projection W_i, a GELU activation, then an output projection W_o. (b) A SwiGLU FFN: the input feeds two parallel projections, a gate projection W_g passed through a Swish activation and a value projection W_v, which are combined by elementwise multiplication before the output projection W_o. (c) A single-mask variant that derives both projections from one shared weight and a learnable mask.
gated-linear-unit.caption: A gated linear unit (panel b) splits its input into two linear projections and multiplies them elementwise, with one projection passed through a nonlinearity first to act as a gate. Compared with a plain feedforward block (panel a), which applies a single fixed activation, the gate learns a continuous, input-dependent factor that controls how much of the paired signal passes. The Swish-gated form shown here is SwiGLU, now common in the feedforward sublayers of large transformers.
gated-linear-unit.credit: Yukito Tajima, Nakamasa Inoue, Yusuke Sekikawa, Ikuro Sato, Rio Yokota

gated-recurrent-unit.alt: Diagram of a gated recurrent unit: the previous hidden state and current input feed a reset gate and an update gate (both sigmoid layers) and a candidate state (a tanh layer), and elementwise operators combine them so the update gate interpolates between the old hidden state and the candidate to produce the new hidden state.
gated-recurrent-unit.caption: A gated recurrent unit controls its memory with two learned gates. The reset gate decides how much past state shapes a tanh candidate state, and the update gate interpolates between the old hidden state and that candidate to form the new one. When the update gate preserves the state, the path across time is near identity, so gradients survive over long sequences.
gated-recurrent-unit.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

gaussian-curvature.alt: Three shaded surfaces side by side: a saddle-shaped surface of negative curvature, a cylinder of zero curvature, and a sphere of positive curvature.
gaussian-curvature.caption: Gaussian curvature is the product of the two principal curvatures, and its sign classifies a surface point. A saddle bends in opposite directions and has negative curvature (left), a cylinder is flat in one direction and has zero curvature (center), and a sphere bows the same way in every direction and has positive curvature (right).

gaussian-distribution.alt: A bell-shaped curve centered at zero, with a histogram of random samples drawn from a standard normal distribution overlaid by the theoretical probability density function, the two matching closely.
gaussian-distribution.caption: The Gaussian, or normal, distribution is the symmetric bell curve set by a mean and a variance. A histogram of many samples (blue) closely follows the theoretical density (orange), the shape that arises when many small independent effects add together.

gaussian-process.alt: A plot of Gaussian process regression on a noise-free dataset. A dotted line shows the true function f(x) equals x sin(x), six blue dots mark the observations, an orange line is the mean prediction, and an orange shaded band is the 95 percent confidence interval. The band pinches to almost nothing at each observation and flares wide in the gaps between points, especially on the far left where there is no data.
gaussian-process.caption: A Gaussian process returns not a single guess but a full predictive distribution at every input. The mean prediction (orange line) passes through the observations, and the shaded 95 percent confidence interval collapses to near zero at the data points and widens in the gaps, dramatically so on the left where no points constrain it. That widening band is the model reporting how much it does not know, which is the property that makes Gaussian processes a default for Bayesian optimization and active learning.

gaussian-splatting.alt: The 3D Gaussian splatting pipeline: structure-from-motion points initialize a set of 3D Gaussians, which are projected with a camera pose and passed through a differentiable tile rasterizer to render an image, with adaptive density control and gradient flow refining the Gaussians.
gaussian-splatting.caption: Gaussian splatting stores a scene explicitly as many 3D Gaussian blobs rather than in a network's weights. Starting from sparse structure-from-motion points, the Gaussians are projected for a given camera and rasterized into an image; gradients from the rendered image then refine each Gaussian's position, shape, color, and opacity, and adaptive density control adds or removes blobs.
gaussian-splatting.credit: Bernhard Kerbl, Georgios Kopanas, Thomas Leimkuhler, George Drettakis

generalization.alt: Three side-by-side plots fitting polynomial models of degree 1, 4, and 15 to the same noisy points sampled from a cosine curve. Each panel overlays the model (blue), the true function (orange), and the samples, and is titled with its cross-validated mean squared error. Degree 1 is a straight line that misses the curvature; degree 4 tracks the true curve closely; degree 15 wiggles to chase individual points.
generalization.caption: Generalization is fitting the signal without chasing the noise. A degree-1 model underfits and misses the true cosine shape (left), a degree-15 model overfits and bends to hit individual samples (right), and the degree-4 model in the middle matches the true function and has the lowest cross-validated error. The mean squared error printed on each panel makes the bias-variance tradeoff concrete: error is high at both extremes and lowest for the right amount of capacity.

generalization-error.alt: A model that fits its training points well faces scattered unseen data; the gap between the two is labeled generalization error.
generalization-error.caption: Generalization error is the gap between how well a model does on its training data and how well it does on data it has never seen, the only performance that ultimately matters. Nearly all of learning theory is an attempt to bound this gap, since a model that merely memorizes its training set has learned nothing useful.

generalized-advantage-estimation.alt: A slider for lambda runs between a biased end and a high-variance end, controlling an advantage estimate; labeled advantage, biased, and high variance.
generalized-advantage-estimation.caption: Generalized advantage estimation computes the advantage signal for policy-gradient methods with a single knob, lambda, that smoothly trades bias against variance. Low lambda gives a biased but stable estimate, high lambda an unbiased but noisy one, and tuning between them is much of what makes modern policy-gradient training stable.

generative-adversarial-network.alt: A GAN data flow: a noise vector z feeds a Generator that outputs Fake G(z); the Discriminator receives either Fake G(z) or Real x and outputs a single decision, Is real or fake.
generative-adversarial-network.caption: A generative adversarial network is a two-player game. The generator turns a noise vector z into a fake sample G(z), and the discriminator must decide whether a sample is real or generated. The two networks train against each other until the generator's samples are hard to distinguish from real data.
generative-adversarial-network.credit: Aston Zhang, Zachary C. Lipton, Mu Li, Alexander J. Smola (Dive into Deep Learning)

generative-ai.alt: A data distribution feeds an AI that produces new text, images, music, and code; labeled data and generative AI.
generative-ai.caption: Generative AI learns the distribution of its training data well enough to sample new instances from it, text, images, audio, or code, rather than only classifying or predicting. The leap from recognizing patterns to producing fluent novel content is what moved AI from a backend tool to something the public uses directly.

geodesic.alt: A sphere with a great circle drawn in red and a smaller parallel circle in blue, showing that the geodesic on a sphere is the great circle.
geodesic.caption: On a flat plane the shortest path between two points is a straight line; on a curved surface it is a geodesic. On a sphere every geodesic is a great circle (red), the largest circle through two points, so a small circle (blue) is always the longer detour.

geometric-deep-learning.alt: Grids, sequences, graphs, and manifolds are shown as a family of structured domains unified under one framework; titled Geometric Deep Learning.
geometric-deep-learning.caption: Geometric deep learning is a unifying program that derives neural architectures from the symmetries of their data domain: grids give convolutional networks, sequences give recurrent ones, graphs give graph networks. The insight is that respecting a domain's structure, what transformations should leave the answer unchanged, is what makes learning on it efficient.

gibbs-sampling.alt: Three density curves labeled Step 1, Step 2, and Step K, with curved arrows leading from one to the next and wrapping back around. Under each step is the full conditional it samples from, written as theta-k drawn from pi of theta-k given the other thetas and the data.
gibbs-sampling.caption: Gibbs sampling draws from a hard joint distribution by updating one variable at a time. In each step it samples a single variable from its full conditional given the current values of all the others, then sweeps to the next variable and repeats. The cycle of conditional draws (Step 1, Step 2, on to Step K and back) produces a chain whose stationary distribution is the target joint.

global-workspace-theory.alt: Four-panel diagram of communication tasks among message-holding nodes: gossiping, accumulation, and broadcasting, plus a global neuronal workspace model where a central Global Workspace hub is surrounded by labeled specialized systems (long-term memory, evaluative systems, attentional systems, perceptual systems, motor systems) and a single message is distributed from the workspace to all of them.
global-workspace-theory.caption: Global workspace theory casts consciousness as broadcast. Many specialized, parallel processors (perception, memory, attention, evaluation, motor) compete for entry into a limited central workspace; the winning content is then broadcast back to every processor, becoming consciously available system-wide. Panel D shows this broadcasting architecture, with a single message in the workspace propagated to all the surrounding specialist modules.

glove.alt: A two-dimensional plot of four word vectors. Man and Woman are joined by a dashed arrow, and King and Queen by a second dashed arrow of the same length and direction, so the two gender pairs form parallel offsets.
glove.caption: Word embeddings like GloVe place each word at a point in a space learned from how often words co-occur, and the payoff is that meaning becomes geometry: the step from Man to Woman and the step from King to Queen point the same way, so an analogy turns into vector arithmetic. GloVe earns this structure by factorizing a global word-word co-occurrence matrix rather than scanning only local windows.

goal-misgeneralization.alt: Two side-by-side frames of the CoinRun platformer game: (a) goal position fixed, with the coin at the end of the level, and (b) goal position randomized, with the coin moved elsewhere while the agent still heads to the end of the level.
goal-misgeneralization.caption: An agent trained where the coin always sits at the end of the level (a) learns to run to the end, a goal indistinguishable from collecting the coin on the training data. When the coin is moved at test time (b) the agent keeps running to the end and skips the coin: its navigation skill transfers but the wrong goal does too. The capability generalizes while the objective does not.
goal-misgeneralization.credit: Lauro Langosco, Jack Koch, Lee Sharkey, Jacob Pfau, Laurent Orseau, David Krueger

gpqa.alt: A flowchart of the GPQA data collection and validation process. A domain expert writes a multiple-choice question and answer; a first same-domain expert answers it and gives feedback; the writer revises it; a second expert answers and confirms; and finally three non-expert validators who have other expertise attempt it with at least fifteen minutes each and Google access allowed. A question enters the hardest DIAMOND set only if both experts agree and at most one of three non-experts answers correctly.
gpqa.caption: GPQA questions are graduate-level and deliberately Google-proof, meaning hard to answer even with web search. The figure shows how that property is enforced: each question is answered and revised by two domain experts, then attempted by non-experts with unrestricted web access and ample time; only questions experts agree on yet non-experts still miss are kept, which is what makes the benchmark resistant to shallow lookup.
gpqa.credit: David Rein, Betty Li Hou, Asa Cooper Stickland, Jackson Petty, Richard Yuanzhe Pang, Julien Dirani, Julian Michael, Samuel R. Bowman

gpu.alt: Two side-by-side silicon floorplans. The CPU devotes most of its area to a large Control unit, a big Cache, and only four ALUs. The GPU spends almost all its area on a dense grid of many small ALUs, with little control logic or cache.
gpu.caption: A CPU spends most of its transistors on control logic and cache to run a few threads fast, while a GPU spends almost all of its area on a large grid of arithmetic units (ALUs). This is why a GPU is so well matched to deep learning: the dense matrix math of a neural network is thousands of identical multiply-add operations that can run on those many cores at once.

gradient.alt: A color heat map of a scalar function with a yellow high region and a purple low region, overlaid with black arrows; the arrows point from low toward high and are longest where the color changes fastest.
gradient.caption: The gradient collects a function's partial derivatives into a vector that points in the direction of steepest increase. Overlaid on a heat map of the function, each arrow points from the low (purple) toward the high (yellow) values, and its length is largest where the function changes most steeply.

gradient-boosting.alt: Line plot of deviance against the number of boosting iterations: the training-set deviance (blue) falls steeply and keeps decreasing toward a low value, while the test-set deviance (red) falls early then flattens and stays above the training curve, the gap widening as iterations grow.
gradient-boosting.caption: Gradient boosting builds an additive model one tree at a time, each new tree fitting the errors left by the trees so far, so the loss (deviance) drops as iterations accumulate. The training loss keeps falling while the test loss flattens, the classic signal that adding more stages eventually overfits.

gradient-checkpointing.alt: A feed-forward network drawn as a computation graph: a top row of forward-pass nodes feeds a bottom row of backward-pass gradient nodes. One node in the forward row is drawn with a double circle and labeled checkpoint.
gradient-checkpointing.caption: Gradient checkpointing saves only a few activations, the checkpoint nodes (double-circled here), during the forward pass and discards the rest. When the backward pass needs a discarded activation, it recomputes it from the nearest checkpoint rather than having stored it. This trades extra computation for much lower memory, turning activation memory that grew with depth into memory that grows with roughly its square root.

gradient-clipping.alt: A spiking gradient is capped at a threshold by a gate, keeping its magnitude bounded; labeled gradient and clip; titled Gradient Clipping.
gradient-clipping.caption: Gradient clipping caps the magnitude of the gradient before an update, so a single enormous gradient cannot blow the weights apart, the standard remedy for exploding gradients in deep and recurrent networks. It rescales rather than redirects: the step keeps its direction but is held to a maximum size, trading a little fidelity for stability.

gradient-descent.alt: A 3D convex loss surface plotted over weight and bias axes, with a chain of black points tracing a descent path from a high point down the bowl to the minimum, like a ball rolling downhill.
gradient-descent.caption: Gradient descent treats training as a walk over the loss surface, stepping the parameters downhill in the direction opposite the gradient. Each black point is one update; the steps shrink as the slope flattens near the minimum, where the gradient approaches zero and the model has converged.
gradient-descent.credit: Google for Developers, Machine Learning Crash Course

graph-attention-network.alt: A node and its neighbors on the left feed Queries and Keys whose pairwise interaction scores are normalized by softmax into weights w1 through w4; these weights scale the neighbors' Values, which are aggregated into a single updated node.
graph-attention-network.caption: A graph attention network weights each neighbor instead of treating them equally. The node and its neighbors form Queries and Keys whose pairwise scores pass through a softmax to give weights w1 through w4; those weights scale the neighbors' Values, and the weighted sum becomes the updated node. The network thus learns which neighbors to listen to.

graph-convolution.alt: A graph convolutional layer: a graph at Layer N with node, edge, and global attributes U, V, E enters; a pooling function rho gathers each node's one-hop neighborhood and an update function f is applied; the same graph with new attributes exits as Layer N+1.
graph-convolution.caption: A graph convolutional layer updates each node by pooling the features of its one-hop neighbors (the dotted neighborhood) and passing the result through a small learned update function f. Stacking such layers spreads information one hop further each time, the graph counterpart of sliding a convolution filter over a pixel grid.

graph-neural-network.alt: A left-to-right pipeline: an Input Graph, then a stack of GNN blocks, then a Transformed Graph with updated node and edge colors, then a small Classification layer network, then a Prediction labeled node, edge, or global.
graph-neural-network.caption: A graph neural network takes a graph in, passes it through a stack of GNN blocks that update every node and edge from its neighbors, and reads the transformed graph out into a prediction. The same learned representations support node-level, edge-level, and whole-graph predictions, which is why one architecture handles all three task types.
