graph-rag.alt: A large knowledge graph rendered as thousands of dots. Each dot is an entity sized by how connected it is, and the dots are colored by community, forming distinct colored clusters of densely related entities spread across the plane.
graph-rag.caption: GraphRAG first turns a corpus into a knowledge graph: a model extracts entities and the relationships between them, and a community-detection algorithm clusters that graph into groups of densely related entities (the colors here). Each community is summarized in advance, so the system can traverse relationships for multi-hop questions or aggregate community summaries for global questions that no single chunk contains. The figure shows an LLM-generated knowledge graph colored by community.

graphical-model.alt: A directed graph of three nodes. RAIN points to both SPRINKLER and GRASS WET, and SPRINKLER also points to GRASS WET. Beside each node is a conditional probability table: RAIN's table is unconditional, SPRINKLER's is conditioned on RAIN, and GRASS WET's is conditioned on both SPRINKLER and RAIN.
graphical-model.caption: A graphical model represents a joint distribution as a graph of variables, with each node carrying only its conditional probability given its parents. Here whether the grass is wet depends on the sprinkler and the rain, and the sprinkler depends on the rain. The missing arrow from rain directly bypassing the sprinkler encodes conditional independence, which lets the full joint be stored as a few small local tables instead of one huge one.

greedy-decoding.alt: A grid with four columns labeled time steps 1 to 4 and rows for tokens A, B, C, and end-of-sequence. Each cell holds that token's probability at that step, and the single highest-probability cell in each column is shaded: 0.5, then 0.4, then 0.4, then 0.6.
greedy-decoding.caption: Greedy decoding takes the single highest-probability token at every step, shown by the shaded cell in each column (A, then B, then C, then end-of-sequence). It never looks ahead or reconsiders, so it is fast and deterministic but can commit to a locally best token that leads to a worse overall sequence.
greedy-decoding.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

grokking.alt: A plot of log loss against training epoch for a network learning modular addition: the training loss (blue) drops to near zero almost immediately, while the test loss (red) stays high for thousands of epochs and then falls sharply to near zero between roughly ten thousand and fifteen thousand epochs.
grokking.caption: Grokking is delayed generalization. Training loss (blue) collapses almost at once as the network memorizes the data, yet test loss (red) stays high for thousands of further epochs and only then plunges to near zero, the moment the network replaces its memorized lookup with a true algorithmic circuit. Generalization arrives long after the training set is already fit.

group-equivariant-network.alt: Two panels. On the left a scalar field: a group action g moves a blob of small blue circles to a new position but leaves each circle unchanged. On the right a vector field: the same action g moves the blob and also rotates each green arrow within it, so the arrows point in a correspondingly rotated direction.
group-equivariant-network.caption: A group-equivariant network is built so its features transform in step with the input. A scalar field (left) only moves when the input is transformed by a group element g, while a vector field (right) both moves and has its arrows rotated, each feature type following its own transformation law. Wiring these laws into the layers makes the symmetry exact by construction rather than learned from data.

group-relative-policy-optimization.alt: A schematic of one GRPO step. For each prompt a group of completions is sampled; a reward model scores each completion, and the rewards are turned into advantages by subtracting the group mean and dividing by the group standard deviation. Below, the current policy and a frozen reference policy give a per-token KL divergence. The normalized advantages and the KL term are combined and averaged into the training objective.
group-relative-policy-optimization.caption: Group relative policy optimization computes each completion's advantage by normalizing its reward against a group of completions sampled for the same prompt (reward minus the group mean, divided by the group standard deviation), so the group average serves as the baseline and no separate value network is needed. A KL penalty against a reference policy keeps the update from drifting too far.

group-theory.alt: Eight nodes arranged in two nested squares, each node showing the letter F in a different orientation; red arrows connect nodes by a rotation and blue edges connect them by a reflection, labeled with group elements e, a, a-squared, a-cubed, b, and their products.
group-theory.caption: The symmetry group of a shape, drawn as a Cayley graph. Each of the eight nodes is one symmetry of the letter F (the identity e, the three rotations a, a-squared, a-cubed, and four reflections), and following a red arrow applies the rotation generator while a blue edge applies the reflection. The picture shows a group concretely: a closed set of transformations that compose and undo, which is what group theory studies as symmetry.
group-theory.credit: Created by Denisgomes, 2007 Modified by Watchduck, 2011

grouped-query-attention.alt: Three attention layouts compared. Multi-head on the left gives every query column its own key and value. Grouped-query in the middle lets each group of query columns share one key and one value. Multi-query on the right has all query columns share a single key and value.
grouped-query-attention.caption: Grouped-query attention (middle) is the compromise between multi-head attention (left), where every query head has its own key and value, and multi-query attention (right), where all heads share one key and value. By giving each group of query heads a shared key and value, it shrinks the KV cache and memory bandwidth that dominate inference while keeping quality close to full multi-head attention.
grouped-query-attention.credit: Joshua Ainslie et al. (GQA paper, arXiv:2305.13245)

guardrails.alt: A deployed model is wrapped by a belt-and-rail structure labeled guardrails through which input passes to output.
guardrails.caption: Guardrails are the constraints placed around a deployed model to keep its inputs and outputs within safe, acceptable bounds, filtering prompts, blocking disallowed content, and checking responses. They are an admission that the model alone cannot be fully trusted, so safety is enforced at the system's edges as well as in its training.

hallucination.alt: An AI confidently asserts a plausible but false fact in a speech bubble, stamped with a red X; titled Hallucination.
hallucination.caption: A hallucination is a confident, fluent, and false statement from a model, an invented fact, citation, or detail that looks just like its correct output because it is generated the same way. It is the defining reliability problem of language models: they are trained to produce plausible text, and plausible is not the same as true.

hard-problem-of-consciousness.alt: An objective brain labeled functions sits across an arrow from a head lit by a glowing bulb labeled subjective experience; titled Hard Problem.
hard-problem-of-consciousness.caption: The hard problem of consciousness asks why physical processing is accompanied by subjective experience at all, why there is something it is like to be you, rather than information being handled in the dark. Mapping every neural function, the easy problems, seems to leave this gap entirely untouched, which is what makes it hard.

hebbian-learning.alt: A plot of synaptic change against the time between the presynaptic and postsynaptic spike. When the presynaptic spike comes first the synapse strengthens (potentiation, red, left), and when it comes after the postsynaptic spike it weakens (depression, blue, right), within a short time window.
hebbian-learning.caption: Spike-timing-dependent plasticity is the precise modern form of Hebbian learning. The synapse strengthens when the input spike arrives just before the neuron fires (potentiation, left) and weakens when it arrives just after (depression, right), so the rule encodes causal order, not mere coincidence. The two French labels read potentiation and depression.

hegelian-dialectic.alt: A diagram with three boxes: Thesis linked to Antithesis, both feeding a Synthesis box below, with a numbered arrow from the synthesis back up to the thesis position.
hegelian-dialectic.caption: The dialectic moves in stages: a thesis (1) generates its opposing antithesis (2), and the tension between them is resolved in a synthesis (3) that preserves and lifts up both. The arrow back to the top shows the process repeating, the synthesis becoming a new thesis, so development proceeds through the productive resolution of contradiction rather than by simple accumulation.

hessian.alt: A curved three-dimensional surface over the x and y plane bearing several marked critical points, including a local maximum, a local minimum, and a saddle where the surface curves up one way and down the other.
hessian.caption: The Hessian is the matrix of second partial derivatives, and it encodes a function's local curvature. At a point where the gradient is zero, the signs of the Hessian's eigenvalues classify the surface: all positive gives a local minimum, all negative a local maximum, and mixed signs a saddle point.

hidden-markov-model.alt: A diagram unrolled in time. A top row of hidden state nodes x(t-1), x(t), x(t+1) is connected by left-to-right arrows forming a chain, and each hidden state has a downward arrow to its own observation node y(t-1), y(t), y(t+1) in a bottom row.
hidden-markov-model.caption: A hidden Markov model unrolled over time. The hidden state x evolves as a Markov chain, where each state depends only on the previous one (the top arrows), and at every step the current state emits a visible observation y (the downward arrows). You see only the bottom row and must infer the hidden top row that produced it.

hilbert-space.alt: A point x above a plane E, with a vertical segment dropping to its foot P_E(x) on the plane; the segment meets the plane at a marked right angle, and dashed lines show x projected onto two lines within the plane.
hilbert-space.caption: A Hilbert space keeps the geometry of ordinary Euclidean space (length, angle, and projection) even in infinite dimensions. The key property pictured here is orthogonal projection: the projection P_E(x) of a point x onto a subspace E is the unique closest point in E, and the leftover vector from x to P_E(x) meets the subspace at a right angle. This is the same projection that underlies least squares and Fourier expansions.

hnsw.alt: Three panels of the same set of points. Layer 2 (Sparse) holds a few points with long-range links, an entry point, and a path toward the red query vector via its nearest neighbor. Layer 1 (Intermediate) has more points, and Layer 0 (Dense, All Points) holds every point. Arrows labeled descend connect the panels left to right.
hnsw.caption: HNSW stacks the vectors into layers and searches by hopping toward the query. The sparse top layer has a few long-range links that cover huge distances in a few steps, and a search descends through progressively denser layers, refining the result until it lands in the true local neighborhood of the query (red). This layered, greedy navigation is what gives the index its logarithmic-like scaling.

human-evaluation.alt: A person reads a model-output bubble and records a verdict on a clipboard of checkmarks and a star rating; titled Human Evaluation.
human-evaluation.caption: Human evaluation has people judge model outputs directly, by rating, ranking, or marking them right or wrong, because for open-ended tasks no automatic metric captures what we actually care about. It is the gold standard and the bottleneck at once: the most trustworthy signal, and the slowest and most expensive to collect.

human-in-the-loop.alt: A decision point inside an agentic workflow timeline. In the left column, current human-agent collaboration, the agent proposes an action, the human sees that action and its context, and the human then decides to approve, modify, or override it before execution. The right column shows a richer variant where the agent also surfaces alternative paths with their simulated outcomes for comparison.
human-in-the-loop.caption: Human in the loop inserts a person at chosen points in an agent's decision cycle. As the left column shows, at a consequential step the agent proposes an action and pauses for a human to approve, modify, or override it before it takes effect. The gate is placed surgically, on actions that are costly or irreversible, so oversight catches mistakes without becoming a bottleneck.

humaneval.alt: A HumanEval problem feeds a code window that is checked and scored on a rising pass-at-k bar chart.
humaneval.caption: HumanEval is a benchmark of hand-written programming problems where a model must generate code that passes hidden unit tests, scored by pass at k, the chance that at least one of k samples is correct. By grading on execution rather than text similarity, it measures whether code actually works, which is why it became a standard yardstick for coding models.

hybrid-search.alt: A column of dense vectors and a column of sparse term matches are combined under a magnifier; labeled dense and sparse.
hybrid-search.caption: Hybrid search blends dense vector retrieval, which matches meaning, with sparse keyword retrieval like BM25, which matches exact terms, then fuses their rankings. Each covers the other's blind spot: embeddings find paraphrases, while keywords catch the rare name or code string that an embedding blurs away.

hyde.alt: A query generates a hypothetical document whose embedding is used to retrieve similar real documents; labeled hypothetical document, embedding, and real documents.
hyde.caption: HyDE, hypothetical document embeddings, answers a query by first having a model write a fake answer, then embedding that and retrieving the real documents most like it. The trick is that a hypothetical answer sits closer in embedding space to the true sources than the terse question does, so the detour through a guess improves retrieval.

hyena.alt: Diagram of the Hyena operator drawn as a recurrence: an input u is projected, then alternately passed through long-convolution matrices labeled S (shown as banded Toeplitz matrices) and data-controlled diagonal gating matrices labeled D, repeated several times to the output y; on the right, the long filters are generated by a small feed-forward network with a window and positional encoding.
hyena.caption: Hyena replaces self-attention with a recurrence of two cheap primitives applied in alternation: very long convolutions (the Toeplitz matrices S, giving a global receptive field) and element-wise multiplicative gating controlled by the input (the diagonal matrices D). The convolution filters are not stored explicitly but generated by a small feed-forward network from each position (right), which keeps the parameter count independent of sequence length and lets the long convolutions run efficiently with the fast Fourier transform.

hyperbolic-embeddings.alt: Image embeddings placed inside the Poincare disk model of hyperbolic space, with thumbnails and points arranged from the center toward the bounding circle.
hyperbolic-embeddings.caption: Embeddings in the Poincare disk, a model of negatively curved hyperbolic space: data is placed inside the disk where exponentially growing volume toward the boundary lets hierarchies spread out with low distortion, unlike flat Euclidean space.
hyperbolic-embeddings.credit: Valentin Khrulkov and collaborators (Hyperbolic Image Embeddings)

hypernetwork.alt: A small network generates the weights that populate a larger network; labeled hypernetwork, weights, and network.
hypernetwork.caption: A hypernetwork is a network whose output is the weights of another network, rather than a prediction. It lets one compact model produce many task- or input-specific models on the fly, trading a direct parameterization for an indirect one that can be far smaller and can adapt instantly.

hyperparameter-optimization.alt: A two-dimensional hyperparameter space with sampled configurations marked as crosses scattered over a contour map of model performance, and rug marks along each axis showing the distinct values tried for that hyperparameter.
hyperparameter-optimization.caption: Random search picks hyperparameter configurations at random across the space rather than on a fixed grid. Because each trial uses a fresh value on every axis (the rug marks along the edges), random search explores many distinct settings of each hyperparameter for the same budget, which finds good configurations faster than grid search when only a few hyperparameters truly matter.

hypothesis-testing.alt: A null-distribution bell curve with a significance threshold marks an observed effect that falls in its far tail; labeled null, threshold, and effect.
hypothesis-testing.caption: Hypothesis testing asks how surprising the data would be if nothing were going on, the null hypothesis, and rejects that null only when the observed effect lands far enough into the tail to be unlikely by chance. The framework is powerful and constantly misread: it bounds false alarms, but a significant result is not the same as a large or important one.

image-captioning.alt: An image captioning model: an input photo of a surfer is read by a convolutional encoder into a grid of image features, which a transformer decoder attends to through cross-attention while generating the caption one word at a time, starting from a start token and producing a surfer riding on.
image-captioning.caption: Image captioning joins a visual encoder that reads the picture to a language decoder that writes the sentence. The encoder turns the image into feature vectors, and the decoder attends to those features as it generates the caption word by word, the inverse of text-to-image generation.

image-classification.alt: LeNet data flow for image classification: a 28 by 28 input image passes through a convolution layer producing six feature maps, a pooling layer, a second convolution to sixteen feature maps, another pooling layer, then three fully connected (dense) layers narrowing to ten output units.
image-classification.caption: Image classification compresses a whole image down to one labeled decision. The convolution layers learn visual features, pooling shrinks the maps, and the final dense layers score each candidate category, here a probability over ten possible digits.
image-classification.credit: Aston Zhang, Zachary C. Lipton, Mu Li, Alexander J. Smola (Dive into Deep Learning)

image-tokenization.alt: Vision transformer pipeline: an image is cut into a 3 by 3 grid of patches, the patches plus a class token are turned into a sequence by a patch embedding with positional embeddings, the sequence passes through repeated transformer encoder blocks (normalization, multi-head attention, MLP), and a head reads a label from the class token output.
image-tokenization.caption: Image tokenization turns a continuous image into a sequence a transformer can read. The picture is cut into fixed-size patches, each patch is flattened and linearly projected into a token embedding, and a positional embedding records where each patch sat, so the patches become tokens just like words in a sentence.
image-tokenization.credit: Aston Zhang, Zachary C. Lipton, Mu Li, Alexander J. Smola (Dive into Deep Learning)

imitation-learning.alt: A robot policy follows the trail left by a human expert; titled Imitation Learning.
imitation-learning.caption: Imitation learning teaches an agent a skill by having it copy expert demonstrations rather than discover behavior through trial and reward. It sidesteps the hard problem of designing a reward function, at the cost of being only as good as its demonstrations and fragile once it drifts into situations the expert never showed it.

implicit-regularization.alt: On a rugged loss landscape, the optimization path settles into a wide, flat minimum rather than a sharp one; labeled optimization and loss; titled Implicit Regularization.
implicit-regularization.caption: Implicit regularization is the way the training procedure itself, gradient descent and especially its stochastic noise, quietly biases a model toward simpler, better-generalizing solutions even with no explicit penalty added. It is a leading explanation for why hugely overparameterized networks, free to memorize, instead tend to find solutions that work on new data.

importance-sampling.alt: Samples are drawn from a proposal distribution and reweighted to estimate quantities under a different target distribution; labeled proposal, target, samples, and reweighting.
importance-sampling.caption: Importance sampling estimates an average under one distribution while drawing samples from a different, more convenient one, then corrects the mismatch by weighting each sample by the ratio of the two densities. It is what lets you reuse off-policy data or sample rare events efficiently, as long as the proposal does not miss regions the target cares about.

in-context-learning.alt: Three prompting setups, each feeding a Transformer decoder marked no parameter update. Zero-shot gives only a task description and the prompt. One-shot adds a single worked example. Few-shot adds several worked examples. All three produce the same translated output.
in-context-learning.caption: In-context learning is the model learning a task from its prompt at inference time, with no parameter update (note the label on every box). Given a task description and zero, one, or several worked examples before the real prompt, the model infers the pattern and applies it. Adding examples in the context, not retraining, is what steers the output.
in-context-learning.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

induction-head.alt: An attention-weight heatmap for an induction head (GPT-2 Small, layer 5, head 1) on the repeated sequence The cat sat on the mat. The cat sat on the. The axes are destination tokens (query) against source tokens (key). In the repeated second half, each token attends back along an off-diagonal stripe to the token that followed its first occurrence, for example the second cat attending to the first sat.
induction-head.caption: An induction head implements in-context copying: if the current token appeared earlier, it attends back to whatever followed that earlier occurrence and predicts it again. The off-diagonal stripe in the repeated half of this attention pattern is the signature, each repeated token reaching back to the token that came after its first appearance, which is the match-and-copy rule behind in-context learning.

inductive-bias.alt: A scatter of noisy data following a wavy periodic pattern, overlaid with a true curve and the fits of three different regression models. The fits agree closely where data is dense but diverge from one another in the region to the right where there are fewer points, each extrapolating in a different way.
inductive-bias.caption: Each model carries an inductive bias, a built-in assumption about what the unseen data looks like. Here several regression methods fit the same noisy points well where data is plentiful but extrapolate differently where it is sparse, because each one's prior (for example a periodic kernel) fills the gaps in its own way. The data alone cannot decide between them; the assumptions do.

information-bottleneck.alt: A schematic of the information plane. The horizontal axis is I(z;x), the mutual information between the representation and the input, and the vertical axis is I(z;y), the mutual information between the representation and the target. A trajectory of red dots first moves right and up (the fitting phase, I(z;x) increases) and then curves back left while staying high (the compression phase, I(z;x) decreases).
information-bottleneck.caption: The information bottleneck is stated in exactly the two quantities this plane tracks: how much a representation tells you about the input, I(z;x), against how much it tells you about the target, I(z;y). A good representation pushes up I(z;y) while squeezing down I(z;x), the compression phase shown here, keeping what predicts the label and discarding the rest of the input.

information-geometry.alt: A family of probability distributions forms a curved manifold on which a geodesic connects two distributions; labeled probability distribution, geodesic, and parameter.
information-geometry.caption: Information geometry treats a family of probability distributions as a curved surface, where each point is a distribution and the natural distance between nearby ones is the Fisher information. Recasting statistics as geometry explains why the straight line in parameter space is the wrong path and the geodesic on this manifold is the right one, the idea behind the natural gradient.

inpainting.alt: A damaged old black-and-white photograph on the left, covered in white tears, scratches, and missing patches, and on the right the same photograph fully restored with the damage filled in seamlessly.
inpainting.caption: Inpainting, the task in its purest form: the damaged regions of this old photograph (left) are filled in to match their surroundings while the rest of the image is preserved (right). Classical restoration did this by hand; modern diffusion-based inpainting automates the same masked-region fill, which is what makes it the workhorse of controllable image editing and object removal.
inpainting.credit: Costica Axinte (original); Bruce C. Cooper (digital restoration)

instance-segmentation.alt: The same photo of balloons shown under four vision tasks: classification (one label), semantic segmentation (all balloons one color), object detection (a labeled box per balloon), and instance segmentation (a distinct colored mask and box for each balloon).
instance-segmentation.caption: The four core vision tasks on one scene. Classification gives a single label; semantic segmentation paints every balloon the same class color; object detection draws a box per object; instance segmentation, bottom right, gives each individual balloon its own pixel-accurate mask, separating two balloons of the same class as distinct instances.

instruction-tuning.alt: A worked instruction-tuning task. A boxed Definition states the task in natural language, a Positive Examples box lists input-output pairs with explanations as demonstrations, and an Evaluation Instances box shows new inputs for which the model produces outputs, illustrating a single task expressed entirely as a written instruction plus examples.
instruction-tuning.caption: Instruction tuning trains a model on many tasks each expressed as a natural-language instruction: a definition plus a few demonstration examples, paired with the desired responses. Trained across a broad mix of such tasks, the model learns to read a prompt as a command to carry out and generalizes to unseen instruction types it was never explicitly shown, the behavior that turns a base model into a usable assistant.
instruction-tuning.credit: Yizhong Wang, Swaroop Mishra, Yeganeh Kordi, et al.

instrumental-convergence.alt: Three sub-goals, self-preservation, resource acquisition, and resisting goal modification, converge into a single arrow toward a final goal; titled Instrumental Convergence.
instrumental-convergence.caption: Instrumental convergence is the observation that a wide range of final goals share the same useful sub-goals: staying operational, acquiring resources, and preserving one's objective. It is central to AI risk because it predicts that even a system with a benign end goal may, as a means, resist shutdown or grab resources unless explicitly designed not to.

integral.alt: A smooth curve labeled f(x) rising across the plane, with the region between the curve and the horizontal axis from x equals a to x equals b shaded gray and annotated with the definite integral symbol for f(x) dx evaluated from a to b.
integral.caption: A definite integral is the signed area between a curve and the horizontal axis over an interval. Here the shaded region under f(x) from a to b is exactly the value of the integral of f(x) dx from a to b, the quantity the integral notation names.

integrated-information-theory.alt: A five-panel schematic, labeled A through E, showing how integrated information theory identifies a substrate of consciousness for a small system of three connected units a, b, and c. Panel A, Existence, shows the units and their transition probability matrix. Panel B, Intrinsicality, restricts attention to the system's effect on itself. Panel C, Information, shows the system selecting a specific cause and effect state. Panel D, Integration, partitions the system and computes the integrated information phi-s, reporting a value of 0.17. Panel E, Exclusion, compares candidate subsets to pick the one with maximal integrated information as the complex.
integrated-information-theory.caption: Integrated Information Theory identifies a conscious system by applying five postulates in turn. Starting from a substrate and its transition probabilities (Existence), the theory keeps only the system's cause-effect power over itself (Intrinsicality), the specific state it selects (Information), then partitions the system and measures how much causal power is lost, a quantity called phi (Integration), here 0.17. Exclusion then picks the subset whose phi is maximal as the actual seat of experience. A system is conscious to the degree that its parts form one irreducible causal whole, which is exactly what a nonzero phi records.

intentional-stance.alt: Belief and desire thought bubbles, a lightbulb and a heart, are attributed alike to a robot and a human; titled Intentional Stance.
intentional-stance.caption: The intentional stance is Dennett's strategy of predicting a system's behavior by treating it as a rational agent with beliefs and desires, without claiming anything about how it works inside. It pays its way by prediction, which is why we apply it to thermostats, animals, and chatbots without first settling whether any of them really has a mind.

intentionality.alt: An original mind whose states are about the world is contrasted with a machine whose aboutness, directed at a target, is derived; labeled original and derived.
intentionality.caption: Intentionality is the aboutness of mental states, the way a thought can be about coffee or a name can refer to a person. The deep question for AI is whether a model's symbols have original intentionality, genuinely about the world, or only derived intentionality, meaningful merely because we interpret them, the way ink on a page means nothing on its own.

inverse-reinforcement-learning.alt: From an expert's demonstrated behavior, a magnifier infers the underlying reward function, which is then handed to an agent; labeled expert, reward, and agent.
inverse-reinforcement-learning.caption: Inverse reinforcement learning runs reinforcement learning backward: instead of optimizing a known reward, it observes an expert's behavior and infers the reward function that would make that behavior optimal. It matters because the reward is usually the hardest thing to specify by hand, so recovering it from demonstration sidesteps the most error-prone step.

isolated-execution.alt: Code runs inside a locked sandbox kept separate from the host's files and data; titled Isolated Execution.
isolated-execution.caption: Isolated execution runs code, especially code a model wrote or a user supplied, inside a sandbox walled off from the host system's files, network, and secrets. It is the load-bearing safety primitive for agents that act: it lets them try things that might fail or misbehave without letting a mistake or an attack reach anything that matters.

jacobian.alt: On the left a regular square grid with a red unit square and two basis arrows; a map f sends them to a curved, deformed grid on the right where the red region is stretched and rotated and the two arrows now span a parallelogram.
jacobian.caption: The Jacobian is the matrix of first partial derivatives that best approximates a vector-valued map by a linear one near a point. It sends the square grid on the left to the warped grid on the right, and the factor by which it scales the area of the small red region is its determinant.

jailbreak.alt: Flow diagram: an attacker supplies an adversarial prompt that jailbreaks an LLM, the model returns a response, and that response is meant to match the attacker's desired output, which is the attack goal.
jailbreak.caption: A jailbreak is an adversarial prompt that slips past a model's refusal training to reach the capability underneath. The attacker crafts an input that steers the model's response toward content it was trained to refuse, so the output matches the attacker's goal rather than the safety policy.

jensens-inequality.alt: An upward-curving function with three of its points joined by chords; a dashed vertical segment shows that the averaged point on the chords sits directly above the point on the curve at the same averaged input.
jensens-inequality.caption: Jensen's inequality says that for a convex function the function of an average is at most the average of the function. Because the chords joining points on the curve lie above it, the averaged height on the chords (top of the dashed line) exceeds the curve's height at the averaged input (bottom), which is the gap that drives the evidence lower bound and the non-negativity of KL divergence.

joint-embedding-predictive-architecture.alt: An encoder maps an input into an embedding space where a predictor infers the embedding of a masked part; labeled encoder, embedding space, and predictor.
joint-embedding-predictive-architecture.caption: A joint-embedding predictive architecture learns by predicting the representation of a missing part of an input from the visible part, in embedding space rather than pixel space. Predicting abstract features instead of every detail lets it ignore unpredictable noise and capture structure, which is the bet behind it as a path to world models that learn more like animals do.

k-means.alt: Animation of k-means clustering: data points colored by their assigned cluster, with two centers that move each iteration as points are reassigned to the nearest center, until the assignments stop changing.
k-means.caption: K-means alternates two steps until it settles. Each point is assigned to the nearest cluster center, then every center is recomputed as the mean of the points now assigned to it. Repeating these steps, shown here over successive iterations, steadily reduces the within-cluster spread and converges to a stable partition.

k-nearest-neighbors.alt: Two side-by-side scatter plots of the iris dataset (sepal length versus sepal width) showing k-nearest-neighbors classification with k equals 11; colored background regions mark the predicted class for every point in the plane, with uniform weighting on the left and distance weighting on the right, and the training points overlaid in three class colors.
k-nearest-neighbors.caption: k-nearest-neighbors classifies a new point by the majority vote of its k closest labeled neighbors, with no training step beyond storing the data. The colored regions show the resulting decision boundary over the feature space; weighting neighbors by distance (right) lets closer points count for more than far ones (left).

kalman-filter.alt: Diagram illustrating the predict and update cycle of the Kalman filter estimating a system state over time
kalman-filter.caption: The Kalman filter alternates predict and update: it projects the state forward, then corrects that estimate with each noisy measurement to track the truth.

kernel-fusion.alt: A transformer layer's operations drawn as a dataflow graph, with dashed boxes grouping consecutive operations into fused regions: a QKV box around the input normalization and the query, key, and value projections; an attention box; an MLP box around the feed-forward layers and activation; and a bias-add box. Each fused region is annotated with a speedup such as 1.5x, 2.9x, 3x.
kernel-fusion.caption: Kernel fusion merges a chain of small operations into one GPU kernel. Here consecutive steps of a transformer layer (the QKV projections, the attention math, the feed-forward block) are each grouped into a single fused kernel instead of many separate ones. Because the intermediate results stay in fast on-chip memory rather than being written to and read back from main memory, each fused region runs several times faster even though the arithmetic is unchanged.

kernel-method.alt: Two panels: on the left, red points clustered inside a ring of purple points in two dimensions, not separable by a straight line; on the right, the same points lifted into three dimensions where the red points sit lower and a flat plane can separate them.
kernel-method.caption: Data that no straight line can separate in its original space (left) often becomes linearly separable after being mapped into a higher dimension (right). The kernel trick computes the needed inner products in that richer space directly from the original points, so a linear method can draw a nonlinear boundary without ever building the high-dimensional coordinates.

kl-divergence.alt: Two bar charts side by side over the outcomes 0, 1, 2: distribution P is a skewed binomial, distribution Q is flat and uniform.
kl-divergence.caption: KL divergence measures how far one distribution is from another. It is the extra cost, in bits, of encoding data drawn from the true distribution P using a code built for the model distribution Q, and it is zero only when the two match.

knowledge-argument.alt: A cartoon of Mary, drawn entirely in grey, stepping out of a grey doorway into a vivid color world of green grass, a tree with a brown trunk, a blue sky, and red and yellow flowers, looking surprised.
knowledge-argument.caption: Mary's Room dramatizes the knowledge argument. The grey scientist knew every physical fact about color while confined to a black-and-white room, yet on stepping out and seeing color for the first time she seems to learn something genuinely new, what red is actually like. If complete physical knowledge still leaves that out, the argument concludes, the felt quality of experience is not captured by the physical facts.

knowledge-bases.alt: Documents and tables feed a central knowledge base that a model then queries; labeled knowledge base and model.
knowledge-bases.caption: A knowledge base is a structured, queryable store of facts, drawn from documents, tables, and databases, that a model can consult instead of relying only on what it memorized in training. It is how a system stays current and grounded, separating slow-changing knowledge from the model that reasons over it.

knowledge-distillation.alt: A flow diagram. An input x feeds both a teacher model and a smaller student model. The teacher's logits pass through a softmax with temperature to give soft labels. The student produces soft predictions at the same temperature and a hard prediction at temperature one; a distillation loss compares the student's soft predictions to the teacher's soft labels, and a student loss compares the hard prediction to the ground-truth label.
knowledge-distillation.caption: Knowledge distillation trains a small student model to imitate a large teacher rather than learning only from hard labels. The teacher's full probability distribution, softened with a temperature, becomes a soft target that carries which wrong answers are nearly right. The student minimizes a distillation loss against those soft labels, often blended with the ordinary loss on the true label, inheriting much of the teacher's competence in a smaller network.

knowledge-graph.alt: A small knowledge graph: nodes labeled Dogs, Cows, Animals, Plants, Herbs, and Living Things, joined by labeled arrows such as 'is' and 'eat'.
knowledge-graph.caption: A knowledge graph stores facts as entities joined by labeled relationships: Dogs is-a Animals, Animals is-a Living Things, Cows eat Herbs. Because the edges are typed and explicit, a system can traverse them to answer a multi-hop question, following the is-a and eat links from one fact to the next, which a flat vector store of documents cannot reconstruct.

kolmogorov-arnold-network.alt: A two-column comparison. The left column, Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP), shows a network with fixed nonlinear activation functions sitting on the nodes and learnable linear weights on the edges, annotated with the universal approximation theorem. The right column, Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN), shows learnable univariate activation functions (drawn as small spline curves) sitting on the edges and simple summation on the nodes, annotated with the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem.
kolmogorov-arnold-network.caption: A Kolmogorov-Arnold Network moves the nonlinearity off the nodes and onto the edges: where an MLP applies a fixed activation at each node and learns linear weights on the connections, a KAN learns the shape of a univariate function (a spline) on each connection and merely sums at the nodes. The design draws on the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem that any continuous multivariate function can be built from sums of functions of one variable.
kolmogorov-arnold-network.credit: Ziming Liu, Yixuan Wang, Sachin Vaidya, et al.

kto.alt: A value function plotted with loss on the left and gain on the right of a horizontal axis crossing a marked reference point. The Kahneman-Tversky curve is concave for gains and steeper for losses (loss aversion), with the DPO and PPO-Clip implied value functions drawn for comparison.
kto.caption: KTO borrows the prospect theory value function of Kahneman and Tversky: value is measured relative to a reference point, gains are concave, and losses loom larger than equal gains (loss aversion). KTO scores each response by how far its likelihood has moved from a frozen reference model, then applies this asymmetric value shape, so it can learn from individual responses labeled simply desirable or undesirable rather than from paired comparisons.
kto.credit: Kawin Ethayarajh, Winnie Xu, Niklas Muennighoff, Dan Jurafsky, Douwe Kiela

kv-cache.alt: A transformer generating text stores each past token's key and value matrices in a cache rather than recomputing them; labeled key, value, and generating transformer; titled KV Cache.
kv-cache.caption: The KV cache stores the key and value vectors a transformer has already computed for earlier tokens, so generating each new token reuses them instead of recomputing attention over the whole sequence from scratch. It is what makes autoregressive generation fast, and the memory it consumes, growing with every token, is one of the main limits on how long a context can be served.

lagrange-multiplier.alt: Concentric dashed level curves of an objective function f, each with small gradient arrows pointing inward, crossed by a red constraint curve g equals c; the red and dashed curves are tangent where the constrained optimum sits.
lagrange-multiplier.caption: The geometry behind Lagrange multipliers. The dashed curves are level sets of the objective f, and the red curve is the constraint g equals c. At the constrained optimum the constraint curve is tangent to a level curve, so the two gradients line up; the multiplier is the scalar that expresses how much one gradient must be scaled to equal the other. Where the curves merely cross, you can still slide along the constraint to a better value.

large-language-model.alt: Three iterations of autoregressive generation by a language model. In iteration one, the tokens What is in are fed to the model and it outputs the. In iteration two, that word is concatenated to the input What is in the and the model outputs box. In iteration three, the input is What is in the box and the model outputs the next token. Each step appends the model's previous output to the running sequence.
large-language-model.caption: A large language model generates text autoregressively: it predicts the next token from all the tokens so far, appends that token to the sequence, and repeats. This single next-token objective, scaled up over billions of parameters and trillions of training tokens, is what gives these models their broad command of language and reasoning.

late-interaction.alt: A diagram of ColBERT. A Query Encoder produces a row of green per-token vectors and a Document Encoder produces a grid of blue per-token vectors (marked offline indexing). For each query token, a MaxSim operator selects its best-matching document token, and the MaxSim values are summed into a single score.
late-interaction.caption: Late interaction keeps a vector for every token instead of pooling each text to one vector. The document tokens (blue) are encoded and indexed offline; at query time each query token (green) finds its best-matching document token via MaxSim, and those best matches are summed into the relevance score. Doing the interaction late, after encoding, preserves term-level detail while keeping the document side precomputable.

latent-diffusion.alt: Block diagram of latent diffusion with three regions: a pink Pixel Space where an encoder E maps an image x to a latent z and a decoder D reconstructs it, a green Latent Space where a denoising U-Net runs the diffusion and denoising steps on the latent with cross-attention, and a Conditioning column feeding text, semantic maps, and images in through an encoder tau.
latent-diffusion.caption: Latent diffusion runs the diffusion process in the compact latent space of an autoencoder instead of on raw pixels. An encoder E compresses the image to a latent, the denoising U-Net does the iterative generation there while a condition such as text enters through cross-attention, and only at the end does the decoder D expand the latent back to a full-resolution image.
latent-diffusion.credit: Rombach, Blattmann, Lorenz, Esser and Ommer (CompVis, High-Resolution Image Synthesis with Latent Diffusion Models)

latent-semantic-analysis.alt: An animated term-document matrix with words such as air, aliens, arms, defense, nuclear, space and treaty on the rows and documents d0 to d19 on the columns, each cell shaded by how often the word appears in the document. The animation reorders rows and columns so darker cells gather into blocks.
latent-semantic-analysis.caption: Latent semantic analysis starts from a term-document matrix, where each cell records how often a word appears in a document. By factorizing this matrix with singular value decomposition and keeping only the largest components, it recovers a small set of latent topics: groups of words and documents that vary together, here emerging as blocks. Words and documents then live in one low-dimensional space where similarity can be measured even without shared words.

latent-space.alt: A grid of decoded MNIST digit images sampled across a 2D latent space, with axes labeled z[0] and z[1], showing digits morphing smoothly from one shape to another as the coordinates change.
latent-space.caption: The learned latent space of a variational autoencoder, decoded on a 2D grid: moving across the coordinates z[0] and z[1] smoothly transforms one digit into another, so proximity encodes similarity and the axes act as interpretable directions of variation.

latent-variable-model.alt: A plate-notation graphical model. Hyperparameters alpha and beta sit outside two nested rectangles (plates). Inside, an arrow runs from an unshaded node theta to an unshaded node z to a single shaded node w, with beta also pointing into w. The inner plate around z and w is labeled N and the outer plate is labeled M.
latent-variable-model.caption: A latent variable model explains observed data through hidden causes, drawn here in plate notation. Only the shaded node w (an observed word) is measured; the unshaded nodes theta and z are latent variables that generate it, and the nested rectangles (plates) mean the structure repeats over N words and M documents. Inference is the task of recovering the hidden nodes from the one shaded node.

layer-normalization.alt: Across the features of each example, an arbitrary distribution is rescaled to a normalized one; labeled features and layer normalization.
layer-normalization.caption: Layer normalization rescales the activations within each individual example to a stable mean and variance, computed across its features rather than across the batch. That independence from batch size and sequence position is why it, not batch normalization, became the default inside transformers.

lbfgs.alt: A contour plot of the curved, banana-shaped Rosenbrock valley. A blue path marked with plus signs follows the bottom of the valley from the upper right around the bend to the minimum, marked with a red X, with small dots showing where the gradient was evaluated.
lbfgs.caption: A BFGS quasi-Newton path following the curved Rosenbrock valley to its minimum. L-BFGS is the limited-memory form of this method: instead of storing a full curvature matrix it keeps only a short history of recent gradient and step pairs and rebuilds the search direction from them, gaining most of this fast, curvature-aware behavior at a memory cost only a few times that of gradient descent.
lbfgs.credit: Scientific Python Lectures (Gael Varoquaux and contributors)

learning-rate.alt: Gradient descent on a parabola with a learning rate that is too large: successive steps overshoot the minimum and zig-zag across it with growing amplitude, diverging.
learning-rate.caption: The learning rate is the step size. Set it too large and each step overshoots the bottom of the valley; the iterates bounce across the minimum with growing amplitude and diverge instead of converging, which is why the learning rate is the single most important hyperparameter to tune.
learning-rate.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

learning-rate-schedule.alt: Plot of learning rate versus epoch following a cosine curve, starting high near 0.3 and decaying smoothly toward zero by the end of training.
learning-rate-schedule.caption: A learning-rate schedule changes the step size over training rather than holding it fixed. This cosine-annealed schedule starts large for fast early progress, then decays smoothly toward zero so the optimizer can settle precisely into a minimum at the end.
learning-rate-schedule.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

learning-rate-warmup.alt: Plot of learning rate versus epoch that ramps linearly up from zero to a peak over the first few epochs, then decays along a cosine curve toward zero.
learning-rate-warmup.caption: Warmup begins with a near-zero learning rate and ramps it up over the first epochs before the main decay takes over. The gentle start keeps the volatile first steps small and lets an adaptive optimizer's running statistics accumulate, stabilizing the most fragile phase of training.
learning-rate-warmup.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

lemmatization.alt: The inflected forms ran, running, and runs funnel down to the single base form run; titled Lemmatization.
lemmatization.caption: Lemmatization reduces a word to its dictionary base form, its lemma, so that ran, running, and runs all map to run, using actual knowledge of word forms rather than blind suffix-chopping. It is the more careful cousin of stemming, slower but accurate, and it keeps distinct words distinct where a crude stemmer would wrongly merge them.

lie-group.alt: A unit circle in the plane with the point 1 marked on the right at the origin's level, and three points z, w, and zw on the upper arc.
lie-group.caption: The circle is the simplest Lie group: a group that is also a smooth manifold. Its points are unit-length complex numbers, the group operation multiplies them (which adds their angles, so z times w lands at zw), the identity is the point 1, and every element has an inverse. Because the points vary smoothly, you can do calculus on the symmetry itself, which is what makes continuous symmetry tractable.

linear-attention.alt: Keys and values are compressed into a running summary state that each query reads, instead of comparing against every position; labeled keys, values, summary, and query.
linear-attention.caption: Linear attention rewrites the attention computation so its cost grows linearly with sequence length instead of quadratically, by maintaining a running summary of past keys and values that each query reads from. It trades some of full attention's expressiveness for the ability to handle very long sequences, and it reveals attention's hidden kinship with recurrent networks.

linear-regression.alt: Scatter plot of blue data points with a red straight line of best fit drawn through them.
linear-regression.caption: Linear regression fits a straight line through the data by choosing the slope and intercept that minimize the squared vertical gaps between the line and the points. The result is the line that sits as close as possible to all the data, the simplest model of how the output changes with the input.

linear-representation-hypothesis.alt: A concept is found as a single direction in a network's activation space, recoverable by a linear probe; labeled neural network, activation space, linear probe, and concept.
linear-representation-hypothesis.caption: The linear representation hypothesis holds that neural networks encode many human-meaningful concepts as linear directions in their activation space, so a concept can be read out, or even added and subtracted, with simple vector operations. If broadly true, it is enormous news for interpretability: it means a model's inner language is far simpler than its tangled weights suggest.

linear-transformation.alt: Two side-by-side grids. On the left, the standard basis vectors (1, 0) and (0, 1) sit on a square grid. After the matrix A is applied, on the right, the same grid is sheared into a slanted lattice and the basis vectors have become (2, 3) and (1, -1).
linear-transformation.caption: A linear transformation is fully described by where it sends the basis vectors, and it carries the whole evenly spaced grid to another evenly spaced grid of parallel lines. Here the matrix A maps (1, 0) and (0, 1) to (2, 3) and (1, -1), which is why each layer of a network is a matrix acting on space followed by a nonlinearity.
linear-transformation.credit: Dive into Deep Learning (Aston Zhang, Zachary C. Lipton, Mu Li, Alexander J. Smola)

lion-optimizer.alt: A lion beside a plot where a momentum curve produces an update step; labeled step and momentum.
lion-optimizer.caption: Lion is an optimizer discovered by automated search that updates each weight using only the sign of a momentum term, a uniform-magnitude step in one direction or the other. Tracking just one momentum vector instead of Adam's two makes it more memory-efficient, an appealing property at the scale of large models where optimizer state is a real cost.

llm-as-judge.alt: A scatter plot with win rate difference between two models on the x-axis (0 to 1) and the agreement between a GPT-4 judge and human judges on the y-axis (about 0.65 to 1.0). The points rise from left to right: when two models are close in quality the judge and humans agree less often, and as the quality gap widens agreement climbs toward 1.0.
llm-as-judge.caption: LLM-as-judge uses a strong model to grade other models' open-ended outputs as a scalable stand-in for human raters. The plot validates the idea: agreement between a GPT-4 judge and humans rises as the gap between the two compared models grows, reaching the same roughly 80 percent-plus agreement that humans reach with each other, so the judge is most reliable exactly when the quality difference is clear.
llm-as-judge.credit: Lianmin Zheng, Wei-Lin Chiang, Ying Sheng, Siyuan Zhuang, Zhanghao Wu, Yonghao Zhuang, Zi Lin, Zhuohan Li, Dacheng Li, Eric P. Xing, Hao Zhang, Joseph E. Gonzalez, Ion Stoica

lm-evaluation-harness.alt: A language model is clamped by a harness to a benchmark report; titled Evaluation Harness.
lm-evaluation-harness.caption: The lm-evaluation-harness is a standard framework that runs a single language model against dozens of benchmarks through one uniform interface, so scores are computed the same way for everyone. Its quiet importance is reproducibility: a reported number means little until the exact prompts, scoring, and few-shot setup are pinned down, which a shared harness does.

local-attractor-basin.alt: Diagram of a region A inside a larger space, with trajectories starting from several different points all flowing inward and converging into region A.
local-attractor-basin.caption: A basin of attraction is the catchment area of a minimum: every starting point inside it flows downhill to the same attractor (region A). Which solution training reaches is decided by which basin the initial parameters fall into, and wide, flat basins tend to generalize better than narrow ones.

local-inference.alt: An arrow moves a model from a cloud API down to run directly on a laptop, a phone, and a personal server; labeled API.
local-inference.caption: Local inference runs a model on your own hardware instead of calling a hosted API, keeping data on the device and removing per-token fees and network round-trips. It is what open-weight models and aggressive quantization unlocked: capable models small enough to live on a laptop or phone rather than only in a data center.

local-minimum.alt: A one-dimensional curve of f(x) with two dips: a shallower one near x = -0.3 labeled local minimum and a deeper one near x = 1.1 labeled global minimum, each marked with an arrow.
local-minimum.caption: A local minimum is lower than its immediate surroundings but not necessarily the lowest point overall. Here the global minimum is the deepest valley, while the local minimum is a shallower basin; a descent-based optimizer that only moves downhill can settle into the local minimum and stop, because every nearby step would raise the loss.
local-minimum.credit: Aston Zhang, Zachary C. Lipton, Mu Li, Alexander J. Smola

logistic-regression.alt: Two panels with an arrow between them: on the left a straight line for the linear function z equals 2x plus 5, on the right the same input passed through the sigmoid y-prime equals 1 over (1 plus e to the minus z), bending the line into an S-shaped curve bounded between 0 and 1, with three matched points (a square, a dot, and a star) shown in both panels.
logistic-regression.caption: Logistic regression computes a linear score and then squashes it through the sigmoid into a probability between 0 and 1. The left panel is the raw linear output and the right is the same values after the sigmoid; the matched points show how a wide range of scores is mapped onto the bounded probability scale.
logistic-regression.credit: Google for Developers, Machine Learning Crash Course

logit-bias.alt: A bias is added to a token's logit, raising its bar in the next-token distribution; labeled logit and logit bias.
logit-bias.caption: Logit bias is a knob that adds a fixed offset to the raw scores of specific tokens before sampling, nudging the model toward or away from them by hand. It is a blunt but direct lever, useful for banning a word outright or forcing a constrained format, applied after all the learning is done.

logit-lens.alt: A layer-by-layer table for GPT-2 Small on the prompt The Eiffel Tower is located in the city of blank. Each row is a layer (0 to 11) with its top-1 prediction and probability. Early layers predict generic words like the, of, and France, then from layer 6 onward the prediction settles on Paris with the probability rising from 12 percent to 93 percent, the rows shaded darker as confidence grows.
logit-lens.caption: The logit lens decodes a transformer's hidden state at each layer through the model's own output head, reading off the next-token prediction as it forms. Here the answer crystallizes with depth: early layers emit generic tokens, then the correct city Paris emerges around the middle layers and sharpens toward the top, showing the prediction take shape as information flows upward.

logits.alt: A network takes an input image and emits three raw scores for the classes cat, dog, and horse: 35.4, 38.1, and minus 5.0. These pass through a softmax box and come out as probabilities 0.06, 0.94, and 0.00.
logits.caption: Logits are the raw, unnormalized scores a model assigns to each option, here 35.4, 38.1, and minus 5.0 for three classes. They carry no direct meaning as probabilities until a softmax exponentiates and normalizes them into a distribution that sums to one (0.06, 0.94, 0.00). Every sampling control, temperature, top-p, and a repetition penalty, edits these logits before the softmax runs.

long-context.alt: A long document overflows a context window while balloons of cost, quadratic attention, KV cache, and training data tug at it; labeled long context and window.
long-context.caption: Long context is the ability to take in very long inputs, whole books or codebases, in a single pass. The window keeps growing, but three costs resist it: attention that scales with the square of length, a key-value cache that grows with every token, and the scarcity of genuinely long training examples that teach a model to use the whole span.

long-horizon-reasoning.alt: A figure sets out from start along a long, winding, error-prone path, past a wrong turn, toward a distant goal flag; titled Long-Horizon Reasoning.
long-horizon-reasoning.caption: Long-horizon reasoning is the ability to carry a chain of dependent steps over a long arc toward a distant goal, where one early mistake can derail everything that follows. It is where current models are weakest: each step may be fine, but small per-step error rates compound over a long plan into near-certain failure.

lora.alt: Three side-by-side adapter diagrams; the leftmost, labeled LoRA, is the relevant one. An input h enters a frozen pretrained weight matrix W0 (gray) and, in parallel, a trainable low-rank path of two green matrices, a down-projection A of shape r by q and an up-projection B of shape p by r, whose matrix product forms the weight update. The two outputs are summed to produce h prime. The right two panels show the LoHa and LoKr variants for comparison.
lora.caption: LoRA (leftmost panel) freezes the pretrained weight matrix W0 and learns the update as the product of two small low-rank matrices, a down-projection A and an up-projection B of inner rank r, added back alongside the frozen path. Only A and B are trained, a tiny fraction of the parameters, and at inference the product can be merged into W0 for no added latency.

loss-function.alt: Plot of several classification loss functions (zero-one, hinge, log, squared hinge, modified Huber, perceptron) as a function of the decision-function output, all decreasing as the prediction becomes more confidently correct.
loss-function.caption: A loss function maps a prediction to a single penalty that training drives down. Plotting common classification losses against the model's output shows how each penalizes a wrong or unconfident prediction differently, so the choice of loss defines what the model is actually rewarded for learning.

loss-landscape.alt: A rendered 3D surface of a ResNet-56 network's loss landscape without skip connections, a rugged terrain of sharp peaks, cliffs, and narrow valleys.
loss-landscape.caption: The loss landscape is the surface formed by a network's loss over its parameters, and its geometry decides how hard optimization is. This is the actual landscape of a ResNet-56 trained without skip connections: a chaotic, non-convex terrain of cliffs and ravines that is treacherous to descend, which is why architectural choices that smooth the surface make training so much easier.
loss-landscape.credit: Hao Li, Zheng Xu, Gavin Taylor, Christoph Studer, Tom Goldstein

loss-of-plasticity.alt: A plot of percent correct on the test set against task number over thousands of sequential tasks on Continual ImageNet; the red Backpropagation curve rises then collapses sharply downward below 82 percent, while Continual Backpropagation, L2-Regularization and Shrink and Perturb stay near 89 to 90 percent.
loss-of-plasticity.caption: Under sustained continual training, a standard backpropagation network (red) loses its ability to keep learning and its accuracy collapses, even though its capacity is unchanged; methods that inject fresh variability or regularize, such as continual backpropagation, retain plasticity and stay high.

lottery-ticket-hypothesis.alt: Test accuracy curves over training iterations for a full network and for sparse subnetworks keeping 64, 16.78, 4.40 and 1.15 percent of weights, where the pruned subnetworks reset to their original initialization match the full network, while the control subnetworks with the same structure but random reinitialization train to lower accuracy, the gap widening as sparsity increases.
lottery-ticket-hypothesis.caption: A winning ticket is a sparse subnetwork that, kept at its original initialization, trains to the full network's accuracy (colored lines tracking the base), while the same structure reinitialized at random (the control lines) does notably worse, showing that the lucky starting weights, not just the connectivity, are what matters.

low-rank-approximation.alt: Four panels of the same red sports car: the original photo, then rank-1, rank-10, and rank-100 low-rank reconstructions, each sharper than the last.
low-rank-approximation.caption: An image is just a matrix, so a low-rank approximation rebuilds it from a handful of singular components. At rank 1 only broad color bands survive; by rank 100 the car is nearly indistinguishable from the original, evidence that most of the matrix's information lives in far fewer dimensions than its size suggests.

lstm.alt: Diagram of one LSTM cell. The memory cell internal state C runs straight across the top, modified only by an elementwise multiply with the forget gate and an elementwise add of new content. The input X_t and previous hidden state feed three sigmoid gates (forget, input, output) and a tanh input node. The output gate multiplies a tanh of the cell state to produce the hidden state H_t. A legend marks the sigmoid layers, the tanh layer, and the elementwise operators.
lstm.caption: An LSTM augments the recurrent hidden state with a protected cell state that runs along the top with only minor linear interactions. Three sigmoid gates regulate it: the forget gate decides what to keep, the input gate decides what new candidate content to write, and the output gate decides what to expose. That nearly uninterrupted cell-state path is what lets gradients survive across many time steps.
lstm.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

machine-learning.alt: A grid of small scatter plots: three rows are three datasets of red and blue points, and the columns are different classifiers, each shading the plane into colored decision regions that separate the two classes.
machine-learning.caption: Machine learning fits a model to labeled examples rather than following hand-coded rules. Here the same red and blue training points (rows are three datasets) are fit by ten different classifiers (columns); each learns a decision boundary, the colored regions, that separates the classes and is meant to generalize to new points.

machine-translation.alt: A grid aligning an English sentence on the rows ('And the programme has been implemented') with a French sentence on the columns ('Le programme a été mis en application'). A filled dot in a cell marks that the row word and column word correspond; the English word 'implemented' has three dots, aligning to 'mis', 'en' and 'application'.
machine-translation.caption: Translation is not word-for-word substitution, as this alignment between an English sentence and its French version shows. Each dot links words that correspond, and the single English word 'implemented' maps to three French words, 'mis en application'. Capturing such one-to-many and reordered alignments is exactly what the attention mechanism gave neural translation.

mamba.alt: Diagram of a selective state space model: a hidden state h flows from left (h at t minus 1) to right (h at t) through the state matrix A; the current input x at t is passed through a Project block (the selection mechanism) to produce the input-dependent matrices B, C, and the step size delta, which are discretized and combined to update the state and emit output y, with a side note showing fast GPU SRAM versus slower GPU HBM memory.
mamba.caption: Mamba makes the state space recurrence selective: the input x is projected to produce the matrices B and C and the step size delta as functions of the current token, so the model decides token by token what to write into its hidden state and what to discard. Because this input-dependent recurrence can no longer be run as a fixed convolution, Mamba uses a hardware-aware scan that keeps the large state in fast GPU SRAM rather than slower HBM.

manifold.alt: A sphere with a small patch on top mapped upward by projection lines onto a flat disk, representing a coordinate chart from a region of the sphere to flat space.
manifold.caption: The defining property of a manifold is that every small region maps smoothly onto a patch of flat space. Here a neighborhood of the sphere is carried by a coordinate chart onto a flat disk, so the curved surface looks ordinary and Euclidean when you zoom in on any one piece.

manifold-hypothesis.alt: A 3D scatter plot of 1500 points forming a rolled sheet, the Swiss roll, colored by a continuous gradient that tracks position along the roll.
manifold-hypothesis.caption: The Swiss roll: data living in 3D ambient space actually lies on a 2D rolled surface, the intuition behind the manifold hypothesis that high-dimensional data concentrates on a low-dimensional manifold. The smooth color gradient is the single intrinsic coordinate along the sheet.

manifold-optimization.alt: A sphere with a flat plane touching it at a single point, marked by a dot, representing the tangent plane to the surface at that point.
manifold-optimization.caption: Manifold optimization moves through the tangent space rather than off the surface. At each point of a curved constraint set (here a sphere) the tangent plane holds the directions a step is actually allowed to take; the Riemannian gradient is the ordinary gradient projected onto this plane, and the optimizer then steps along the manifold so every iterate stays on the valid set instead of being projected back after the fact.

market-dynamics.alt: Investment flows into AI compute, which yields returns that cycle back into more investment; labeled investment, return, and market dynamics.
market-dynamics.caption: AI market dynamics are dominated by a reinforcing loop: capital buys compute, compute produces more capable models, and capability draws revenue and more capital. Whether that flywheel is a durable engine or an overextended bubble is the central financial question hanging over the field.

markov-chain.alt: State diagram of a two-state Markov chain with states E and A; arrows show E to E at 0.3, E to A at 0.7, A to E at 0.4, and A to A at 0.6.
markov-chain.caption: A Markov chain moves between states with the next state depending only on the present one. Each state's outgoing transition probabilities sum to one (0.3 plus 0.7 from E, 0.4 plus 0.6 from A), the rows of its transition matrix.

markov-chain-monte-carlo.alt: Six small plots arranged in a grid, labeled 100, 500, 1000, 5000, 10000, and 50000 samples. Each overlays a blue target density (a bimodal curve with a tall left peak and a smaller right bump) with an orange curve estimated from the samples. The orange curve is jagged and off at 100 samples and grows steadily closer to the blue curve until the two are nearly identical at 50000 samples.
markov-chain-monte-carlo.caption: Markov chain Monte Carlo builds a random walk whose long-run equilibrium is the target distribution. With few samples the estimate (orange) is rough and misses the target (blue), but as the chain runs longer the collected samples reproduce the target ever more closely. This convergence in the limit is what lets MCMC approximate posteriors that have no closed form.

markov-decision-process.alt: A directed graph with three green state nodes (S0, S1, S2) and orange action nodes (a0, a1) branching from each state. Edges from action nodes carry transition probabilities such as 0.7 and 0.3, and two yellow squiggly arrows mark rewards of plus 5 and minus 1.
markov-decision-process.caption: A Markov decision process is defined by states (green), the actions available in each state (orange), the probabilities of where each action leads (the numbers on the edges, such as 0.7 and 0.3), and the rewards it pays (the yellow plus 5 and minus 1 arrows). The next state depends only on the current state and action, which is the Markov property that makes the problem solvable.

mask-r-cnn.alt: An image passes through a network that outputs both a bounding box and a per-pixel mask around an object; labeled box and mask; titled Mask R-CNN.
mask-r-cnn.caption: Mask R-CNN extends object detection from drawing a box around each object to outlining it pixel by pixel, adding a parallel mask-prediction branch to a detector. It became the standard instance-segmentation model by showing that detection and segmentation could share almost all their computation, getting the finer answer nearly for free.

masked-autoencoder.alt: Masked autoencoder pipeline: an input image with most patches hidden, a heavy encoder that processes only the visible patches, and a lightweight decoder that fills in placeholder tokens to reconstruct the full target image.
masked-autoencoder.caption: A masked autoencoder learns by filling in the blanks. Most patches are hidden, a heavy encoder processes only the small visible set, and a lightweight decoder reconstructs the missing pixels. Because the target is the image's own content, no labels are needed, and the high masking ratio forces genuine understanding rather than copying nearby pixels.
masked-autoencoder.credit: Kaiming He, Xinlei Chen, Saining Xie, Yanghao Li, Piotr Dollar, Ross Girshick

masked-language-modeling.alt: The sentence My [MASK] is Sylvain with one token replaced by a [MASK] placeholder. An arrow points down from the masked position to the predicted word name, showing the model recovering the hidden token from the surrounding words on both sides.
masked-language-modeling.caption: Masked language modeling hides a fraction of the tokens in a text and trains the model to predict each hidden token from the words on both sides of it. Because the model sees context to the left and the right, it learns deeply bidirectional representations.

mathematical-reasoning.alt: A staircase of steps rises through a head toward a verified answer, beside algebra and a geometry figure; titled Mathematical Reasoning.
mathematical-reasoning.caption: Mathematical reasoning is the multi-step, rule-bound deduction that math demands, and a revealing test for models because the answer is unambiguously right or wrong. It exposed the value of letting a model show its work: writing out intermediate steps, rather than leaping to an answer, sharply improves how often it lands the correct one.

matrix.alt: An m-by-n matrix drawn in large brackets with entries a sub i,j. A blue arrow down the left marks the m rows where the index i changes, and a red arrow across the top marks the n columns where the index j changes.
matrix.caption: A matrix is a rectangular grid of numbers with m rows and n columns, each entry addressed by its row index i and column index j. This two-index layout is what lets one matrix hold a whole table of data, and, read as a function, apply a linear transformation to every vector at once.

matrix-multiplication.alt: Matrix A on the left and matrix B above feed into the product matrix at lower right. A highlighted row of A and a highlighted column of B both point with colored arrows to a single highlighted entry of the result.
matrix-multiplication.caption: Each entry of the product is the dot product of one row of the left matrix with one column of the right matrix, as the colored arrows show. The shared inner dimension must match for these row-by-column dot products to line up, which is the single rule that governs whether two matrices can be multiplied.

matryoshka-embeddings.alt: A diagram of Matryoshka Representation Learning. A single embedding vector z in R^d is shown as nested colored prefixes, and a separate loss is computed on each prefix (the first d/16, d/8, d/4, d/2, and the full d dimensions), then summed into one combined loss. On the left, the resulting vector feeds adaptive retrieval (shortlisting then re-ranking) and adaptive classification.
matryoshka-embeddings.caption: Matryoshka representation learning trains one embedding so that nested prefixes of it are each usable on their own. The training objective applies a loss not just to the full vector but simultaneously to a series of shorter prefixes (here d/16, d/8, d/4, d/2, and d), which forces the most important information into the earliest coordinates. The vector can then be truncated to any trained length, enabling coarse-to-fine retrieval: a short prefix shortlists candidates cheaply, then the full vector re-ranks them.
matryoshka-embeddings.credit: Aditya Kusupati and the Matryoshka Representation Learning authors

maximal-marginal-relevance.alt: From retrieved documents, a selector balances a relevance dial against redundancy to pick a diverse subset, with one distinct document highlighted; titled Maximal Marginal Relevance.
maximal-marginal-relevance.caption: Maximal marginal relevance picks results that are relevant to the query while penalizing similarity to what has already been chosen, so a list does not fill up with near-duplicates of the single best hit. It is the standard fix for redundancy in retrieval, trading a little pure relevance for coverage of more of the query's facets.

maximum-entropy-inference.alt: Among distributions consistent with the data, the broadest, highest-entropy one is selected; labeled entropy and maximum.
maximum-entropy-inference.caption: Maximum-entropy inference picks, among all distributions consistent with what you actually know, the one with the most entropy, the flattest and least presumptuous. The principle is a discipline against smuggling in unwarranted assumptions: commit only to what the constraints force, and stay maximally uncertain about everything else.

maximum-likelihood-estimation.alt: A likelihood curve plotting the likelihood of observed data on the vertical axis against a parameter theta from 0 to 1 on the horizontal axis, the curve peaking near theta equals 0.69.
maximum-likelihood-estimation.caption: Maximum likelihood estimation picks the parameter value that makes the observed data most probable. For a coin that came up heads 9 of 13 times, the likelihood as a function of the bias theta peaks at the estimate near 9 over 13.
