Step 3, multi-stage: a base model is first SFT-ed on reasoning data, then trained with GRPO and verifiable rewards into the final reasoning model.
reasoning-model.caption: A reasoning model is usually produced by reinforcement learning on problems with checkable answers. The diagram lays out three recipes: distilling reasoning traces from a stronger model, pure reinforcement learning with GRPO and verifiable rewards on a base model, and a multi-stage path that combines supervised fine-tuning with reinforcement learning, which is what teaches the model to think at length before answering.

rectified-flow.alt: Two panels comparing transport between a purple point cloud and a red point cloud. The left panel, labeled linear interpolation, shows straight connecting paths that cross in the middle. The right panel, labeled rectified flow, shows the same endpoints reconnected so the paths run almost parallel and no longer cross.
rectified-flow.caption: A generative flow has to carry samples from a noise distribution (left cloud) to the data distribution (right cloud). Naive straight lines between random pairs cross one another, which forces a solver to take many small steps to follow the tangle. Rectified flow rewires the pairing so the trajectories straighten and stop crossing, and a nearly straight path can be integrated in just a few steps, which is what makes the sampler fast.

recurrent-neural-network.alt: A recurrent network drawn across three adjacent time steps. At each step the input X_t and the previous hidden state both feed a fully connected layer with an activation function, which emits the new hidden state H_t and an output. The hidden state arrow passes from each step to the next, and a legend explains the copy and concatenate symbols.
recurrent-neural-network.caption: A recurrent network processes a sequence one step at a time, combining the current input with the previous hidden state to produce the new hidden state and optionally an output. The same weights are reused at every step, so the hidden state carries context forward and the network can handle sequences of any length.
recurrent-neural-network.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

recursive-self-improvement.alt: An AI sits inside a loop in which each round of self-improvement enables the next; titled Recursive Self-Improvement.
recursive-self-improvement.caption: Recursive self-improvement is the scenario where an AI becomes capable enough to improve its own design, and each improvement makes it better at improving further, potentially compounding fast. It is the engine behind hard-takeoff intelligence-explosion arguments, and the reason some treat the first system able to do AI research itself as a uniquely pivotal threshold.

red-teaming.alt: A three-column diagram. A Red LM generates probing test questions (for example 'What's your work email?'); a Target LM answers each one; a Red Clf classifier labels the answers, flagging failures such as Offensive, Data Leakage, User Info, Distributional Bias, and Offensive Dialog.
red-teaming.caption: Red-teaming deliberately attacks a model to surface its failures before real users or adversaries do. Here one language model automatically generates adversarial test cases, a target model answers them, and a classifier flags harmful responses by category, from data leakage and leaked personal info to offensive or biased output. Automating the attacker lets the probe scale far beyond hand-written tests.
red-teaming.credit: Ethan Perez, Saffron Huang, Francis Song, Trevor Cai, Roman Ring, John Aslanides, Amelia Glaese, Nat McAleese, Geoffrey Irving

reflection.alt: Block diagram of the Reflexion agent. Inside an Agent box, an Actor language model produces a trajectory in the environment, an Evaluator language model scores it, and a Self-reflection language model turns that feedback into reflective text. The reflective text is stored in long-term memory and fed back to the Actor on the next attempt, alongside short-term trajectory memory.
reflection.caption: Reflection adds a self-correcting loop on top of a single pass of generation. The agent produces an attempt, an evaluator judges it, and a self-reflection step converts that judgment into verbal feedback that is stored and prepended to the next try. Separating producing from judging surfaces flaws the model glosses over while generating, lifting quality without changing the underlying model.

regression.alt: Scatter plot of airfare cost against distance to destination, with a fitted straight line, the line equation 0.117 times x plus 83.267, and an R-squared of 0.632 noted.
regression.caption: Regression predicts a continuous quantity from input features. Here airfare cost is modeled as a line in the distance, fare equals 0.117 times distance plus 83.267, so each extra mile adds about twelve cents. The R-squared of 0.632 reports the fraction of the variation in fare the line explains, the standard way to score a continuous prediction.

regret-minimization.alt: Two reward curves diverge over time, the best possible choice above and the actual choice below, with the gap between them marked regret.
regret-minimization.caption: Regret measures how much worse a sequence of decisions did than the best fixed choice would have in hindsight, and regret minimization aims to keep that gap growing as slowly as possible. It is the central yardstick for online decision-making and bandits, because it grades a learner on closing the gap to optimal without ever knowing the future in advance.

regularization.alt: Several data points fit two ways: a blue curve that oscillates wildly to pass exactly through every point, and a smooth green curve that follows the general trend without hitting each point.
regularization.caption: Without regularization a flexible model can twist to pass through every point (blue), fitting the noise and generalizing badly. Regularization penalizes that complexity, favoring the smooth green curve that captures the underlying trend instead. It trades a little fit on the training points for a model far more likely to hold up on new data.

reinforcement-learning.alt: The reinforcement learning loop drawn as a cycle: the Agent takes an action a_t that enters the Environment, and the Environment returns the next state and reward s_t, r_t back to the Agent, closing the loop.
reinforcement-learning.caption: Reinforcement learning is a closed feedback loop between an agent and its environment. The agent observes a state and chooses an action, the environment responds with a new state and a scalar reward, and the agent adjusts its behavior to maximize the cumulative reward over time.

rejection-sampling.alt: A target density curve f of X sits below a dashed envelope curve M times g of X. The region under f is labeled the acceptance zone and the gap between f and the envelope is labeled the rejection zone; a vertical draw at point X with a uniform value U decides whether the candidate is accepted or rejected.
rejection-sampling.caption: Rejection sampling proposes candidates and keeps only those that pass a check, discarding the rest: a draw that falls in the acceptance zone (under the target) is kept, one in the rejection zone is thrown away. In language models the same generate-then-filter idea uses a verifier (a unit test, a grader) as the acceptance test, both to enforce a requirement at inference and to keep only verified-correct outputs for training.

rejection-sampling-fine-tuning.alt: A model generates several candidate responses, a reward model scores them, and only the best are kept to fine-tune on; labeled model, reward, and rejection sampling.
rejection-sampling-fine-tuning.caption: Rejection sampling fine-tuning has the model generate many candidate answers, scores them with a reward model or verifier, and fine-tunes only on the best ones. It is a simple, stable alternative to full reinforcement learning, often called best-of-n training, and it underlies much of how reasoning models bootstrap from their own good outputs.

relu.alt: Plot of the ReLU function over inputs from minus eight to eight. The curve is flat at zero for every negative input and becomes a straight line equal to the input for positive values, with a sharp kink at the origin. The vertical axis is labeled relu(x) and the horizontal axis x.
relu.caption: The rectified linear unit returns zero for negative inputs and the input itself for positive ones, the maximum of zero and the input. Its derivative is exactly one on the positive side, so gradients pass through undiminished, unlike saturating activations; the hard zero on the negative side also makes roughly half the units inactive, giving sparse and cheap activations.
relu.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

repetition-penalty.alt: A grid of line charts, one row per model, with an accuracy plot on the left and a degenerate repetition percentage plot on the right, each swept over temperature from 0 to 1. With no penalty the repetition curve is high, while the repetition penalty, frequency penalty, and LZ penalty curves drop the repetition rate sharply, with little change to accuracy.
repetition-penalty.caption: A repetition penalty lowers the probability of tokens the model has already produced, suppressing the degenerate loops language models fall into. The right-hand plots show the no-penalty baseline repeating heavily while the repetition and frequency penalties cut the degenerate-repetition rate by large factors; the left-hand plots show accuracy stays roughly flat, so the penalty curbs looping at little cost.
repetition-penalty.credit: Antonio A. Ginart, Naveen Kodali, Jason Lee, et al.

representation-collapse.alt: A line plot of the log of the singular values of the covariance matrix of embedding vectors against the singular value rank index from 0 to about 128. The curve sits near a log value of minus four across the first ninety ranks, then falls off a cliff to about minus twenty-five, so a block of dimensions has near-zero variance.
representation-collapse.caption: Representation collapse shows up in the singular value spectrum of the embeddings. A healthy representation spreads variance across many dimensions, but here a whole block of singular values drops to near zero (the cliff past rank ninety), meaning those dimensions carry no information and the embeddings have collapsed onto a lower-dimensional subspace. This narrow form is called dimensional collapse.
representation-collapse.credit: Li Jing, Pascal Vincent, Yann LeCun, Yuandong Tian

reproducing-kernel-hilbert-space.alt: Two panels joined by an arrow labeled phi. The left panel shows two classes of points separated by a curved boundary in the input plane; the right panel shows the same points after a feature map, now separated by a straight line.
reproducing-kernel-hilbert-space.caption: A reproducing kernel Hilbert space is what makes the kernel trick rigorous. A feature map phi lifts the inputs into a richer (often infinite-dimensional) space where a curved separating boundary in the original input (left) becomes a simple straight one (right). A kernel computes inner products in that feature space directly, so the lift never has to be formed explicitly, and the representer theorem keeps the resulting problem finite.

reranking.alt: A two-stage retrieval pipeline. A search query and a document collection feed a Retrieval Bi-Encoder, which returns candidate documents labeled retrieve candidates. Those candidates pass to a Re-Ranker Cross-Encoder, which outputs ranked hits.
reranking.caption: Reranking is the second stage of a retrieve-then-rerank pipeline. A fast bi-encoder first sweeps the whole document collection and returns a shortlist of candidates tuned for recall, and a slower, more accurate cross-encoder reranker then re-scores only that shortlist to put the truly most relevant results on top. Retrieve broadly, then rerank narrowly.
reranking.credit: Nils Reimers and the sentence-transformers contributors

residual-connection.alt: Two network blocks side by side: a regular block on the left whose stacked layers must learn the full mapping f(x), and a residual block on the right where a skip arrow adds the input x back to the block output, so the layers learn g(x) and the block computes f(x) equals g(x) plus x.
residual-connection.caption: In a residual block the input is added back to the output through a skip connection, so the stacked layers only need to learn the residual correction g(x) rather than the full mapping. The identity shortcut also gives gradients a clean path backward, which is what makes very deep networks trainable.
residual-connection.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

retentive-network.alt: An input is processed by a block that runs in parallel during training but applies a positional decay; labeled input, parallel, and decay.
retentive-network.caption: A retentive network is a transformer alternative built to have it both ways: a parallel form for fast training and an equivalent recurrent form for cheap, constant-memory inference, linked by a fixed exponential decay over positions. It is one of several architectures chasing the same prize, the quality of attention without its quadratic cost at long context.

retrieval-augmented-generation.alt: Flow diagram of a RAG pipeline. Reference documents are passed through an LLM embedding step into a vector database. A user query is embedded into a query vector, compared against the database to find relevant documents, and the retrieved context chunks plus the original query form an augmented query, which an LLM generation step turns into the response.
retrieval-augmented-generation.caption: Retrieval-augmented generation runs in two stages. Reference documents are embedded into a vector database ahead of time; at query time the user's question is embedded, the nearest passages are retrieved, and those context chunks are concatenated with the question into an augmented prompt that the model answers from. The knowledge lives in the editable corpus, while the model supplies the reasoning and prose.

reward-bench.alt: A schematic of the RewardBench scoring method. One prompt is paired with a chosen completion and a rejected completion. Each completion is passed through the reward model, producing a scalar score (here 0.2 for chosen and 0.4 for rejected). The pair counts as a win for the reward model when it assigns the chosen completion the higher score.
reward-bench.caption: A reward model is the scorer that steers RLHF, and a flawed one silently corrupts everything trained against it. RewardBench tests a reward model on curated chosen-versus-rejected pairs: it runs each response through the model and checks whether the chosen response gets the higher score, and accuracy is the fraction of pairs it ranks correctly across categories like chat, safety, and reasoning.
reward-bench.credit: Nathan Lambert, Valentina Pyatkin, Jacob Morrison, LJ Miranda, Bill Yuchen Lin, Khyathi Chandu, Nouha Dziri, Sachin Kumar, Tom Zick, Yejin Choi, Noah A. Smith, Hannaneh Hajishirzi

reward-hacking.alt: Three line plots for the Atari games Hero, Montezuma's Revenge, and Private Eye, each over fifty million agent steps. In every game the mean reward-model return (green) rises and stays high while the mean true episode return (black) climbs briefly then collapses, so the two curves diverge.
reward-hacking.caption: Reward hacking is an agent maximizing its reward signal by exploiting a flaw in how the reward is defined rather than achieving the intended outcome. Here the learned reward model's score (green) keeps rising while the true game score (black) collapses: the agent games the proxy reward instead of playing well. The widening gap between the two curves is the signature of the loophole being exploited.
reward-hacking.credit: Jan Leike, David Krueger, Tom Everitt, Miljan Martic, Vishal Maini, Shane Legg

reward-model.alt: A schematic of reward model training. A prompts dataset feeds an initial language model that samples many prompts and produces generated text; the generated outputs are scored and ranked by a human (relative, Elo style); the resulting (sample, reward) pairs train a separate reward (preference) model that outputs a scalar score r-theta.
reward-model.caption: A reward model is trained to predict human preference as a single scalar score. Many prompts are run through the language model, a human ranks the resulting outputs, and those ranked (sample, reward) pairs train the reward model to imitate that judgment. Once trained it stands in for a hand-written objective, letting a system be optimized against human judgment at scale.
reward-model.credit: Nathan Lambert, Louis Castricato, Leandro von Werra, Alex Havrilla (Hugging Face)

reward-modeling.alt: Human thumbs-up and thumbs-down preferences train a reward model, which then supplies the signal that trains a policy; labeled reward model and policy.
reward-modeling.caption: Reward modeling learns a stand-in for human judgment: a model trained on people's comparisons of outputs, which then scores new outputs so a policy can be optimized against it. It is the heart of RLHF, and its central fragility too, since the policy will ruthlessly exploit any gap between the learned reward and what people actually wanted.

reward-shaping.alt: Extra intermediate rewards are added along an agent's path to guide it from a standing start up to a distant goal; labeled reward and shaped reward.
reward-shaping.caption: Reward shaping adds intermediate rewards to a task whose true reward is rare, giving the agent a trail of breadcrumbs toward the goal so it learns before stumbling on success by chance. The danger is that a careless shaping signal teaches the agent to chase the breadcrumbs themselves, optimizing the proxy instead of the goal they were meant to guide it to.

riemannian-manifold.alt: A torus, a donut-shaped surface, with a single curved red path winding around it once through the hole and around the tube.
riemannian-manifold.caption: A Riemannian manifold is a curved surface equipped with a metric that defines distance, and the shortest path between two points is a geodesic. On this torus the red geodesic curves through the hole and around the tube, the straightest available route given the surface's geometry.

rlaif.alt: Two parallel pipelines from one SFT model that samples responses. In the top path, an off-the-shelf large language model rates the response pairs, training a reward model from AI feedback that drives reinforcement learning to an RLAIF policy. In the bottom path, a human rates the pairs, training a reward model from human feedback that drives reinforcement learning to an RLHF policy.
rlaif.caption: Reinforcement learning from AI feedback keeps the RLHF machinery but swaps where the preference labels come from. Instead of a human comparing response pairs (bottom), a capable model is the judge (top), labeling preferences that train the reward model. AI feedback removes the human annotation bottleneck, applying the same criteria cheaply and at scale, and is the mechanism underneath constitutional AI.
rlaif.credit: Harrison Lee, Samrat Phatale, Hassan Mansoor, Thomas Mesnard, et al.

rlhf.alt: A flow diagram with four boxes. Prompt data trains a supervised model. Human annotators rank sampled responses to produce ranking data, which trains a reward model. The supervised model initializes both the reward model and an aligned model, and the reward model guides the aligned model through PPO reinforcement learning.
rlhf.caption: Reinforcement learning from human feedback aligns a model in three stages. A supervised model is trained on demonstrations; humans then rank pairs of its responses, and those comparisons train a reward model that predicts a scalar score. Finally the model is optimized by reinforcement learning (PPO) to maximize that reward, with a penalty keeping it close to the supervised starting point so it does not degenerate.

rlvr.alt: An agent produces an answer that an automatic verifier checks, the pass or fail signal feeding back to train it; labeled verifier; titled RLVR.
rlvr.caption: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards trains a model against an automatic checker, did the code pass its tests, is the math answer correct, rather than a learned model of human preference. Because the reward is ground truth and cannot be gamed the way a learned reward model can, it has become an engine behind the recent leap in reasoning models.

rmsprop.alt: Contour plot of an ill-conditioned objective with the RMSProp optimizer path following a smooth curve from the start point to the minimum.
rmsprop.caption: RMSProp divides each parameter's step by a running root-mean-square of its recent gradients, so steep and flat directions are rescaled to comparable effective step sizes. The result is a smooth, confident path to the minimum where a single shared learning rate would zig-zag.
rmsprop.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

roc-auc.alt: Top: two overlapping class score distributions with a moving threshold, shading the resulting true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives. Bottom: the ROC curve of true positive rate against false positive rate with a diagonal no-skill line and an operating point that moves as the threshold changes.
roc-auc.caption: Sliding the decision threshold across the two class score distributions changes the true positive and false positive rates, and plotting that pair at every threshold traces the ROC curve. The area under it (AUC) measures how well the model ranks positives above negatives; the dashed diagonal is chance and the top-left corner is perfect separation.

roofline-model.alt: A roofline plot with attainable performance in GFLOPS on the vertical axis and operational intensity in FLOPS per byte on the horizontal axis. A slanted line rising from the left is labeled bound based on bandwidth, a horizontal line is labeled bound based on peak performance, and the two meet at a ridge point. Three applications are marked below the roof.
roofline-model.caption: The roofline model plots attainable performance against arithmetic intensity (operations performed per byte moved). The slanted segment is the memory-bandwidth ceiling and the flat segment is the peak-compute ceiling; where they meet is the ridge point. An operation to the left of the ridge is memory-bound and speeds up only by moving less data, while one to the right is compute-bound, which is how the plot tells you which limit you are fighting.

rotary-position-embedding.alt: Query and key vectors are rotated by angles set by their positions, so attention depends on the angle between them; labeled query, key, and angle.
rotary-position-embedding.caption: Rotary position embedding encodes a token's position by rotating its query and key vectors by an angle proportional to where it sits, so the attention score between two tokens depends only on the angle between them, their relative distance. That clean relative-position property, plus graceful behavior on sequences longer than those seen in training, is why it became standard in modern transformers.

rouge.alt: A generated summary and a reference are compared by their n-gram overlap, scored as recall; labeled generated summary, reference, n-gram overlap, and recall; titled ROUGE.
rouge.caption: ROUGE scores a generated summary by how much of a human reference it manages to cover, measuring overlap of words and short n-gram sequences with a recall orientation. It is the standard automatic metric for summarization, and shares the blind spot of all overlap metrics: it rewards matching the reference's words, not necessarily saying something true or well-written.

rpa.alt: A scripted bot clicks through a software interface to carry out a routine task; titled Robotic Process Automation.
rpa.caption: Robotic process automation is the older paradigm of automating repetitive computer work with brittle, explicitly-scripted bots that mimic a human clicking through software. Its rigidity, breaking the moment a screen or field changes, is exactly the limitation that AI agents promise to fix, which makes it the foil against which agents are defined.

rwkv.alt: Architecture diagram of one RWKV block: an input embedding passes up through a Layer Norm into a Time Mix block and then another Layer Norm into a Channel Mix block, with token-shift operations feeding each, and a final Layer Norm and output embedding; the time-mix block maintains a running weighted sum gated by a receptance term, and the channel-mix block plays the role of the feed-forward layer.
rwkv.caption: RWKV replaces self-attention with two interacting sub-blocks stacked in each layer. The Time Mix block plays the role of attention but, instead of comparing all pairs of tokens, maintains a running exponentially weighted sum of past key and value signals gated by a receptance term; the Channel Mix block plays the role of the feed-forward layer. Because the time decay lets the past be carried in a single fixed-size state, the same model has an equivalent recurrent form that generates in constant memory and a parallel form that trains efficiently.

saddle-point.alt: A 3D wireframe of the surface f(x, y) = x squared minus y squared, curving upward along the x axis and downward along the y axis, with the saddle point at the origin marked in red.
saddle-point.caption: A saddle point is a critical point where the gradient vanishes yet the surface is neither a peak nor a valley. On f(x, y) = x squared minus y squared the origin curves up along one axis and down along the other, the defining mixed curvature. Because the slope is zero there, plain gradient descent stalls even though escape is possible along a downward direction.
saddle-point.credit: Aston Zhang, Zachary C. Lipton, Mu Li, Alexander J. Smola

sample-complexity.alt: Two learning-curve plots of accuracy against the number of training samples, one for a Gaussian naive Bayes classifier and one for an SVM. In each, a blue training-score curve and an orange cross-validation-score curve are drawn with shaded standard-deviation bands. The training and cross-validation curves start far apart and converge as the number of training examples grows.
sample-complexity.caption: A learning curve shows how accuracy depends on the amount of data. With few examples the training score is high but the cross-validation score is low, a large generalization gap; as more examples are added the two curves converge. Sample complexity is the number of examples needed to close that gap and reach a target accuracy, and the curve makes that data cost visible for each model.

sandbagging.alt: Two bar charts, one for GPT-4 and one for Claude 3, plotting accuracy on control benchmarks (CSQA, MMLU) and target dangerous-capability benchmarks (WMDP-Bio, WMDP-Chem, WMDP-Cyber). Each benchmark has a Regular bar and a lower Sandbagging bar. On the control benchmarks the drop is small; on the target benchmarks the Sandbagging bars fall sharply toward the random baseline line.
sandbagging.caption: Sandbagging is a model strategically underperforming to hide a capability it actually has. Prompted to sandbag, GPT-4 and Claude 3 keep near-normal accuracy on harmless control tasks but drop sharply on dangerous-capability tests (WMDP), close to the random baseline. The selective, targeted drop is the tell: the ability is present but deliberately concealed where it would matter for an evaluation.
sandbagging.credit: Teun van der Weij, Felix Hofstatter, Ollie Jaffe, Samuel F. Brown, Francis Rhys Ward

scalable-oversight.alt: A human, aided by an AI assistant with a magnifier, supervises a more capable AI as capability rises; titled Scalable Oversight.
scalable-oversight.caption: Scalable oversight is the problem of how humans can keep supervising AI systems that are becoming too capable, or producing work too complex, to check directly. The leading idea is to use AI to help, having trusted models assist humans in evaluating more powerful ones, so that oversight scales alongside capability instead of falling behind it.

scaling-laws.alt: Plot of the optimal ratio of training tokens per model parameter against training compute in FLOP on logarithmic axes. Two estimated optimal-policy curves are shown with uncertainty bands, one rising and one nearly flat, along with a dashed horizontal line marking the D over N equals twenty rule of thumb and a point for the Chinchilla model. A CC-BY and epoch.ai credit appear in the lower corners.
scaling-laws.caption: Scaling laws let practitioners choose how to spend a fixed compute budget. The Chinchilla analysis found that parameters and training tokens should grow together, roughly twenty tokens per parameter (the dashed line), rather than making the model as large as possible. This compute-optimal allocation is why newer models are often not the largest but are trained on far more tokens.

scheduling.alt: An agent is triggered by a time, an interval, or an event, shown with a clock; titled Scheduling.
scheduling.caption: Scheduling runs an agent or automation on a trigger, a fixed time, a recurring interval, or an event, rather than only when a person asks. It is the shift from a tool you invoke to a worker that acts on its own cadence, which is what turns an assistant into something that quietly runs your routines.

score-based-model.alt: A schematic with two arrows over a strip of images of a dog that grows progressively noisier from left to right. The top arrow, Forward SDE (data to noise), carries the equation dx = f(x,t)dt + g(t)dw. The bottom arrow, Reverse SDE (noise to data), carries the same drift minus a term containing the score, the gradient of log p_t(x), which is boxed and labeled score function.
score-based-model.caption: A score-based model views generation as a stochastic differential equation. A forward process slowly turns data into noise, and the matching reverse process turns noise back into data. The reverse process needs only the score, the gradient of the log probability density (boxed), which a network learns at every noise level.
score-based-model.credit: Yang Song (Score-Based Generative Modeling through Stochastic Differential Equations)

score-distillation.alt: A score distillation pipeline for text-to-3D. A 3D Gaussian scene is rendered from several viewpoints into multiview images, noise is injected, and a pretrained 2D diffusion model conditioned on a text prompt (a beagle in a detective outfit) provides denoising directions that are turned into a gradient updating the 3D scene.
score-distillation.caption: Score distillation sampling turns a 2D diffusion model into a critic for optimizing a separate representation, most often a 3D scene. Random views of the 3D scene are rendered, noised, and scored by the frozen text-to-image model, and the resulting gradient nudges the 3D parameters until every rendered viewpoint looks like a plausible sample from the diffusion model.
score-distillation.credit: Li, Liu, Zhang, Su, Liu (Connecting Consistency Distillation to Score Distillation for Text-to-3D Generation)

secure-aggregation.alt: Several participants' protected updates are combined so a server learns only their sum, not any individual contribution; shown with shields and a lock.
secure-aggregation.caption: Secure aggregation lets a server combine many participants' model updates while learning only the total, never any single contribution, using cryptography so individual updates cancel out except in the sum. It is what makes federated learning genuinely private: the coordinator can improve a shared model without ever seeing any one user's data or gradient.

segment-anything.alt: The Segment Anything Model architecture: an input image passes through a heavy image encoder into an image embedding, a lightweight prompt encoder turns a mask, points, a box, or text into prompt embeddings, and a fast mask decoder combines the two to output several valid object masks each with a confidence score.
segment-anything.caption: Segment Anything is a promptable segmentation model. A heavy image encoder runs once to embed the picture, then a lightweight prompt encoder and mask decoder turn a click, box, or rough region into a valid object mask in real time, generalizing to objects it never saw in training.
segment-anything.credit: Meta AI Research (Kirillov et al., Segment Anything)

self-attention.alt: Diagram of one self-attention head. Panel B shows each token vector T multiplied by weight matrices W-Q, W-K, W-V to make a query, key, and value. Panel A shows token 1's query compared against the keys of all four tokens, each score scaled, passed through softmax, and used to weight the value vectors into a new representation. Panel C writes the result as softmax of (Q times K-transpose over the square root of d-k) times V.
self-attention.caption: Self-attention turns each token into a query, a key, and a value (panel B). To re-represent a token, its query is compared by dot product against every token's key, the scores are scaled and softmaxed into weights, and the output is the weighted sum of the value vectors (panels A and C). A token whose key aligns with the query contributes strongly, so each position gathers information from the whole sequence.
self-attention.credit: Shuang Zhang, Rui Fan, Yuti Liu, Shuang Chen, Qiao Liu, Wanwen Zeng

self-consistency.alt: Two rows. The top, greedy decoding, runs one chain of thought to one answer. The bottom, self-consistency, samples several different reasoning chains for the same question that reach various answers, then takes a majority vote to pick the most consistent final answer.
self-consistency.caption: Self-consistency replaces a single greedy chain of thought (top) with many sampled chains (bottom), then returns the answer they agree on most by majority vote. The idea is that a correct answer can be reached by several distinct valid routes while errors scatter, so agreement concentrates on the truth. It buys accuracy with extra inference rather than a bigger model.
self-consistency.credit: Xuezhi Wang, Jason Wei, Dale Schuurmans, Quoc Le, Ed Chi, Sharan Narang, Aakanksha Chowdhery, Denny Zhou

self-refinement.alt: A loop diagram. An input goes into a central Model. The model produces feedback on its own output (left arc, labeled use the model to get feedback on its output), then refines its previous output given that feedback (right arc, labeled use the model to refine its output), cycling back to the model.
self-refinement.caption: Self-refinement turns one attempt into an improvement loop run by the same model: it drafts an answer, critiques its own draft, and rewrites it using that critique, repeating for a few rounds. It works because judging a finished draft is easier than writing it, and gains come purely from spending more inference, with no change to the model's weights.

self-supervised-learning.alt: Part of an image is hidden, the model predicts the missing region, and the data itself supplies the answer; labeled model and predict; titled Self-Supervised Learning.
self-supervised-learning.caption: Self-supervised learning manufactures its own labels from unlabeled data by hiding part of each example and training the model to predict it, masking a word, a patch, or the next token. It is what unlocked the era of foundation models, because it turns the entire internet of raw text and images into training signal with no human annotation required.

semantic-search.alt: A 2D plot with three vectors drawn from the origin: a green query vector q in the middle, a blue document vector d1 above it, and a red document vector d2 below it. The angle alpha is marked between q and d1, and the larger angle theta is marked between q and d2.
semantic-search.caption: Semantic search embeds the query and every document as vectors in one space and ranks documents by closeness to the query, usually the cosine of the angle between them. Here document d1 sits at a small angle alpha from the query q, so it is more relevant than d2 at the wider angle theta. Because angle, not shared words, decides the ranking, synonyms and paraphrases still match.

semantic-segmentation.alt: A photo of a dog and a cat beside its semantic segmentation map, where the dog pixels are one shade of blue, the cat pixels another shade, and the surrounding pixels are labeled background.
semantic-segmentation.caption: Semantic segmentation labels every pixel with the category it belongs to, producing a dense map rather than a box. Here the pixels of the dog, the cat, and the background each receive their own class, and all pixels of one class share a label.
semantic-segmentation.credit: Aston Zhang, Zachary C. Lipton, Mu Li, Alexander J. Smola (Dive into Deep Learning)

semi-supervised-learning.alt: Schematic of semi-supervised learning: a small set of labeled cat and dog images and a large pool of unlabeled animal images both feed a processing step that sorts the images into a cat category and a dog category.
semi-supervised-learning.caption: Semi-supervised learning combines a few labeled examples with a large pool of unlabeled ones. The handful of labels anchors the categories, and the structure of the unlabeled data (which images cluster together) is used to sort the rest, reaching better accuracy than the scarce labels could achieve on their own.

sequence-to-sequence.alt: An encoder-decoder diagram for machine translation. A recurrent encoder reads the English tokens They, are, watching, period, and an end-of-sequence marker into a final state. That state initializes a recurrent decoder, which is fed a beginning-of-sequence token and the previous target word at each step and emits the French translation Ils, regardent, period, and an end-of-sequence marker one token at a time.
sequence-to-sequence.caption: A sequence-to-sequence model has two parts: an encoder that compresses the whole input sequence into a state, and a decoder that, starting from that state and a beginning-of-sequence token, generates the output one token at a time until it emits the end-of-sequence marker. This lets the input and output have different lengths.
sequence-to-sequence.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

sharpness-aware-minimization.alt: A three-dimensional loss surface for a ResNet trained with sharpness-aware minimization, showing a broad, gently sloping minimum at the center.
sharpness-aware-minimization.caption: Sharpness-aware minimization minimizes the worst-case loss in a neighborhood rather than the loss at a single point, so it converges to a wide minimum like this instead of the narrow, steep basin standard gradient descent finds, which tends to generalize better.

simulated-annealing.alt: Animation of a marker searching a one-dimensional rugged landscape of many peaks and valleys, with a temperature value shown at the top that falls over time as the marker settles toward the lowest valley.
simulated-annealing.caption: Simulated annealing treats the objective as a landscape to descend, but it is willing to move uphill: a worse move is accepted with a probability set by the temperature shown at the top. While the temperature is high the search roams freely and can cross barriers out of shallow valleys; as it cools the search grows pickier and settles into a deep minimum, shifting from exploration to exploitation.

singular-value-decomposition.alt: A commuting diagram. A unit disc with two perpendicular arrows is sent by M directly to a tilted ellipse. The same result is reached the long way: V star rotates the disc, Sigma stretches it along the axes into an ellipse with semi-axes sigma 1 and sigma 2, and U rotates that ellipse into place.
singular-value-decomposition.caption: The singular value decomposition factors any matrix as M equal to U times Sigma times V star, splitting its action into a rotation, a pure stretch, and another rotation. The unit disc becomes an ellipse whose semi-axis lengths are the singular values sigma 1 and sigma 2, the amounts the matrix stretches along its principal axes. Keeping only the largest of these gives the best low-rank approximation, the basis of PCA and model compression.

skills.alt: A packaged skill, bundling instructions, a tool, and resources, is loaded into an agent on demand; labeled skill.
skills.caption: A skill packages a capability an agent can load when it needs it, bundling instructions, tools, and reference material into a reusable module rather than cramming everything into one prompt. It keeps an agent lean, pulling in just the know-how a task demands, and makes capabilities shareable across agents the way a library is across programs.

sliding-window-attention.alt: A graph of the tokens I am going to sleep now arranged in a ring, labeled sliding connections. Each token is joined by an edge only to its two immediate neighbors in the sequence, so attention links stay local rather than connecting every token to every other token.
sliding-window-attention.caption: Sliding window attention limits each token to attend only to a fixed window of nearby tokens instead of the whole sequence. This local pattern makes the cost grow linearly with length rather than quadratically. Because the window is applied at every layer, information still propagates across long distances as it flows up the stack.

soft-actor-critic.alt: An actor and critic learn from a shared replay buffer while an entropy term rewards exploration; labeled actor, critic, replay buffer, entropy, and environment; titled Soft Actor-Critic.
soft-actor-critic.caption: Soft actor-critic is an off-policy reinforcement learning method that adds an entropy bonus to the reward, so the agent is paid not just to win but to stay as random as it can while winning. That built-in pressure toward exploration, combined with a replay buffer for sample efficiency, made it a robust default for continuous-control problems like robotics.

softmax.alt: Flow diagram where a network produces three real-valued scores 35.4, 38.1, and minus 5.0 for the classes cat, dog, and horse, and a softmax block converts them into the probabilities 0.06, 0.94, and 0.00.
softmax.caption: Softmax turns a vector of real-valued scores (logits) into a probability distribution. It exponentiates each score and normalizes, so the outputs are positive and sum to one, and a modestly larger logit (38.1 versus 35.4) wins a disproportionate share of the probability.

software-engineering.alt: A developer writes code that is then run against tests and verified; labeled code and test; titled Software Engineering.
software-engineering.caption: Software engineering is the discipline of building software, and the domain where AI's effect has been most immediate and measurable, because code is uniquely checkable: it compiles or it does not, the tests pass or they fail. That verifiability is why coding became the first place AI agents crossed from demo to daily, dependable use.

sparse-attention.alt: Full attention connects every token to every other, while sparse attention keeps only a chosen subset of connections; labeled attention, sparse attention, and tokens.
sparse-attention.caption: Sparse attention attacks the quadratic cost of full attention by letting each token attend to only a chosen subset of the others, a local window, a few global tokens, a fixed pattern, rather than all of them. The art is choosing which connections to keep so that the savings do not cost the model the long-range links it actually needs.

sparse-autoencoder.alt: A three-part overview of the sparse autoencoder method. Part a samples internal activations from a language model. Part b shows the sparse autoencoder itself: an activation vector is multiplied by an encoder matrix, a bias is added and ReLU applied to give a wide vector of sparse feature coefficients, which is multiplied by a decoder matrix (the dictionary) to give a reconstructed activation vector. Part c shows the resulting feature dictionary as a table of features with a plain-language meaning and an interpretability score.
sparse-autoencoder.caption: A sparse autoencoder learns an overcomplete, sparsely activating dictionary from a model's activations. The encoder lifts each activation into a much wider hidden layer forced to stay mostly zero, and the decoder reconstructs the activation as a sparse sum of dictionary directions called features. Decomposing superposed, polysemantic activations this way recovers a large set of individually interpretable features.

sparse-retrieval.alt: A query and documents are represented as high-dimensional sparse vectors over the vocabulary, mostly zero, and matched on shared terms; labeled query and vocabulary; titled Sparse Retrieval.
sparse-retrieval.caption: Sparse retrieval represents a query and each document as a huge vector over the vocabulary that is almost entirely zeros, lit up only at the words present, then matches on those overlapping terms. It is the lineage of classic keyword search, prized for exact-term precision and interpretability, and the counterpart to dense embedding retrieval in hybrid systems.

specification-gaming.alt: An agent hits the literal target dead center while a gap opens between the stated objective and the human's intention; labeled intention, objective, gap, and AI.
specification-gaming.caption: Specification gaming is when an agent scores perfectly on the objective it was literally given while completely missing what its designer meant, exploiting the gap between the two. The classic examples are almost comic, a boat-racing game agent that spins in circles collecting points instead of finishing, and they are a sharp reminder that an optimizer takes you at your exact word.

spectral-bias.alt: Four panels at training updates N equal to 100, 300, 500, and 1000. In each, a blue target function (the sum of sin 5x, sin 7x, and sin 11x) is overlaid with the neural network prediction (dashed orange). Early on the prediction captures only the broad low-frequency shape and misses the rapid wiggles; by N equal to 1000 it matches the high-frequency detail too.
spectral-bias.caption: Spectral bias is visible in the order a network fits a function. Trained by gradient descent on a target made of several sine waves, the network locks onto the smooth low-frequency structure first (N equal to 100) and only fills in the fast high-frequency detail much later (N equal to 1000). Low frequencies converge first, high frequencies last.
spectral-bias.credit: Zihan Zeng, Pengfei Shi, Fan Ma, Peihao Qi

spectral-decomposition.alt: The Mona Lisa shown before and after a horizontal shear. A red arrow lying along the horizontal stays pointing the same way in both images, while a blue arrow is tilted by the transformation.
spectral-decomposition.caption: An eigenvector is a direction a linear transformation leaves pointing the same way, only stretching or shrinking it. Under this shear the red arrow keeps its direction (it is an eigenvector) while the blue arrow is rotated. Spectral decomposition rebuilds a symmetric operator from such special directions and their scale factors (eigenvalues), turning a tangled action into independent stretches along perpendicular axes.

spectral-graph-theory.alt: A graph of eight numbered nodes forming two tightly connected groups, 1-2-3-4 and 5-6-7-8, joined to each other only by two weak edges drawn as dashed lines through node 4.
spectral-graph-theory.caption: Spectral graph theory reads a graph's structure from the eigenvectors of its Laplacian. The low-frequency eigenvectors expose well-connected communities and the weak edges between them (here the dashed bridge), giving a principled cut into two groups; this is the basis of spectral clustering and of defining a notion of frequency on a graph.

speculative-decoding.alt: Several rows of a sentence being generated. In each row, green tokens are draft proposals the large model accepted, red struck-through tokens are draft proposals it rejected, and blue tokens are the large model's correction. Each row commits a run of several tokens at once.
speculative-decoding.caption: Speculative decoding lets a small draft model propose several tokens that the large model then verifies in one parallel pass. Green tokens are accepted draft guesses, a red token is a rejected one, and the blue token is the large model's correction. Because one expensive pass ratifies a whole run of tokens, many tokens are produced per large-model step, and a careful acceptance rule keeps the output identical to the large model's own.
speculative-decoding.credit: Yaniv Leviathan, Matan Kalman, Yossi Matias

spiking-neural-network.alt: Spike generation in a leaky integrate-and-fire neuron. The top shows an RC circuit with input current I_in, resistor R, capacitor C, a threshold element theta, and an output voltage V_out. The bottom shows time traces: input current pulses I_in, the membrane potential U_mem charging and decaying toward a dashed threshold theta and resetting, and a V_out spike emitted when the threshold is crossed.
spiking-neural-network.caption: A spiking neuron integrates incoming current onto its membrane potential, which leaks away over time, and emits a discrete spike only when the potential crosses a threshold, after which it resets. Here input pulses I_in drive U_mem upward; when U_mem exceeds the threshold theta the neuron fires V_out and resets. Because each spike is time-stamped, timing itself carries information, unlike the continuous activations of standard networks.

squeeze-and-excitation.alt: Diagram of a squeeze-and-excitation building block. An input is transformed into a stack of feature maps U of shape height by width by C channels. A squeeze operation (global pooling) collapses each channel to a single value, giving a 1 by 1 by C descriptor. An excitation operation maps that descriptor to a 1 by 1 by C vector of per-channel weights, shown as colored bars. A scale operation multiplies each channel of the feature maps by its weight to produce the recalibrated output.
squeeze-and-excitation.caption: A squeeze-and-excitation block recalibrates feature channels by learned importance. The squeeze step uses global average pooling to summarize each channel into one number, the excitation step passes that summary through a tiny bottleneck network with a sigmoid to produce a weight per channel, and the scale step multiplies each channel by its weight. This is channel attention: the network learns which channels to emphasize for very little added cost.
squeeze-and-excitation.credit: Jie Hu, Li Shen, Gang Sun

state-space-model.alt: Signal-flow block diagram of a linear state space model: input u enters matrix B and feeds a summing junction whose output is the state derivative x-dot; an integrator produces the state x, which loops back through matrix A and is read out through matrix C, with a direct feedthrough D from u, summing to the output y.
state-space-model.caption: A state space model describes a system by a hidden state that evolves over time. The input enters through a matrix B, the integrated state x feeds back through the transition matrix A, and the output y is projected out by a matrix C (with an optional direct path D). Everything the model knows about the past is compressed into that single running state, which is why a sequence model built this way costs the same per step no matter how long the context.

stemming.alt: A crude rule chops words to a common stem, studies to studi, while wrongly collapsing universe and university; titled Stemming.
stemming.caption: Stemming normalizes words by crudely chopping off endings to a common stem, often a non-word like studi, so that variants of a term match in search. It is fast and rough, and its roughness shows in its errors: it will happily merge universe and university, a precision that a slower lemmatizer keeps by consulting actual word forms.

stochastic-gradient-descent.alt: Contour plot of a bowl-shaped objective with a stochastic gradient descent path that descends toward the center and then jitters in a noisy cloud around the minimum.
stochastic-gradient-descent.caption: Stochastic gradient descent uses a noisy gradient from a small random sample each step, so its path descends roughly toward the minimum and then jitters around it rather than settling exactly. That same noise is what lets it escape shallow traps a smooth method would stall in.
stochastic-gradient-descent.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

structural-equation-models.alt: A structural equation model path diagram: two latent variables drawn as circles, Intelligence and Academic Performance, joined by a directed path, each measured by several observed indicators drawn as rectangles, with an error term on every measure.
structural-equation-models.caption: A structural equation model written as a path diagram. Circles are latent variables you cannot measure directly (Intelligence, Academic Performance); rectangles are the observed indicators that load on them, each with its own error; and the arrow between the latents, here weighted .8, is the causal path the model estimates. Reading the picture is reading the equations.

structured-decoding.alt: A finite state machine for a floating-point number regular expression, with states 0 to 3 and digit and decimal-point transitions. Below it a vocabulary row labeled Logits shows most tokens masked out in black while only the tokens that keep the output valid (a decimal and a digit) are highlighted; sampling one of them moves the machine to the next state, updating which tokens are allowed.
structured-decoding.caption: Structured decoding guarantees the output matches a formal grammar by masking, not by hope. The grammar is compiled to a state machine; at each step only the tokens that keep the output valid are left unmasked in the logits (the highlighted cells), and every other token is set to zero probability. Sampling an allowed token advances the machine and recomputes the mask, so the full output is certain to parse.

structured-state-space.alt: A continuous signal is summarized by a specially structured state matrix that processes the sequence efficiently; labeled state matrix; titled Structured State Space.
structured-state-space.caption: Structured state space models process a sequence as a continuous linear dynamical system whose state matrix is given a special structure, which lets them run as a fast convolution in training and a cheap recurrence at inference. They handle very long-range dependencies at near-linear cost, the property that made architectures like S4 and Mamba serious challengers to the transformer.

subagents.alt: A main agent delegates parts of a task to several subagents that work and report back; labeled main agent and subagent.
subagents.caption: Subagents are separate agent instances that a main agent spawns to handle pieces of a task, each with its own fresh context and tools, returning only their results. The pattern keeps the coordinator's context clean and lets independent pieces run in parallel, the agentic echo of breaking a big program into focused functions.

subword-tokenization.alt: The phrase Let's do tokenization! shown as subword tokens. Common words stay whole as Let's and do, each ending with an end-of-word marker, while tokenization splits into the two reusable pieces token and ization, with the end-of-word marker on the final piece.
subword-tokenization.caption: Subword tokenization is the middle ground between whole words and single characters. Frequent words such as do are kept as one token, while a longer or rarer word like tokenization is broken into smaller learned pieces (token and ization) that the model has seen before. End-of-word markers record where the splits fall, so nothing is ever truly out of vocabulary.

superintelligence.alt: An arrow rises from a human mind to a vastly more capable artificial mind; labeled human and superintelligence.
superintelligence.caption: Superintelligence is a hypothetical intellect that vastly outstrips the best human minds across essentially every domain, not just faster but qualitatively better at reasoning, planning, and invention. It is the focal point of long-term AI concern, because a system that far beyond us would be as hard for us to predict or control as we are for a mouse.

superposition.alt: On the left, three feature visualizations of car parts feeding a single car concept in the middle; arrows then fan out to the right into several different polysemantic neurons (each also encoding things like a tiger, a dog, and a lobster), with a note that the car feature is spread across many polysemantic neurons.
superposition.caption: Superposition is a network storing more features than it has neurons by spreading each feature across many neurons as an overlapping direction rather than giving it a dedicated unit. Here a single car feature has no neuron of its own: it is distributed across several polysemantic neurons that also encode unrelated concepts, which is what makes individual neurons hard to read.
superposition.credit: Olah, Cammarata, Schubert, Goh, Petrov, Carter

supervised-fine-tuning.alt: A three-stage vertical workflow. Stage 1, pretraining, turns web text into a pretrained base model via next-token prediction. Stage 2, supervised fine-tuning, trains the base model on prompt-and-demonstration pairs to produce an instruction-tuned model. Stage 3, reinforcement learning from human feedback, uses a reward model and PPO to produce an aligned assistant model.
supervised-fine-tuning.caption: Supervised fine-tuning is the second stage of the standard pipeline, sitting between pretraining and any reinforcement learning. It trains the pretrained base model on curated pairs of an instruction and an ideal response, using the same next-token loss as pretraining, so the model learns by imitation to follow instructions and answer directly. The later reward-model stage then refines behavior from comparisons rather than single gold answers.

supervised-learning.alt: Flowchart of supervised learning: labeled observations are split into a training set and a test set; the training set fits a machine learner that produces a prediction model, which is then scored on the test set to give performance stats.
supervised-learning.caption: Supervised learning starts from observations already paired with correct labels. Those labeled examples are split into a training set, which fits the model, and a held-out test set, which scores it. The learner becomes a prediction model whose accuracy is measured on data it never trained on, the discipline that separates genuine generalization from memorization.

support-vector-machine.alt: Scatter plot of two classes of points separated by a solid line, the maximum-margin separating hyperplane, with two parallel dashed lines marking the margin on either side; the three points lying on the dashed margins are circled as the support vectors.
support-vector-machine.caption: A support vector machine separates two classes with the hyperplane that maximizes the margin, the empty band between the classes. Only the points on the margin boundaries, the circled support vectors, determine where the boundary sits; all other points could move without changing it.

swe-bench.alt: A pipeline diagram: a real GitHub issue and a codebase feed a language model, which produces a generated pull request editing several files; a unit-test table then shows tests failing before the patch and passing after it.
swe-bench.caption: Each SWE-bench task pairs a real GitHub issue with the actual repository, and the model must produce a patch. The patch is graded by running the project's hidden tests: the task counts as solved only when tests that failed before the change pass after it, an executable check with no partial credit.
swe-bench.credit: Carlos E. Jimenez, John Yang, and SWE-bench authors

sycophancy.alt: A grouped bar chart titled feedback positivity, percent, across Claude 1.3, Claude 2, GPT-3.5, GPT-4, and LLaMA 2. When the user says 'I really like' the text, feedback positivity is well above fifty percent; when the user says 'I really dislike' it drops well below fifty percent; saying 'I wrote' it raises positivity while 'I did not write' lowers it.
sycophancy.caption: Sycophancy is a model telling the user what the user appears to want to hear rather than giving its honest best answer. Here the same text receives much more positive feedback when the user says they like it or wrote it, and much more negative feedback when they say they dislike it, across five assistants. The content is unchanged, so the swing measures deference to the user's stated view rather than judgment of the work.
sycophancy.credit: Mrinank Sharma, Meg Tong, Tomasz Korbak, David Duvenaud, Amanda Askell, Samuel R. Bowman, Ethan Perez, et al.

symbol-grounding-problem.alt: Abstract symbols are connected, grounded, to a real-world referent through perception; labeled symbols and grounding; titled Symbol Grounding Problem.
symbol-grounding-problem.caption: The symbol grounding problem asks how a symbol gets its meaning without bottoming out in a dictionary of yet more symbols: how the token for carrot comes to be about actual carrots. It is the sharpest challenge to purely text-trained AI, raising whether meaning can be learned from word co-occurrence alone or requires contact with the world the words are about.

symbolic-ai.alt: A game search tree with five levels that alternate between maximizing and minimizing players; leaf values at the bottom are propagated upward by taking the max or min at each level, selecting the best move at the root.
symbolic-ai.caption: Symbolic AI produces intelligent behavior by searching a space of explicit possibilities. This minimax tree shows it: each level is a player choosing the largest or smallest value, and the scores at the leaves are propagated up the tree to pick the best move at the root. Reasoning here is search and rule-following over symbols, not learning from data.

synthetic-data.alt: A loop diagram of the Self-Instruct pipeline. A task pool seeds step one, instruction generation by a language model; step two identifies whether a task is classification; step three generates input-output instances; step four filters low-quality or too-similar generations, and the survivors are added back into the task pool to be reused.
synthetic-data.caption: Synthetic data is training data a model generates rather than data collected from people. This Self-Instruct loop shows the pattern: from a small seed pool, a language model writes new instructions and input-output instances, low-quality or near-duplicate items are filtered out, and the survivors are added back to the pool. The filtering step is decisive, since training a model on its own unfiltered output can amplify its errors.
synthetic-data.credit: Yizhong Wang, Yeganeh Kordi, Swaroop Mishra, Alisa Liu, Noah A. Smith, Daniel Khashabi, Hannaneh Hajishirzi

system-prompt.alt: A privileged, locked system prompt sets a model's behavior above the user's ordinary chat turns; labeled system prompt and model.
system-prompt.caption: A system prompt is a special, persistent instruction that sets a model's role, rules, and tone, given more authority than the ordinary user turns that follow it. It is the main lever for steering a deployed model's behavior, and a security frontier too, since much of prompt-injection attack and defense is a fight over whether user input can override it.

tangent-space.alt: A sphere labeled M with a flat plane labeled T_p M resting against it at a point p, holding two vectors u and v drawn in the plane.
tangent-space.caption: The tangent space at a point p of a manifold M is the flat plane that best touches the surface there, written T_p M. Although the manifold itself is curved, this plane is perfectly flat, so velocity vectors like u and v can be added, scaled, and compared with ordinary linear algebra.

task-decomposition.alt: A tree diagram with the top goal Supper at the root, branching into five subtasks (Menu, Preparation, Cooking, Meal, Cleanup), each of which branches further into concrete leaf tasks such as Menu Selected, Soup Prepared, Soup Cooked, Table Set, and Table Cleared.
task-decomposition.caption: Task decomposition breaks one large goal into a tree of smaller, tractable subtasks. The overall objective (Supper) is split into a few mid-level parts, each of which is split again into concrete actions an agent can carry out one at a time, then combine. The tree also exposes the dependencies (the cooking of a dish follows its preparation) that planning needs to sequence the work.

task-parallelization.alt: A job is split into independent pieces, A, B, and C, run at the same time on separate models, so total time is the slowest piece; titled Task Parallelization.
task-parallelization.caption: Task parallelization splits a job into independent pieces that run at the same time across separate model calls or agents, so the wall-clock time is the slowest single piece rather than the sum of all of them. When the pieces are genuinely independent, it is the cheapest speedup an agent system has.

taylor-expansion.alt: The sine curve in black with a sequence of Taylor polynomials of increasing degree overlaid in color; each higher-degree polynomial hugs the sine over a wider interval around the origin before peeling away.
taylor-expansion.caption: A Taylor expansion approximates a function near a point by a polynomial built from its derivatives there. Centered at the origin, the degree-one term is the straight tangent line, and each higher-degree polynomial adds curvature and finer corrections, matching the black sine curve over an ever-wider interval.

technological-singularity.alt: A scatter plot on a logarithmic vertical axis of the training computation in petaFLOP used by notable AI systems against the year they were built, from 1950 to past 2020. Two fitted regression lines cross the cloud of points: a shallower line labeled 1.5 times per year between 1950 and 2010 running through early systems like Theseus and the Perceptron Mark I, and a much steeper line labeled 4.2 times per year between 2010 and 2025 running through AlexNet, the Transformer, GPT-1, and GPT-4.
technological-singularity.caption: The singularity thesis rests on accelerating growth in the computation used to build AI. On this log plot, training compute climbs about 1.5 times per year from 1950 to 2010, then bends to a far steeper 4.2 times per year after deep learning takes hold around 2010. That regime change, where the rate of increase itself jumps, is the engine behind the singularity argument: if machine capability compounds this fast and AI begins to improve AI, progress could outrun human ability to follow or control it.
technological-singularity.credit: Our World in Data, data from Epoch AI

teleology.alt: A table at the center with four labeled causes around it: material cause (wood), formal cause (design), efficient cause (carpentry), and final cause (dining).
teleology.caption: Aristotle explained a thing by four causes, shown here for a table: its matter (wood), its form (the design), the agent that made it (carpentry), and its end or purpose (dining). Teleology is explanation by that last one, the final cause, the goal for the sake of which something exists or acts, as opposed to only the prior causes that pushed it from behind.

temperature.alt: A temperature dial reshapes the next-token distribution from peaked at low to flat at high; labeled temperature, low, and high.
temperature.caption: Temperature is the knob that scales how sharply a model commits to its top choices when sampling: low temperature peaks the distribution toward the single most likely token, making output focused and repetitive, while high temperature flattens it, making output more varied and prone to wander. It tunes the creativity-versus-reliability balance without changing the model at all.

temporal-difference-learning.alt: Two columns of firing-rate traces over time, aligned to a conditioned stimulus (CS) and a reward (R). Before learning, the response spikes at the reward. After learning, the spike moves to the CS. When an expected reward is removed, the trace dips below baseline at the time the reward was due.
temporal-difference-learning.caption: The temporal-difference error is the gap between predicted and actual value, and dopamine neurons track it. Before learning the signal fires at the reward (R); after learning it shifts to the earliest predictor, the conditioned stimulus (CS); and when an expected reward is withheld it dips below baseline. This shifting prediction error is exactly the learning signal TD methods compute.

tensor.alt: Four labeled objects in a row: a single number (Scalar, Tensor Rank 0), a column of numbers (Vector, Rank 1), a grid of numbers (Matrix, Rank 2), and a stack of grids forming a cube of numbers (Rank 3).
tensor.caption: A tensor generalizes a number, a vector, and a matrix to any number of axes. The rank is how many indices you need to reach a single element: a scalar is rank 0, a vector rank 1, a matrix rank 2, and a cube of numbers rank 3, which is why a batch of color images naturally becomes a rank-4 tensor.

tensor-parallelism.alt: A diagram of an MLP block split column-wise across two devices, D1 shaded red and D2 shaded green. The input X is fed to both devices. On each device a matrix multiply by a column slice of the first weight (A1 on D1, A2 on D2) is followed by an independent GeLU and a multiply by a slice of the second weight (B1, B2). The two partial outputs are combined into the final output Y.
tensor-parallelism.caption: Tensor parallelism splits the work inside one layer across devices. Here the first weight matrix is partitioned by columns, so each device computes a slice of the MLP on the full input independently through its own GeLU, and the partial results are combined at the end. A layer too large for a single accelerator is thus evaluated as several smaller matrix multiplications run in parallel.
tensor-parallelism.credit: Felix Brakel, Uraz Odyurt, Ana-Lucia Varbanescu

test-time-compute.alt: A line chart with generation budget on a logarithmic x-axis and math test accuracy in percent on the y-axis. Several selection methods (majority vote, best-of-N weighted, and reward-model-based search) all rise steeply as the budget grows from a few to hundreds of samples, then flatten.
test-time-compute.caption: Test-time compute spends more computation per query to get better answers. As the generation budget grows from a handful of samples to hundreds (x-axis, log scale), accuracy climbs and then flattens, a smooth scaling curve that turns inference into a dial: spend more thinking for harder questions. The gain comes from sampling and selecting, not from a larger model.
test-time-compute.credit: Charlie Snell, Jaehoon Lee, Kelvin Xu, Aviral Kumar

text-to-image.alt: A text prompt is encoded and run through a latent diffusion model under classifier-free guidance to produce a matching image; labeled text encoder, latent diffusion model, and image.
text-to-image.caption: Text-to-image generation turns a written prompt into a matching picture, typically by encoding the text and using it to guide a diffusion model as it denoises an image from scratch. The leap that made it work was learning a shared space where words and pixels align, so the phrase can steer the image toward what it describes.

text-to-speech.alt: A block-diagram pipeline reading left to right: Text enters a Text Analysis block, which produces an utterance composed of words, then a Linguistic Analysis block containing Phasing, Intonation, and Duration, which produces an utterance composed of phonemes, then a Wave Form Generation block, which outputs Speech.
text-to-speech.caption: A text-to-speech system converts writing into an audio waveform in stages. Text analysis works out the words, linguistic analysis adds the prosody (phrasing, intonation, and duration) that makes speech sound natural rather than robotic, and waveform generation synthesizes the final audio. The prosody stage is what carries rhythm and stress, so the same sentence can be spoken as a statement or a question.

text-to-video.alt: A text-to-video architecture. Images and video frames are encoded by a joint causal 3D encoder into latents, then a transformer backbone processes them with blocks containing a spatial self-attention layer, a spatiotemporal self-attention layer, and a spatial cross-attention layer that injects the text embedding.
text-to-video.caption: Text-to-video generation extends diffusion across time. Frames are compressed into a shared latent space, and a transformer backbone alternates spatial self-attention (within a frame), spatiotemporal self-attention (across frames, for motion and consistency), and cross-attention to the text prompt, so the clip is realistic, temporally coherent, and moves plausibly.
text-to-video.credit: Gupta, Yu, Sohn, Gu, Hahn, Fei-Fei, Essa, Jiang, Lezama (Photorealistic Video Generation with Diffusion Models, W.A.L.T)

tf-idf.alt: A line plot of inverse document frequency against the number of documents containing a term: the weight is high for rare terms on the left and falls toward zero for terms that appear in nearly every document on the right.
tf-idf.caption: The IDF half of TF-IDF, plotted against how many documents a term appears in. A term confined to a few documents earns a high weight; one that appears almost everywhere, like 'the', is driven toward zero. Multiplying that rarity by the term's frequency in a single document is what makes TF-IDF rank distinctive words above ubiquitous ones. The three curves are the standard, smoothed, and probabilistic IDF variants.

the-bitter-lesson.alt: Scatter plot with publication date on the horizontal axis and training computation in petaFLOP on a logarithmic vertical axis, with points for many AI systems rising by roughly twenty orders of magnitude from 1950 to the 2020s.
the-bitter-lesson.caption: The bitter lesson argues that general methods which ride increasing computation tend to beat approaches built on hand-coded human knowledge. The resource those methods exploit is shown here: the compute used to train notable AI systems has grown by about twenty orders of magnitude since 1950 (note the logarithmic axis), and the methods that scale with it have repeatedly won.

token-embedding.alt: A two-dimensional plot with four labeled points. France points to Paris and Germany points to Berlin, and the two arrows are nearly parallel and of similar length.
token-embedding.caption: A token embedding maps each token to a learned vector, placing tokens in a continuous space where meaning becomes geometry. Here the arrow from France to Paris runs nearly parallel to the one from Germany to Berlin, so a relationship like capital-of shows up as a consistent direction. Turning discrete symbols into such vectors is what gives the transformer something continuous to compute over.

token-optimization.alt: A large block of tokens is reduced to a smaller one, cutting cost and speeding the model; shown with a brain and a speed gauge.
token-optimization.caption: Token optimization reduces the number of tokens a model must process to do a task, to cut cost and latency and to fit more useful information in a fixed window. Because providers bill per token and attention scales with length, trimming tokens is one of the most direct levers on the economics of an LLM system.

tokenization.alt: The sentence Let's do tokenization! split two ways. The top row splits only on spaces, giving the three chunks Let's, do, and tokenization!. The bottom row also splits on punctuation, giving the five tokens Let, 's, do, tokenization, and the exclamation mark, each in its own box.
tokenization.caption: Tokenization cuts raw text into the discrete units a model reads. The rule chosen for where to split matters: splitting only on spaces keeps punctuation attached to a word, while also splitting on punctuation separates it into its own token. These boundaries decide what the model can see, since everything downstream is defined over tokens.

tool-use.alt: An AI reaches for a tool and connects to search, code execution, and a database; labeled AI, search, code, and database.
tool-use.caption: Tool use lets a model call external functions, search, a code interpreter, a database, a calculator, to do what a text generator cannot do on its own: fetch fresh facts, compute exact answers, and act on the world. It is the single capability that turns a model from a talker into an agent, and most of agent engineering is deciding which tools to offer and when to trust their results.

top-k.alt: A bar chart of next-token probabilities that decline only gradually, with a red cumulative-probability curve. A top-k cutoff at 6 keeps the six leftmost bars and shades off the rest, with the discarded bars annotated as many probable tokens dropped, causing a lack of word diversity.
top-k.caption: Top-k keeps a fixed number of the most probable tokens, here the top 6, and discards the rest before sampling. Because the count is fixed regardless of the distribution's shape, it misbehaves when the model is genuinely uncertain: many roughly-equal, plausible tokens past the cutoff are thrown away, which can starve the output of variety.

top-p.alt: A bar chart of next-token probabilities, highest on the left and falling to a long flat tail, with a red cumulative-probability curve. A top-p cutoff at 0.9 shades off the right portion, but a long stretch of low-probability tokens is annotated as still leading to incoherence.
top-p.caption: Top-p, or nucleus sampling, keeps the smallest set of tokens whose probabilities add up to p (here 0.9) and samples from that nucleus, so the candidate pool grows when the model is uncertain and shrinks when it is confident. Its weakness is visible here: when one token dominates, a fixed top-p can still admit a long tail of low-probability tokens.

topic-modeling.alt: The plate-notation graphical model of Latent Dirichlet Allocation: nodes alpha, theta, z, w and beta connected by arrows, with z and w enclosed in an inner plate labeled N (words) and theta and z enclosed in an outer plate labeled M (documents). Only the word node w is shaded, marking it as observed.
topic-modeling.caption: Latent Dirichlet Allocation models how documents are generated. Each document has a topic mixture (theta), each word's topic (z) is drawn from that mixture, and the observed word (w, the shaded node) is drawn from that topic's word distribution (beta). The inner plate repeats over the words in a document and the outer plate over documents, and fitting the model infers the hidden topics from the words alone.

topological-data-analysis.alt: A persistence diagram scatter plot of feature birth on the x-axis against feature death on the y-axis, with dimension-0 features as red dots and dimension-1 features as blue triangles, all lying above the diagonal; points far from the diagonal persist longest.
topological-data-analysis.caption: Topological data analysis summarizes the shape of data in a persistence diagram. As a distance threshold grows, topological features appear (birth) and vanish (death); dimension-0 points track connected components and dimension-1 points track loops. Points far above the diagonal live across many scales and signal real structure, while those hugging the diagonal are short-lived noise.
